Title: C2 Capability - Why do we fail to improve it? Exploring Barriers to the Development of C2 Capability
Authors: Wikberg, P.; Bryant, D.; Granåsen, M.; Houghton, P.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract:
Doctrine and concepts for C2 have changed considerably over the past three decades, due to peace-enforcement operations, war in the Gulf, and more latterly a declared 'war' against terrorism and insurgent forces. We now find ourselves in an era of constant competition, hybrid warfare and rapid technological development. Nonetheless, and compared to developments in other areas of warfare, such as weapon technology, platforms, etc., C2 practice, including current training and education, forms of organisation, and employment of enabling technology, are largely the same as twenty years ago.

This observation raises an important question, which is whether there is good reason for the lack of change; i.e., is it because C2 does not need to change, or because it is not changing despite best efforts to transform. The answer would appear to be the latter, especially given that C2 remains as a critical capability for NATO, enabling it to respond effectively to the rapidly changing context of future allied operations.

Title: A Multilevel Model for Command and Control in the Information age
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: In search for a Command and Control philosophy that will be adequate for servicemen's ever-changing characteristics, technology, and the environment's complexity, Mission Command stands in the fore. Nevertheless, even this philosophy requires a periodic inspection to adapt its application (the methods) to the means in-hand. This article suggests examining the philosophy's core-principles and its execution using a multilevel method: Three core constructs (Human, Means, and Information) and two levels of interactions built around them. This article argues that this attitude might be used as a holistic approach for extrapolation of former philosophies, and perpetual inspection and adaptation of the Command and Control philosophies, nowadays and in the future's challenges

Title: Enabling and Managing Data Provenance in a Command and Control Environment
Authors: Putnam, C.; Waters, J.; Chao, J.; Rasmussen, B.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Information provenance is a record of the origin and transition history of information. Provenance includes the date and time of key events in the information lifecycle, including where and when the information was created and what processing was applied to tailor or transform the information, by who and when. Provenance provides context, establishes trust, provides validation, and ensures reliability and quality of information. In a digital age, our military receive, share and rely on more information, derived and processed from ever more sources. Given such an inundation, provenance plays a key role in ensuring people can understand and assess the veracity and quality of the information. As the military becomes more AI-enabled, provenance can serve to understand, document and search the chain of causality, to improve AI explainability, security and meaning with context. In no environment is this more critical than Command and Control. How does an organization enable information provenance? What practical approaches are available? What are recommended best practices? This paper outlines several approaches for enabling and managing data provenance, including tool selection, standards, track-changes, logs/log-filtering, wiki-history, semantics, linked data, benefits of containers for reproducibility, importance of unique naming and versioning CM, data modeling, blockchains, and instrumentation. The authors are beginning to explore enabling this advanced information provenance roadmap across enterprise systems.

Title: Agile Control: Distributed Collaborating Deciders
Authors: O'Neill, J.; Ballard, R.; Turner, D.; Koprek, M.; Symons, R.; Davies, P.; Kingston, G.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The conceptualisation and implementation of Information Age and Network-Centric Warfare (NCW) constructs has paralleled the evolution of models of jointness. In 2002, the Australian focus was on developing Headquarters Joint Operations Command, with 'Hierarchical Command, Network Control' seen as an aspirational state. Today, the Future C2 concept 'Hierarchical Command, Agile Control' has evolved beyond this aspirational state and shifted NCW thinking from a 'blue' capability perspective, to the conduct of operations by distributed, collaborating deciders operating in contested and congested environments, spanning kinetic and non-kinetic effects. This paper is an early operationalisation of Agile Control and presents a Decider Concept, Modes, and Patterns for how operations may be conducted as network connections become available / unavailable in contested and congested environments.

Title: The RISEN Project - A Novel Concept for Real-time on-site Forensic Trace Qualification
Authors: Manso, M.; Chirico, R.; Peltola, J.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract:
Following a chemical or biological attack, the rate at which a forensic investigation proceeds is critical for capturing perpetrators and preventing future incidents. Crime Scene Investigation (CSI) is a process that aims at recording the scene as it is first encountered and recognizing and collecting all the physical evidence potentially relevant to the solution of the case. Conventional CSI processes involve sensitive and delicate steps related with the identification and transportation of traces that may subsequently be subjected to laboratory analysis, a process that can take several hours or days.

In this paper, we present the Real-tIme on-site forenSic tracE qualificatioN (RISEN) project, an innovate concept in forensic investigations in the context of CSI of sites affected by a chemical or biological attack. Coordinated by ENEA, RISEN will develop a set of networkenabled real-time contactless sensors for handling traces on site and accurate 3D recreation mechanisms of the entire crime scene, providing an immersive environment for investigators to evaluate hypotheses and conduct highly detailed investigations. The RISEN concept will allow forensics investigators and judicial authorities, to gather high quality information from a vast list of visible and invisible traces (localisation, identification/classification, interpretation and labelling) from a crime scene through standardised reports and a secure way, also speedingup the forensic investigation process.

The RISEN project started in July 2020 and has a duration of 4 years. RISEN public information can be assessed at the project website https://www.risen-h2020.eu/.

Title: Multi-Agent Systems Collaborative Teaming (MASCOT) Definition Process to Create Specifications for Multi-Agent System (MAS) Development
Authors: Heisey, C. W.; Maki, D. E.; Brittain, M. W.; Bush, K.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The US Army envisions heterogeneous teams of advanced machines and humans that will collaborate together to achieve a common mission goal. It is essential for commanders to quickly and effectively respond to dynamic mission environments with agile re-tasking and computerized aids for plan definition/redefinition, and to perform some tasks with bounded autonomy. Workload constraints limit an individual's ability to concurrently control many platforms, so some mission segments many need to be autonomous or to be quickly selected via a tactics playbook. Denied environments also dictate the need for machine participants in some mission segments to be autonomous (or semi-autonomous). A Multi-Agent System (MAS) provides a natural paradigm for describing a system of agents that work together in such environments. An agent can be a human or machine, but is generally a machine. Creating MAS systems and requirements has proved to be a formidable task due to mission complexities, the necessity to deal with unforeseen circumstances, and the general difficulty of defining autonomous behaviors. We define a process called Multi-Agent Systems Collaborative Teaming (MASCOT) Definition Process that starts with a Subject Matter Experts (SME), produces a set of agent specifications, and derives system requirements in sufficient detail to define a MAS that can be modeled in a test-bed, used for facilitation of a safety analysis, and produced into an actual system. The MASCOT process also enables concurrent development of an effects based ontology. We demonstrate the MASCOT process on an example case study to show the efficacy of our process.

Title: The influence of harmonization levels on multi-domain operations effectiveness: The moderating roles of organizational environment and command and control variety
Authors: Valaker, S.; Stensrud, R.; Haugen, T.; Eikelboom, A. R.; van Bemmel, I.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: This paper presents a research model regarding inter-entity collaboration. Today's mission environments often require collaboration between and among entities. Such collaboration we conceptualize as harmonization: transforming a set of entities into an effective enterprise by using harmonization arrangements to achieve collaboration. Harmonization varies as to the closeness of collaboration and we call this variation harmonization levels. Recent command and control (C2) theorizing in the NATO research group SAS-143 have suggested that selecting the appropriate harmonization level, closeness of collaboration, may positively influence multi-domain operation (MDO) effectiveness. In this paper, we discuss whether specific aspects of the organizational environment, uncertainty and ambiguity, and C2 variety moderates a positive relationship between harmonization level and MDO effectiveness. We argue that higher harmonization levels will be more positive for MDO effectiveness when environmental uncertainty and ambiguity is high. C2 variety we define as having three dimensions: the C2 approach of an entity, its harmonization options available, and its ability to make use of its C2 approach to integrate with other entities. We suggest that higher C2 variety together with environmental uncertainty and ambiguity will further strengthen the positive relationship between harmonization level and MDO effectiveness. We draw on a case study to develop the notion of C2 variety; we then develop hypotheses about the interactions among harmonization level, organizational environment and C2 variety. Finally, we discuss theoretical implications, suggestions for future , and implications for practice.

Title: 5th generation fighter aircraft and future command and control: A note on some possible changes
Authors: Stensrud, R.; Mikkelsen, B.; Valaker, S.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: In this paper we consider some possible consequences for command and control of the technological characteristics of fifth generation aircraft (increased sensor, network and stealth capability). We suggest that when delegating decision rights to the pilot, e.g. target engagement authority, commanders need to weigh the cost and benefits of sharing and not sharing information. When relinquishing the opportunity to share information the potential consequence might be escalation, although it may be a benefit in terms of solving the mission tactically. In making a delegation decision, the commander could benefit from a formal framework, where additional known factors influencing delegation decisions are systematically examined. This could shorten the decision making time at the same time as reducing biases from omitting crucial factors in decision making. Based on such a framework we discuss implications for Air Force and Joint C2.

Title: Operationalizing Joint All Domain C2 in the U.S. Military 25 th ICCRTS Paper
Authors: Tillman, M. E.; Conley, K. M.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract:
The command and control (C2) research community has had limited success in convincing naturally cautious military audiences to apply C2 research findings.  This reticence may be changing.  As the environment in which forces are operating becomes more complex and dynamic, a series of senior military leaders has explicitly advocated for changes in C2 approaches.  Based on Institute for Defense Analyses research on the operational application of Agile C2 in the U.S. military, we postulate that incremental adaptation of existing techniques may be an effective mechanism for generating buy-in for research findings and thus for reaping the rewards of resilient and agile C2.  Two practical cases are discussed.  The first focuses on a U.S. Combatant Command headquarters and suggests a technique for establishing a more effective multi-domain C2 construct that will enable the Command to transition from steadystate conditions through crisis action planning and wartime operations.  The Command was aware of a faulty existing battle rhythm and had attempted to apply existing processes to correct the situation.  Using C2 research findings, we proffered an adaptation to these processes that would better leverage the human capital on the Combatant Command's headquarters staff. The second practical case involves a Service headquarters section that had been charged with facilitating C2 of multi-domain operations (MDC2).  The headquarters staff recognized the need to convert the existing hierarchical structure for operational C2 into a mesh network that would be more resilient.  C2 research suggests that such a network would be more resilient, especially when faced with a contested communications environment.  In this case, we proposed to the staff that they extend two doctrinal constructs for delegating decision-making authority: the Conditional Authorities Matrix and the Decision Support Template.  This proposed construct, dubbed the Decision Authorities Matrix (DAM), provides a framework for recognizing and visualizing linkages among decision makers, critical informational requirements, and resultant information flows.  If validated and introduced into Service or Joint doctrine, the DAM can enable planners to visualize and implement more resilient C2 designs.  In both cases, we suggest that by extending existing processes consistent with C2 research findings, military organizations can reap rapid, critically needed gains in effectiveness.

The command and control (C2) research community has experienced limited success in convincing naturally cautious military audiences to apply its research findings.  Despite substantial evidence over the years that a military organization's C2 approach 1 must adapt to changing circumstances, there has been no corresponding change in the C2 doctrine in the U.S. armed forces to define a C2 approach, nor

Title: Building the Command and Control Range: Measuring C2 Approach in the C2 Range.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: 'Scoring' a Command and Control (C2) approach in relation to other approaches or in different conditions requires a facility closely resembling the more familiar military training range. This C2 Range must present Commanders both an indication of their current C2 approach and an optimal approach  where a shot strikes and aim point in a small arms range analogy. Both scoring the current C2 approach and  specifying  an  optimal  approach  require  a  method  to  quantify  C2.  This  paper  uses  the  C2  Space approach  defined  and  expanded  in  previous  NATO  System  Analysis  & Studies  activities,  with components of graph theory to propose a method for quantifying a C2 approach. Comparison of different C2  approaches  with  regard  to  outcomes  is  possible  in  experiments,  exercises  or  on  operations.  The operational value in identifying the optimal C2 approach for a given environment is in, of course, the success of the mission but also in the ability to configure and train a task force knowing how best to approach  C2  in  the  expected  environment.  This  study  used  a  simulation-based  method  to  assess  and contrast different C2 configurations. It was conducted for the Canadian Joint Warfare Centre in support of an  ongoing  Agile  Pan-Domain  Comprehensive  Campaigning  Experiment  to  examine  C2  challenges, opportunities and requirements in the information age.

Title: Parakeet: a C2 Framework for Efficient AI Capability Development
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Command and Control (C2) concepts and practices have always co-evolved with communications and information technology. The recent advances in Artificial Intelligence (AI) have changed the way the information technology world performs. This requires C2 to quickly adapt in order to take full advantage of AI potential. Deep Learning (DL) has been at the forefront of the recent advances in AI. As an example, in the field of computer vision, DL has enabled the development of object detection and classification models that can rival human capabilities in some domains [1]. However, DL algorithms are typically data hungry and they need to be trained on thousands of labelled samples. Annotated datasets that are relevant to military operations can be hard to come by. Although open source datasets are easily accessible, they rarely present the following characteristics that are prevalent in military operational context: Multiple sensor modalities (such as visible, infrared, thermal or hyper spectral); Degraded images (Noisy, blurred, low resolution); Labelled instances of specialized military equipment (not only everyday ordinary objects); and Aerial point of view (not only ground-based imagery). To fill this gap, there is a need to complement datasets available from open source or allies, with data collection activities. Even though data collection itself requires significant efforts, the data labelling phase is often the most effort-intensive step of dataset creation. It is considered the main bottleneck holding back further AI adoption [2]. Labelling is challenging due to time constraint (it is a long and tedious process) and to subject matter expert availability for validating dataset quality.

Title: Convergence in Understanding Complex Information Environments Through an Iterative Process of Sampling and Sense-making
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Network Centric Warfare (NCW) was initially introduced by Cebrowski and Gartska (1998) and represented a sea change in war-fighting. The key enabler for NCW was a technology driven information network that would provide greater shared situation awareness and lead to enhanced agility and effectiveness through self-synchronization. Although the ICCRTS community has worked to empirically validate these concepts, the information network has evolved from its humble origins to become a vastly complex and complicated entity. So much so that those tasked to analyse this space are faced with the question \\what are the important bits in this mess?\\". To help Commanders and their staff

The Three Orders model was originally created to support C2 as an enabler of self-synchronization by defining an ideal state of shared situation awareness from which breaks in awareness that limit self-synchronization could be identified. Title: Enabling Data Driven Multi-Int Analysis: Implications and Exploitation
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: In recent times, situation analysis has seen a shift from researching clearly identified, and somewhat known entities, to trying to make sense of complex operational environments. Indeed, modern conflicts have shifted from huge battles to fighting insurgents and asymmetrical warfare, which makes identifying key priority intelligence requirements difficult. This requires the exploitation of large-scale multi-int data to filter events, entities and transaction. It also leads to the development of new analysis paradigms, among which is Activity-based intelligence (ABI). ABI is a multi-int analysis methodology that centers on rapid integration and analysis of big data in order to discover relevant patterns and determine and identify change. The use of this modern approach has a direct impact, not only on intelligence analysis, but also on the prioritization of information requirements, and on the way analysis tools are developed. This paper discusses the evolution of conflicts and the way it impacts modern situation analysis. The necessity of discovering unknown unknowns is presented in the context of operational intelligence and of system development. An approach to exploitation of multi-int big data in a way that supports goal and data driven analysis is finally presented.

Title: Data-driven, Service-oriented, Knowledge-based Command and Control
Authors: Verhoosel, J.; Nouwt, B.; Voogd, J.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Defense operational and tactical activities are increasingly shifting towards Joint, Interagency, Multinational and Public (JIMP) collaborations with various different partners. In addition, C2 is based on data being generated at an increasing rate by an increasing number of sources in the physical, human and cyberspace landscapes. These sources become knowledge bases when raw data is aggregated to a higher level of information that can provided via a service-oriented interface. Future C2 systems can cope with this varying number of heterogeneous, distributed knowledge bases using an information services architecture described in this paper. In this architecture, the Semantic Service Directory component keeps track of all available services that disclose a knowledge base. The semantics of the interface of a service is modelled in a common ontology maintained in a Knowledge Base component. A Smart Connector component is introduced to enable request/response of, and publish/subscription to, knowledge that is provided by a service. It connects knowledge bases using supply and demand of knowledge and provides ontology-based access control. We designed a small experiment to show the feasibility of our architecture in a proofof-concept environment. It concerns tracking of high-value targets in a multinational setting between different organizations. Results show that the components allow new, unforeseen knowledge bases to be automatically discovered and integrated within existing C2 applications. Starting/stopping of services, matching of demand with knowledge suppliers, as well as information distribution via request/response and publish/subscribe are supported. Future challenges are to show how connections to different types of services and scalability of the architecture can be achieved.

Title: Aligning Multi-Domain Command and Control (C2) and Cybersecurity
Authors: Teske, K. D.; Miller, M. E.; Guerin, P. J.; Robinson, G.; Rosas, R.; Schmidt, J.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Mission Partner and Coalition Command and Control (C2) and Cyber forces must develop options and capabilities that enhance inter-dependence and further their alignment 'harmonization' to increase total organizational effectiveness and understanding, while reducing the Cyber risk to the mission. The following four principles are a necessity in order to achieve alignment and integration: Common view with goals and objectives, Common understanding of capabilities, Alignment of efforts to ensure coherency, and Assessment to change course or direction as needed. Both alignment and integration will help address the challenges associated with any Cyber Risk to Mission in today's ever-changing environment. Applying and evolving a framework approach that was discussed in the 19th ICCRTS, 21st ICCRTS, 22nd ICCRTS, and 24th ICCRTS demonstrates the importance of comprehending each and every partner; Special Operations, Conventional Forces, Domestic and Foreign Ministries, Departments, Bureaus, and Agencies are equally important to better understand and confront C2 and Cyber risk, problems, and issues. We must use our collective lessons learned, best practices, approaches, and strategies identifying common goals, areas of interest, capabilities, and common categories of effort to be applied by each of the organizations as the focus area to maximize our Cyber-enabled capabilities.

Title: Coronavirus: Gray Zone Tactics in Cyberspace 1 (Concept Paper)
Authors: Gill, R.; Seaboyer, A.; Fitzpatrick, M.; Duncan, M.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: In cyberspace, 'gray zone' tactics represent a central challenge facing military decision-makers globally. Authoritarian regimes like China are moving their main operational effort into the gray zone, where there are no constraints, both legally and ethically. This is in contrast to democracies that must abide by a more consensus-driven approach, which requires adherence to both legal and ethical  rules  (Belo  &  Carment,  2019).    State  and  non-state  actors  effectively  use  propaganda, disinformation, economic coercion and the covert support of non-state entities to achieve their strategic  agenda  (Belo,  2019).  Unrestricted,  they  have  executed  powerful  online  influence operations in the United States (Gill, van de Kuijt, Rosell & Johansson, 2019) and many other countries in the world (Singer & Brooking, 2018).  Russia sowed confusion and discord during 2016 US presidential elections and has tried to influence democratic processes across Europe by using online information maneuvers (Gill et al., 2019; Hoffman, 2020).  These strategies have attracted a significant amount of attention, but it did not represent Russia's first foray into gray zone cyber tactics. As early as 2007, they employed large scale cyber-attacks against Estonia, which resulted  in  a  denial  of  services  and  essentially  paralyzed  the  country  (Hoffman,  2020). Using the current pandemic as the most recent example, this paper examines cyber warfare tactics and strategies employed by state actors in the gray zone. We further examine the implications of these tactics on democracies, the extent to which allies are equipped to counter those specific to the cyberspace domain.  The paper concludes by identifying recommendations and risks of how allies can effectively operate in the cyberspace domain.

Title: Avoid Predictability in COA Development for Missions Coping with Complexity
Authors: Andersson, I.; Josefsson, A.; Svanborg, C.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: For many years, we have noticed that different courses of action (COA) developed by Joint Operational Planning Groups (JOPG) for solving the same mission seldom rarely differ more than marginally. This can lead to plans that are predictable for an opponent. If we want to be able to expose an opponent to surprise and complex problems, predictable plans are not good. Planning doctrine only based on the past experience is most often not the best when preparing for missions in future operations, therefore more creative and divergent thinking is needed. In this paper we discuss conditions for COA development that stimulate creative and divergent thinking. We also discuss how planners continually alternate between divergent and convergent thinking before and during execution of operations.

Title: The Impacts of U/lizing Non-human Intelligent Collaborators on the Appropriateness of C2-Harmoniza/on Arrangements
Authors: Alberts, D. S.; Simpson, M. L., Jr; Phister, P.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: It is well recognized that traditional software development and acquisitions cannot keep pace with changes in operations or advances in technology. National security considerations require that we are able to take full advantage of emerging technologies in a timely manner. There are four general approaches that have been used to explore, test, and ultimately field new technologies or applications of existing technologies. These include: 1) trail and error; 2) a deliberate requirements driven process (e.g. DOD 5000); 3) incremental changes to proven capabilities (do the same things but better); 4) use theory to identify opportunities and guide technology development and implementation to make dramatic improvements. This paper reports upon part of a larger research effort being undertaken by SAS-143 to extend C2 Agility Theory to collections of entities engaged in multi-domain, multi-dimensional operations that depend upon cross-domain harmonization for their success. NATO research group SAS-143 is in the process of exploring the effectiveness and efficiency of alternative approaches to C2-Harmonization under a variety of mission challenges and conditions. Given the increasing utilization of automation, Artificial Intelligence (AI), and autonomy, one of SAS-143's research lines of effort has been to design and conduct a series of ELICIT experiments that involve Non-human Intelligent Collaborators (NIC). These experiments compare the performance of a set of entities participating in a set of increasing challenging multi-domain operations with and without NIC team members. This paper discusses the nature of the ELICIT experimental platform, how it was employed in these experiments, the results, and conclusions regarding the utilization of these technologies.

Title: Tactical Interoperability with JC3IEDM: Performance Measurements
Authors: Oliveira, A. F.; Cecílio, E. L.; Botelho, T. A. T.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The C2 Interoperability project (INTERC2), sponsored by the Brazilian Ministry of Defense, built an interoperability platform, PINTERC2, initially developed for the operational/strategic level, based on JC3IEDM and ADEM, running over cabled networks. The INTERC2 team is developing a proof of concept (PoC) for its use in tactical interoperability level using a distributed gateway in a joint tactical radio network based in the Brazilian Software Defined Software (SDR). We created a simulated tactical environment composed of vessels, aircrafts and vehicles in three tactical radio networks, with their respective tactical gateways that extract and convert the necessary information and send it via raw TCP/UDP to a strategic gateway, which generates the JC3IEDM messages and sends them to PINTERC2. The INTERC2 team has made measurements using JC3IEDM (SOAP), TCP and UDP protocols in the simulated tactical environment, revealing significantly greater delay and extra bytes transmitted when using SOAP. It was decided to use raw TCP/UDP to send only the necessary information needed to build the JC3IEDM messages later. Once this information is received, only the operational/strategic gateway generates JC3IEDM messages and sends them to PINTERC2 via SOAP. Performance measurements in the simulated PoC allowed the definition of the distributed gateway architecture and the implementation limits. Testing with real tactical means will take place in late 2020. The contribution of this work is to demonstrate the Brazilian Model of tactical-operational Interoperability Platform based on Multiple Link Radios, the deployment of the Interoperability Platform to achieving a shared situational awareness with multiple ontologies and presents an analysis of the results gathered in the performance measurements.

Title: HOW C2 CAN BENEFIT FROM 3D MODELING OF URBAN ENVIRONMENTS FOR MILITARY OPERATIONS
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Whether decentralized or not, persistent or episodic, the purpose of Command and Control (C2) is to increase  Situation  Awareness  (SA)  of  commanders  at  all  levels  to  enable  them  to  make  timely  and appropriate decisions. SA is defined as the knowledge of the elements of the operational environment necessary  to  make  well-informed  decisions.  Knowing  the  enemy  and  knowing  oneself  are  the  classic elements that characterize SA. An important element of SA in urban operations is the complexity of the physical terrain. According to TC 2-91.4/ (Intelligence Support to Urban Operations), 'Operations within the urban environment follow standard Army doctrine regarding the wargaming process. Because of the level of detail, however, there is a need for a modeling tool. These tools can range from plastic or wooden models to overhead images to sand tables. Probably the best is the combination of overhead imagery and three-dimensional (3D) models.' First, 3D urban environment models can assist with mission planning, as well as in establishing and maintaining SA. During operation implementation, these models can provide critical information during briefings. Afterwards, they can be used to help understanding what happened on the theatre of operations and also to evaluate decisions and actions taken during the operation. This manuscript discusses (1) different ways of creating and updating 3D urban environment models whether in preparation of or during operations, and (2) their usage by commanders as a tool combined or not with information from diverse sensors evolving in the theatre of operations, and from analysis systems evolving at  the  command  centre.  The  results  are  based  on  experiments  that  took  place  under  The  Technical Cooperation Program (TTCP)'s 'Contested Urban Environment' strategic challenge.

Title: COMMAND AND CONTROL AGILITY PERFORMANCE METRICS, A PRACTICAL APPROACH.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract:
Victory in modern warfare relies heavily on a Commander's ability to act upon timely, and actionable intelligence to achieve 'decision advantage.' As a result, Command and Control (C2) agility is a critical capability, as it enables commanders to make changes needed for delivery of military objectives. Typically, lack of information negatively impacts C2 performance and subsequent realization of military objectives. This paper describes a new concept based on measuring 'agility' as a way to allow commanders to generate a Common C2 Picture (C3P) and apply corrective actions when necessary.

To achieve this, a literature review on the concept of Agility was conducted. A focus for the review was how aspects of C2 performance can be measured in a practical way to determine levels of agility for an Air C2 organization. The review aimed to identify real world, subjective characteristics that can be translated into objective, numerical descriptions [9] [10], allowing the correlation of entity behavior with key C2 performance parameters. Metrics and collection tools to determine agility and C2 approaches were identified.

Title: UNDERSTANDING URBAN OPERATIONAL ENVIRONMENTS BY INTEGRATING HUMAN AND PHYSICAL DOMAINS
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Trends in demographics indicate a continuous and significant increase in both the number and size of urban environments (UEs) throughout the world, and it is likely that military operations will increasingly take place in UEs in the future. Generally these environments are characterized by an intricate topography and a large concentration of population. They are also multi‐faceted, diverse, and highly dynamic environments. But each one is unique and will react to and affect military operations differently. All UEs are defined by three dimensions (also called an urban triad) that are generally so intertwined as to be virtually inseparable: The physical domain – i.e., the dynamic and complex human-made physical terrain superimposed on an existing natural terrain; The human domain – i.e., a population of significant size, density, and varying sociocultural groupings; The interactions and interdependencies that connect the physical and human domains. A thorough understanding of this operational environment, in which military personnel could be deployed and have to operate, is thus essential. However, data on each of these dimensions tend to be collected and processed in silos. This paper will examine ways in which data from the human and physical domains could be combined in order to augment understanding of UEs, and so enable enhanced situation awareness and decision making in urban military operations.

Title: Police-AI Teaming: Development of User-Centric Incident Profile Platform for the Dutch Police
Authors: Schaap, T.; Wemmers, S.; Bakker, R.; Venrooij, W.; Koning, M.; Huis in 't Veld, M.; Pieneman, R.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: In this paper we introduce a first concept of the usercentric incident profile platform (UCIPP), which will aim to [in the future] connect data and provide insights using intelligent visualizations to support Real Time Intelligence Centers (RTIC) in the Netherlands. RTIC is a police department that provides additional safety information to patrolling officers responding to emergency situations. Information analysts (IAs) within RTIC are supposed to provide information on incidents within five minutes; however, they currently work with systems which do not optimally support the execution of their tasks. Currently, IAs can search through over 40 different databases in order to find information relevant to cases on which they are working. With so much available information, it is difficult to search through and find all relevant information under time constraints. The development of UCIPP is based upon in-depth interviews and collaboration with domain experts, who were able to relay to our development team what kinds of challenges exist and which requirements such a platform needs to fill; UCIPP will be built based on outlined business, cognitive, information services, and technical requirements. UCIPP will consist of four modules that we believe will address these requirements: 1) a data cleaning module, 2) a data mining module, 3) an ontology module, and 4) a visualization module. Future work on UCIPP includes further development of modules and testing its application within RTIC to ensure that it is more efficient than current searching methods.

Title: Research on Constructing and Evolving Agile C2 System
Authors: Duanmu, Z.; Li, H.; Mao, X.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: To adapt to the changes from the battlefield and the self-status, command and control (C2) system should have the ability of agility. How to construct and evolve an agile C2 system is still an open problem in the literature of military command and control. In this paper, the evolutionary process architecture of agile C2 system has been proposed, including system construction, dynamic development and system resource coordinated scheduling. Then, the agile operating mechanism coping with the above-mentioned changes is described from the perspective of motivation perception, responsive decision making and reconstruction execution. Further, the application modes of agile C2 system are discussed. Finally, the problems and corresponding key technologies to be further studied are put forward.

Title: When Things Go Amiss: Lessons for Successful International Partnerships in the Defense Research and Development Community
Authors: Carreño, J.; Galdorisi, G.; Morton, C.; Shigley, P.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Alliances and partnerships constitute an integral element of U.S. strategic defense guidance. Foremost among these stands the National Defense Strategy, directing the strengthening of alliances and attracting new partners. Not to put too fine a point on it, these relationships are crucial to the endurance of a 'free and open international order,' ascribing them as 'providing a durable, asymmetric strategic advantage that no competitor or rival can match.' Chief of Naval Operations, Admiral Michael Gilday, underscores the point in his recent guidance, CNO FRAGO 01/2019. The naval services need to expand relationships to 'broaden and strengthen global maritime awareness and access,' and to operate and exercise with allies and partners focusing 'on full interoperability at the high end of naval warfare.' For the U.S. Navy's laboratories and warfare centers, strengthening alliances and partnership takes the shape of strong engagements between their respective scientific and engineering communities. Robust cooperation and information sharing at the initial levels of development can pay dividends by addressing interoperability issues at an earlier stage of technical development. At the Naval Information Warfare Center Pacific, we have Information Exchange Program (IEP) Agreements with almost two dozen nations, providing an avenue for our scientists and engineers to cooperatively evolve and develop command, control, communications, and computer (C4) technologies that enable our respective navies to interoperate as seamlessly possible. Establishing these relationships and cooperative project agreements takes time as nations work through their respective organizations and leadership. And while successful examples abound, some project agreement and information exchanges result in sub-optimal outcomes despite everyone's best intentions. Some of our most useful lessons in establishing successful cooperative agreements derive when things go amiss.

Title: Organizational Agility and Resilience: Lessons from Distributed Operations
Authors: Carreño, J.; Crellin, S.; Paule, P.; Riley, S.; Tolentino, G.; Wood, J.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The Novel Coronavirus Disease 2019 (COVID-19) pandemic rapidly pushed organizations to a distributed mode of operations. Transitioning from centralized office and onsite laboratory locations to a nearuniversal telework situation immediately raised challenges of how to effectively 'command and control' in a situation where traditional modes of operations were rendered moot almost overnight. Face-to-face meetings, in-person team collaborations, and informal question and answer engagements over the proverbial watercooler no longer proved viable. Managers and supervisors lost the ability to conduct 'management-by-walking around' or to directly engage and observe subordinate workgroups. Like other large organizations, the COVID-19 pandemic forced Naval Information Warfare Center (NIWC) Pacific to recalibrate its workforce flexibility posture while balancing the need to meet its mission. Despite the challenge of having a sizeable portion of its workforce in a distributed mode of operation, NIWC Pacific continued to meets its mission, and in certain cases witnessed improved personal and team performance. This paper will look at the case of NIWC Pacific and of its Command and Control Enterprise Engineering Department (C2E2) specifically, as it adapted to operating under this pandemic. It examines this experience through the prism of the six dimensions of agility put forth by Dr. David Alberts and Dr. Richard Hayes in their work Power to the Edge : robustness; resilience; responsiveness; flexibility; innovation; and, adaptation. Some of these lessons and assessment could provide other entities a point of comparison by which to assess their respective responses, and inform plans whenever a similar 'shock to the system' prevails upon us.

Title: Simulation in NATO Federated Mission Networking
Authors: Pullen, J. M.; Galvin, K.; Brook, R. A.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract:
Two decades ago in Afghanistan, NATO's International Security Assistance Force learned by doing: the multinational  force  was  hampered  in  operations  until  the  Afghan  Mission  Network  (AMN)  was assembled to support collaboration and coordination of forces. Today Allied Command Transformation is  preparing  for  a  future  where  coalition  forces  have  a  network  far  superior  to  AMN  on  Day  Zero  of coalition operations. Toward this end, the Federated Mission Networking (FMN) project is assembling a framework of NATO and commercial standards with the expectation that the 30 member nations will configure their networking capabilities to interoperate over the FMN standards.

The NATO Modelling and Simulation Group (MSG) Technical Activity 145 and SISO Product Development Group  for  the  C2-Simulation  Interoperation  (C2SIM)  have  been  working  together  to  standardize  and operationalize a new capability, which has been described in previous ICCRTS papers by the authors. The team that assembled C2SIM standards now finds a new challenge: assembling and justifying a collection of  standards  for  modeling  and  simulation  (M&S)  that  suit  FMN  needs,  with  C2SIM  an  obvious cornerstone  of  that  collection.  This  paper  addresses,  from  a  systems  engineering  viewpoint,  the technical issues and process whereby standards for networked computer simulation within the FMN are nominated.  The  paper  introduces  the  FMN  concept,  followed  by  a  discussion  of  the  role  of  M&S  in coalition operations, and finishes with a review of likely standards for networked military simulation that will be included.

Title: Mobility Nodes Beyond the Tactical Edge for Close Air Support Situational Awareness and Survivability
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: This operations research focuses on Multi-Domain Command and Control 1 for Close Air Support (CAS) and Stability Operations (SO). Use of unmanned platform systems with Artificial Intelligence (AI) is examined for CAS with Situational Awareness (SA) and protection for Area Denial Methods 2 . Cross Domain maneuvers beyond the line of sight (BLOS) with CAS provide Tactical  Agility  and  tempo.  This  capability  is  produced  by  Mobility  Nodes  (MNs)  being  the concept of unmanned robotic platforms to deliver SA for CAS by providing Mobility  in  Area Denial (AD) environments expanding the coverage and range for Ground Operators by providing visual confirmations and critical information to commanders in urban and open zones. Capability Gaps are filled preventing unacceptable loss of life and critical mission failure by inserting assets for  Counterland  missions.  The  Multi-Domain  Capability  simultaneously  creates  synergy  with MNs for USAF to conduct air-ground missions for ground Movement and Maneuvers (M2). Mobility Nodes combine the Capability of SA and protection for Close Air Support and Stabilization  Security  Operations  (SecOps).  To  fulfill  these  Operational  Needs  signifies  the proper use of sensors and Artificial Intelligence reliability. This synchronizes operational tempo with SA mobility capabilities. Robust autonomous unmanned platforms operating in austere DIL environments with scalable modular technology provides cost effective robotic components for CCDRs  Capabilities.  This  also  synergizes  cross-domain  missions  with  SA  with  dynamic retasking of Dismounted Warfighters where IED/mines/hybrid threats exist for Tactical Agility. This same operational concept applies for Humanitarian Efforts and civilian departure activities.

Title: Human -AI Teaming with the Digital Battlespace Framework
Authors: Mousavi, P.; Reedel, G.; Specht, E.; Lim, J.; Busler, J.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: With the emergence of UAV swarms and hypersonic threats, it is hard to dismiss the adversaries' offensive capabilities. Moreover, insurgencies and battles with multiple fronts occurring at ever -increasing speeds challenge the operators in handling large amounts of information and making effective and timely decisions. In this context, there is a need to shift the paradigm from a stove -pipe, human -in -the -loop -style execution of C2 processes to new concepts and highly integrated models based on cutting -edge AI technology to team with operators. The goal of this paper is to introduce an initial conceptual framework focused on the air domain that leverages from simulations and human -AI teaming to assist the C2 operator with decision -making at the operational level. The Digital Battlespace Framework will be a collection of analytical tools leveraging Machine Learning technologies to generate action recommendations. The operator's feedback, communicated through decisions, will facilitate continuous learning through both imitation and utilization of large data streams, enabling a transition from manual to semi -autonomous systems as the technology matures. In this work, we explore the likely hurdles in implementing the Digital Battlespace Framework, the understanding of context which is lacking in current simulation environments and limitations of the existing and new approaches. To trust the proposed human -AI teaming approach, we rely on explainability and trustworthiness, the pillars for promoting AI as an essential component in the future of defence.

Title: Unsupervised creation of profiles for information management
Authors: Ahmed, O.; Gököz, F.; Schmitz, H.-C.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: For operators to get exactly all relevant information at the right time - and, therefore, neither be underinformed nor overtaxed - information systems, among them Command & Control Information Systems (C2IS), have to be equipped with suitable information filters. Such filters allow for the creation of overlays in a Geographical Information System (GIS) and the selection of messages from a news feed. They assess the relevance of information with respect to the actual situation, on-going processes, the role and the current task of the operator. To that end, respective profiles are to be created. One approach to the creation of profiles is rule-based: filtering criteria are manually defined as rules for assessing information items. An alternative approach that enables a higher degree of automation relies on the analysis of the working context: key entities are extracted from procedural documents and, with reference to lexical databases, transferred to concept graphs. These are linked with respective graphs derived from military information models. Finally, a linked subgraph of an information model can be applied as a profile for information management.

Title: C2Sketch: Rapid Definition and Testing of Task-Oriented C2 Systems
Authors: Lijnse, B.; Jansen, J. M.; Bolderheij, F.; van Asten, B.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Time is a luxury that designers of Command and Control (C2) systems cannot always afford. In times of crisis, when conditions change faster than existing systems can accommodate, organizations need to scramble to create alternatives quickly. In this paper we present C2Sketch: a web-based tool for rapid definition and testing of C2 system designs. It facilitates the specification of C2 system designs as structured task-oriented mission plans. These mission plans cover both organizational aspects as well as information flow and automation to achieve a set of mission goals. To evaluate these plans, C2Sketch has a built-in interpreter that provides a bare-bones C2 system that supports the tasks, roles and actors defined in the mission plans. This allows designs to be tested immediately against (simulated) scenarios. In addition to testing, a basic level of internal consistency and feasibility can be verified automatically. And finally, when plans have been found acceptable, they can be exported as human-readable documents for further implementation. Besides its main purpose as a design aid for C2 system designers, C2Sketch also has potential value in an educational context. The structured no-code approach enables people without software development skills to define and evaluate simple, but complete C2 systems within the time available in a course. Furthermore, the structure imposed by the tool forces novices to think about the key elements of a C2 system. We present C2Sketch's overall design, its key features, and we report on our first experiences using the tool during a C2 course.

Title: Data centric C2-services deployment: an experiment on fleets of military vehicles
Authors: Pingen, G.; van der Geest, J.; Beaujon, M.; Voogd, J.; Pieneman, R.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Defense operational and tactical activities are increasingly shifting towards joint, interagency, multinational and public (JIMP) partners. In addition, C2 is based on data being generated at an increasing rate by an increasing number of sources in the physical, human and information landscape. Future C2 systems need to cope with this varying number of heterogeneous, distributed knowledge bases using an information services architecture, as well as operational circumstances such as varying connectivity. We therefore propose that a data centric approach for the enabling infrastructure may provide a better and future-proof basis than a traditional application centric architecture, which has been a major focal point for IT-design and development over the last decades. Using an experimental data centric architecture, we aim to understand how a vehicle fleet data processing infrastructure can offer: Quicker access to the data(sets) users require for developing their services by automated data synchronization and discovery; Better insights into service performance by automatically gathering telemetry to generate recommendations for fleet managers.

Title: Evaluation of Message Broker approaches for Information Exchange in Disadvantaged Tactical Networks in a Federated Environment
Authors: Johnsen, F. T.; Manso, M.; Jansen, N.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract:
The research task group NATO STO/IST-150 «NATO Core Services profiling for Hybrid Tactical Networks» is working towards profiling Core Services for the tactical domain, which can be described as DIL (disconnected, intermittent and limited) environments. In this context, our work is intended to feed into future spirals of the Federated Mission Networking (FMN) concept, which, until recently, has been mostly focused on fixed/stable infrastructure networks. In IST-150, we specifically address information exchange needs of deployed coalition forces. Hence, our work is an enabler for the future of command and control systems, which will need to use efficient and appropriate solutions that ensure timely delivery of data and information superiority up to the last mile.

In IST-150 we have analyzed and experimented with several message broker mechanisms for information exchange, including the NATO recommended WS-Notification standard for publish/subscribe. We have found that the Message Queuing Telemetry Transport (MQTT) industry standard exhibits low overhead and is worth further studies.

In this paper, we continue our analysis in the application of MQTT to tactical networks. We pursue a comparative experiment between three different approaches to sharing data in a federation. Two of these approaches involve multiple MQTT brokers, using the MQTT bridge and a bespoke MQTT mesh approach, respectively. The third approach is a serverless solution based on UDP. The paper investigates these approaches from a performance viewpoint, varying characteristics such as data exchange frequency, network delay and packet loss in an emulated tactical network. The data exchange consists of Blue Force Tracking information transmitted by all units.  We present the obtained performance results between brokers considering the different network characteristics. Importantly, we identify the brokers' advantages and bottlenecks allowing a better understanding of the conditions in which they can be deployed.
Author Keywords: MESSAGE QUEUING TELEMETRY TRANSPORT (MQTT); PUBLISH/SUBSCRIBE MIDDLEWARE; TACTICAL DISCONNECTED INTERMITTENT LIMITED (DIL) NETWORKS; FEDERATED MISSION NETWORKING (FMN); MULTI-BROKER BRIDGING AND MESH CLUSTERING; UDP-BASED MQTT COMMUNICATION; PERFORMANCE EVALUATION METRICS; BLUE FORCE TRACKING (BFT)

Title: Mathematical Modelling of Headquarters C2 Battle-Rhythm for Multi-Domain Operations
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Over many years I have pursued an approach to mathematically model Command and Control (C2) using the dynamical system formalism of synchronisation of phase oscillators on networks . Here the phases represent the decision states of agents, for example the position in an agent's ObserveOrient-Decide-Act (OODA) loop, the network is the structure of communications between agents, natural frequencies correspond to the decision-making speeds of individual agents when left to themselves, and coupling describes the intensity or responsiveness in interactions between any two agents. Since 2008 I have shown how this formalism can be used to model adversarial C2, the Common Joint Staff System (CJSS), the interactions between longer cycle J5 and shorter cycle J3 staff work, sociotechnical aspects of a headquarters such as the information artefacts used in staff work, and how multi-domain operations can be modelled with both kinetic and non-kinetic warfighting effects. A key open question through all this work is what constitutes the networks ; to date I have largely used organisational charts but with peer links reflecting a typical headquarters work flow, J55, J53/J35 and J33, with supporting intelligence and logistic staff interactions.

Title: One Ring to Rule them All and through the Headquarters Bind them: AI, emergence, and the Planning-Execution-Evaluation continuum in a Fifth Generation
Authors: Kalloniatis, A.; Kwok, H.-W.; Oxenham, M.; Unewisse, M.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: In 2016 Yue, Kalloniatis and Kohn proposed the concept of a “Fifth Generation headquarters” as a future vision of operational headquarters. Here C2 Agility is “baked in” through ICT modernisation based on flexibility, Artificial Intelligence (AI)/Machine-Learning (ML) based-bots or entities, and integrated data analytics. In addressing the structure of such a future organisation this idea was elaborated by one of us in 2018 to identify that planning, execution and evaluation are enduring functions that need to be performed in headquarters. This should manifest a coherent synchronised OODA loop for the whole organisation as an emergent property of the individual decision cycles of human staff and artificial agents in the socio-technical system. AI may produce many highly specialised apps based on AI and ML functions across the vast spectrum of tasks needing to be performed. Thus, the 2018 earlier work proposed that a centralised application (one of us called it ‘Plan-as-an-App’) that threads across the entire planning, execution and evaluation continuum is essential to team the many individual tools that may build up over time as Defence/Defense enterprise ICT achieves a degree of flexibility analogous to that displayed in many civilian social-media systems. In this paper we advance this idea further, to clarify how this may be achieved in realisable systems. Within the context of the theory of organisational design that encompasses the role of technology to aide coordination, we describe the micro-services architectural approach taken by the Defence Science and Technology (DST) Group for achieving a flexible system of ICT implementation. This, in turn, enables a gradual update of individual system applications as AI tools proliferate. We discuss how a ‘teaming’ of specialised tools by an overarching set of applications, services or algorithms may be achieved that spans the planning-execution-evaluation continuum through a combination of top-down and peer-level interactions between system components: orchestration, or centralised control by a component of other components, and choreographing, where peer agents powered by various algorithms update asynchronously. Finally, we describe how current staff processes in headquarters may interact with such a system design.

Title: The Exploitation of C2SIM Standards in Support of Evolving Command and Control Capabilities
Authors: Brook, A.; Patel, B.; Wardman, B.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: This paper describes work by the UK Defence Science and Technology Laboratory (Dstl) working as part of NATO Modelling & Simulation Group 145 (MSG-145) to identify opportunities to exploit the latest Command and Control to Simulation (C2SIM) interoperability standard.  C2SIM has evolved from being a conceptual or experimental capability several years ago to a rigorously proven standard which has been recommended for adoption by NATO.  To have the standard is one thing but the development investment only pays when it can be used to bring benefits to end users.  The exploitation described in the paper covers a number of areas where the C2SIM standard can benefit and support evolving capabilities, including: HQ and collective training; preparation for exercises; supporting coalition and multi-agency operations; application within C2 battle labs; testing policy and procedures; acquisition support; automating routine processes; and man-machine teaming.  The paper will show how C2SIM together with current simulation architectures such as composable, service-based simulations fits in with the requirements needed for these capabilities.  The work has pulled together both UK-specific and international capabilities and is thus widely applicable.

Title: BPMN-Driven Real-Time Mission Awareness
Authors: Krisler, B.; Diller, D.; Miller, J.; Myers, J.; Marshall, T.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Mission awareness is essential for satisfying commander's Intent and ensuring overall mission success. Critical to providing and maintaining mission awareness is the development of a real-time operational picture of the mission. Constructed from vast amounts of disparate data ranging from low-level  position  reports  to  high-level  mission  plans,  this  picture  helps  to  form  a  coherent narrative that provides commanders and mission planners with detailed insight into the mission as it progresses. In this paper we will detail the Advanced Mission Toolkit (AMT), a micro-servicebased solution to providing mission awareness from observed data feeds within the context of a high-level mission plan. An AMT operator constructs mission workflows using Business Process Modeling Notation (BPMN) 2.0 describing activities in the workflow and communication flows within and between the various entities. Ontologies are used to bind BPMN elements with data producers providing the link between real-world assets and modeled constructs. In addition, we have developed a number of custom extensions to BPMN, including the modeling of temporal and spatial constraints, two modules for parsing, and comprehending natural language text, and an alerting system for improving situational awareness for the mission commander. AMT provides a number of visualizations such as mission graph execution and Gantt chart views, for real-time event monitoring and post-mission review. Developed as part of research performed for Air Force Research Labs (AFRL), our approach aggregates and makes sense of multiple data feeds to form an operational picture of a mission in real-time.

Title: Emerging needs for C2 research capability - a literature review
Authors: Svenmarck, P.; Stenius, C.; Jansson, O.; Johansson, B. J. E.; Bengtsson, K.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Research on Command and Control (C2) is cross-disciplinary and covers diverse fields, such as computer science, systems science, behavioral science, organisational science, policy research, operations research, etc. This paper presents a literature review of the growing number of publications on C2. The aim of the literature review was to identify: 1) dominant fields of enquiry in C2 research, 2) current research challenges, and 3) needs for developing research capabilities for further C2 research. The literature review was performed using 1) literature search in Scopus, 2) literature selection of relevant publications based on the research questions, and 3) categorization of publications according to a taxonomy consisting of application domain, research domain, research method, and unit of analysis. The literature review showed that civil and military C2 are the dominant fields of enquiry, but that cross-domain C2 is an emerging application domain. The large variety of research areas investigated made it difficult to identify any particular research challenges. Finally, many publications focused on construction of infrastructures for performing C2 research where infrastructures for research on cross-domain C2 is an emerging research area.

Title: In the face of uncertainty: The effects of presenting increased degrees of imprecise probabilities in risk descriptions in time critical decisionmaking situations
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Effective C2 requires the ability to cope with uncertainty and to make timely decisions in situations often characterised by risk. This experimental study, with 56 participants, investigated how decision-makers handled these problems in a fictive time critical situation. More specifically, the study examined the effects of presenting two different degrees of uncertainty (low/high) on the choice to make a direct decision or to wait for additional information, in order to test if increased degrees of uncertainty lead to more people waiting for information and to longer waiting times. The overall purpose was to contribute to the debate regarding how uncertainty should be communicated to decision-makers, and to our knowledge concerning the practical consequences of presenting uncertainty to decision-makers in time critical situations. The study could not demonstrate any effect of increased degrees of uncertainty on the choice to make a direct decision or to wait for additional information. Neither could the study demonstrate any effect on the waiting time. However, the results show that almost all of the participants in both the experimental and the control group decided to wait for additional information, and that most of them showed little restraint regarding their waiting time. These results strengthen the conclusion from a previous study by Rydmark, Kuylenstierna, and Tehler (2020) - that presenting uncertainty in risk descriptions can be a practical problem in time critical decision-making situations, and that educating decision-makers in handing these problems may be required if uncertainty is to be presented in these kinds of situations.

Title: Time aspects of command and control
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The command and control (C2) research community mainly agree upon the overall purpose of C2 - to generate desired effects in an operational environment thereby fulfilling stated goals. This purpose indicates an action oriented approach that focuses on the C2 process and its constituent activities. This paper inquires the persistently elusive question of when the C2 process starts and ends. This question has a strong linkage to whether the C2 process begins by a top-down and beforehand need or if it is commonly initiated as a reaction to a, more or less sudden and unforeseen, situation or event. These two different approaches have significant impact on C2 theory, for instance how much effort should be dedicated to preparation before an operation/mission, and indeed if these approaches are compatible within the same theoretical framework at all. Further, this paper surveys the often mentioned need for speed in regard to the C2 process - to get inside the enemy´s decision cycle. This line of thought is in turn related to the choice between an analytical or intuitive decision making approach in the C2 process. This paper applies a literature review method that compares a national C2 process standard with an international equivalent.

Title: C2 support in complex and dynamic situations
Authors: van de Kuijt, J. T.; Lassche, D.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The objective of this study is to provide further insight  in  the  required  C2  support  functionalities  for decision-making in complex and dynamic situations. Most current C2 systems are focused on the physical landscape and have little attention for the human and informational landscape. Furthermore, they can rarely cope with dynamic circumstances, large amounts of data and multiple (non)military (JIMP) actors. Therefore this study is aimed at exploring how situational awareness (SA) can continuously be obtained and decisions can be optimally supported, while operating in ecosystems that are characterized by high complexity  and  high  dynamics.  Specifically,  the  study  will  elaborate  on  dealing  with  these  complex dynamics by being able to assess the scope and the consequences of a changed situation. We will define a framework for decision support  to provide decision makers with insight in whether  courses of action (CoA's) are  still  worthwhile  following a change in the operational environment. What does a changed situation in the operational environment means for consequences, for the costs ('spent' resources ) and benefits (positive and unintended effects) of these CoA's ? For example, will it be sufficient to adjust the amount of resources by  downscaling or upscaling? Or is it necessary to choose a new course of action and to assign available resources to other priorities?  In order to assess this, this paper offers a framework for describing costs and benefits by exploring the use of different indicators and measures, and ways to visualize this in a comprehensive manner.  The results will stimulate future military forces in reflecting on how to do the right things at the right time in these complex dynamic mission environments. 1

Title: Problems and considerations of sensor C2 in a future operational environment ⋆
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract:
The development of ballistic missiles, more effective cruise missiles, and increased sensor capabilities imply both new capabilities and challenges in a future operational environment. One effect this has is that the time from detection of a threat, to being able to respond, and to act on it will be significantly reduced. This article illuminates some current and future threats that are emerging and will be part of an increasingly complex battle space. The purpose of the research is to test and evaluate different C2 solutions for future ISR. The benefit of the proposed research is that it can provide design propositions for future ISR systems and its C2 function. In addition, the research may support design and acquisition of future automated systems and AI. Framing a conceivable development of a future battlefield and its character is important to obtain sufficient realism under the conditions that the proposed experimental series is intended to test. For the experimental series, the microworld C3Fire is currently under consideration as a platform for the trials. This, among other things, for its good configuration possibilities and being able to create reliable experimental conditions.

being developed that are both smarter and can achieve supersonic speed. One consequence of this is that such weapons systems are becoming increasingly difficult to detect and that the ability to fight them in time are becoming increasingly limited. What the concept of hybrid warfare and MDO really means for future operations is still under debate (e.g., see Atkins, 2018; Wass de Czege, 2020), however following the definition of how hybrid warfare should be understood contributes to create a conceivable development of future operations (Cullen and Reichborn-Kjennerud, 2017; Monaghan, 2019):

[T]he synchronized use of multiple instruments of power tailored to specific vulnerabilities across the full spectrum of societal functions to achieve synergistic effect.

In turn MDO is intended to wrest the advantage from potential adversaries and restore a credible conventional deterrent and war-fighting capability against peer competitors (Johnson, 2018) and can be characterised as a combination of operations from multiple domains for example by the following attributes:

- An operation in one domain can support operations in another domain.
- It can generate offensive and defensive effects independently and from different domains.
- It is capable of presenting multiple, simultaneous dilemmas for an adversary.
- It is conducted at a tempo that an adversary cannot match.

Title: Is the Future of C2 Doctrine Anti-Positivist?
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: This paper applies the theme of the 25 th ICCRTS - the future of C2 - to C2 doctrine. It surveys and reflects on twenty examples of C2 doctrinal publications in the open literature going back 25 years to the 1 st ICCRTS in 1995. Using Jackson's (2013) monograph on the evolution of military doctrine and Bousquet's (2009) study of complexity and chaos in warfare as the analytic lens, it assesses the extent to which theory and concepts from C2 R&D have influenced C2 doctrine in military operations and emergency management. Jackson (2013) identified four schools of doctrinal ontology, all based on a positivist epistemic foundation, characterised by rationality and objectivity. Positivist doctrine failed in Iraq, Afghanistan, Libya, and Syria, in part because of the complexity of orchestrating international coalitions. New doctrine manuals stress the need to enquire into the nature of the problem, rather than applying standard procedures. While the physical domain requires rational and objective thinking, increasing chaos and complexity in the social domain demands more emphasis on intangible factors and participants' subjective experience. Emerging concepts in C2 doctrine - particularly in the UK and in Australia - have begun to embrace chaos and complexity, foreshadowing Bousquet's (2009) chaoplexic warfare 1 . Looking ahead to the future, this paper evaluates two possible theories for operating under chaos and complexity: Rittel & Webber's (1973) formulation of wicked problems and Snowdon & Boone's (2007) Cynefin framework. One outcome is a new insight into that old mainstay of C2 doctrine: Boyd's OODA loop.

Title: Assessing the Endeavor Space Using the C3Fire Microworld
Authors: Bjurling, O.; Granlund, R.; Johansson, B. J. E.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The ever-increasing complexity of 21st century endeavors has forced organizations to develop and implement new coping mechanisms. A framework that describes these mechanisms is Command and Control (C2) Agility, which is the ability of an entity to (re)organize following different C2 approaches-ranging from centralized  to  fully  networked  structures-to  cope  with  change.  These  approaches  are  theorized  to  be appropriate  for  managing  endeavors  with  different  complexities  or  characteristics.  These  characteristics, described in the Endeavor Space model, have been suggested to consist of three subjective or perceived dimensions: tractability, dynamics, and dependencies. Previous research into implementing and manipulating these in human-in-the-loop studies have found promising but inconclusive support for the three dimensions. This  paper  presents  a  study  conducted  using  the  Command,  Control,  and  Communication  (C3)Fire microworld where 14 participants completed two forest firefighting scenarios-designed to be distinctly different in complexity according to the Endeavor Space model-and rated their complexity using a selfassessment  questionnaire.  Analyses  of  the  questionnaire  and  simulator  performance  data  found  that  the  scenarios differed significantly in perceived tractability, dynamics, and dependencies, along with perceived difficulty and objective performance scores. Furthermore, difficulty was found to correlate with tractability and dynamics, and objective performance score correlated with dynamics and difficulty. Taken together, these results provide tentative support for the Endeavor Space model as a description of endeavor complexity. The paper discusses the theoretical and empirical implications for the Endeavor Space model and how it can be studied, and suggestions for measuring subjective endeavor complexity are proposed.

Title: Sowing  Discord,  Countering  Fear:  Force  Protection  and  Resilience  to Disinformation
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Disinformation has long played a pivotal role in war and continues to do so. From nation-states to extremists, state and non-state actors seek to leverage the resulting fear and anxiety to strategic benefit. As a result, military decision-makers must contend with a bewildering threat landscape. Often targeted at civilians, disinformation  also  represents  a  pressing  threat  to  force protection. Today's military members are more closely tied to the wider world than any preceding generation. Even on deployment, they can be reached and influenced. This  paper  examines  how  adversarial  actors  have  historically  used  disinformation  to  mislead military populations and whether there are recognizable patterns in these campaigns. Secondly, it looks at how targeted countries have generally responded to falsity. Growing evidence indicates the best way to protect audiences from disinformation is to educate them to recognize it. Therefore, it  will also examine if and how armed forces have educated personnel to distinguish fact from fiction and what current initiatives are underway in key NATO countries, like the US and Canada. Disinformation played a particularly prominent role during the Cold War (1945-1989). As such, this paper focuses on reviewing these years and looks at both periods of open war and what would now be described as 'grey zone' conflict.  Therefore,  it  will  principally  look  at  disinformation spread  by  the  West's  primary  adversary  during  this  period,  Russia.  The  paper  concludes  with preliminary observations on lessons we can derive from the past and vital gaps still to be addressed.
