Title: Towards a Deception Detection Framework for Social Media
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The democratization of communication media has had significant consequences for military command and control. Never before have adversaries had such free and direct access to our local populations allowing for influence through propaganda, disinformation and deception. In fact, social media platforms can help target messages  to  the  exact  demographic  desired,  while  keeping  attribution  hidden.  Commanders  have  been reluctant  to  embrace  the  new  communication  technologies  which  has  resulted  in  playing  catch  up. Meanwhile,  our  opponents  have  infiltrated  our  communication  spaces  and  'recruited'  thousands  of followers who spread their messages unwittingly. This paper will present a new research framework for deception  that  will  help  overcome  the  issues  of  attribution  of  message  originators.  Concentrating  on uncovering narratives, methods and intent rather than individuals alleviates many ethical problems of social media analytics within western societies. The framework will help to guide research on deception detection and increase assessment confidence for intelligence analysts and public affairs involved in the ongoing information environment clashes of power and politics.

Title: C2 as a Capability in the Land Environment: principles of road map design
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The  UK  Development,  Concepts  and  Doctrine  Centre  (DCDC)  publication,  Joint  Concept Note (JCN) 2/17, Future of Command and Control , makes a case for considering Command and Control (C2) as a sociotechnical system and developing it as a capability in its own right. This recognises that C2 has traditionally been developed as a set of individual capabilities (people, processes, structures and technology) in the absence of an overall vision, with only local  coherence  between  such  capabilities  and  no  guarantee  of  their  convergence  as  a coherent  or  effective  C2  system.    In  2018,  the  British  Army  commissioned  research  to develop a draft road map for C2 development in the Land Environment out to 2035, in line with these key JCN 2/17 arguments.  This concept paper discusses the design principles for that draft road map, including the consideration of transformational change vs. adaptational (or evolutionary) change, integration of distinct lines of capability development, governance and road map visualisation.  The paper argues that such principles are applicable beyond the  Land  Environment  and  across  other  nations  that  are  grappling  with  C2  capability development.

Title: Experimental Evaluation of the Mockets Communications Protocol at the Tactical Edge
Authors: Freeman, J.; Gregory, T.; Lee, M.; Michaelis, J.; Trout, T.
Source title: 25th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Successful convergence of cross-domain capabilities is dependent on rapid and continuous interoperability and integration across joint military services, interagency, and multinational partners. Despite these requirements, tactical networking environments are often characterized by limited bandwidth and unreliable connectivity due to their wireless ad-hoc nature. Systems and applications linking sensors in support of joint force operations can produce high volumes of data, yet capacity for information dissemination across domains and echelons can easily be constrained by challenging network environments. For wireless networks, such challenges include frequent disconnections, congestion, packet loss, and data delivery latency due to backlogs in both application and network queues. In particular, wireless applications using the traditional TCP/IP suite, designed for wired networks, can suffer from inadequate communication performance. The potential consequences to missions could range from missed opportunities to execution of actions based on outdated information. The Mockets (Mobile Sockets) communications protocol is designed to ensure the timely and reliable delivery of data in tactical wireless environments. This paper discusses experimentation on the performance of Mockets in supporting client-to-server data transmission on the U.S. Army's Integrated Sensor Architecture (ISA), focusing on metrics of timeliness and robustness of data transport. Qualitative analysis of obtained results demonstrate that Mockets can improve message transmission performance under constrained network environments.

Title: Internet of Hybrid UAV-UGV Things for Empowering Command and Control
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Integrated forms of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) and Unmanned Ground Vehicles (UGVs), called hybrid UAV-UGV, will widely be used in many military and civilian applications. To support decision-making in Command and Control, it is important to connect hybrid UAV-UGVs into a network of hybrid UAV-UGV Things. Each hybrid UAV-UGV has a large number of 'things' which generate massive information packets and transmit them to a base station. A multichannel Slotted ALOHA based massive machine-type communication (mMTC) provides the network connectivity for the transmission of information packets. In multichannel Slotted ALOHA, multiple parallel fixed size slots exist. If only one packet is transmitted into a given slot, then the receiver can receive those packets. In the case of multiple packets transmitted into the same slot, a collision occurs and packets are discarded. By leveraging machine learning tools, the 'collisions among the packets transmitted into the same slot' problem was transformed into a preamble-based exploration learning problem and used as a solution for fast retransmission retrials of collided packets even before the collisions occur. Generalized analytical expressions for maximum throughput and minimum delay with and without machine learning empowered fast retransmission trials for a finite number of things are presented in this paper. The study indicates that the machine learning empowered fast retrial may reduce the delay up to a factor of 0.613. Results can be used in all Slotted ALOHA based protocols.

Title: The Swedish pandemic landscape on twitter - An exploratory study using statistical methods
Authors: Schüler, M.; Johansson, K.; Matuszczyk, J. V.; Robinson, Y.
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: During the Covid-19 pandemic social media have become an important tool for spreading information from government agencies regarding restrictions. Government accounts and public health care organizations have used different social media platforms such as Twitter to communicate with the Swedish public. The Swedish public have interacted with the information, arguing for a stricter or a  more  relaxed  approach  to  Covid-19  recommendations.  This  social  network  analysis  aims  at  exploring  statistical  methods  to investigate patterns made by twitter accounts commenting the Swedish Armed Forces field hospital activities and the national Covid19 strategy during the Covid-19 pandemic. Data was collected using the twitter platform and the Ncapture add-on with Google Chrome. The interactions stored in the tweets and replies section (TRS) from 227 twitter accounts were collected and coded with the NVivo auto code function. Twitter usernames that occurred in less than 35 % of the 227 TRS were deleted. The 227 extracted TRS were treated as scale items and occurring twitter-names which interacted with the TRS as respondents n=761. Analysis of the factor structure with PCA and CFA indicated four factors: 1) Military policy, 2) Right wing politics, 3) Law enforcement, 4) Politics and strategy. Structural Equation Modelling revealed interrelationships between the factors. Thus, Military policy, Law enforcement and Politics and strategy had a direct effect on Right wing politics. Politics and strategy had a direct effect on Military policy and Law enforcement. This study revealed that PCA, CFA and SEM have the potential to discover the core of a thought collective. Despite the obvious dangers with misinformation and political extremism on social media, policymakers need to tackle misinformation and disinformation, protecting electoral processes and facilitating public discussion, built on the three fundamental democratic principles of equality, representation and participation.

Title: Clustering and Aggregating Battle Space Objects
Authors: Stucken, S.; Gökgöz, F.; Schmitz, H.-C.; Lena, P.
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Assessing a complete situational picture of red forces in a battlefield comes as a difficult task to achieve. We approximately know the type of red forces that are expected, the red doctrinal rules as well as the terrain in the theatre of operations. In addition, we continuously gather tactical reconnaissance information on red battle space objects (BSO). By taking terrain-related constraints into consideration, we can cluster the detected BSOs in order to determine the red centre of gravity and assess the opponent's combat  power.  By  referring  to  expectations  regarding  the  opponent's  ORBAT  and  knowledge  of  the  doctrinal  rules,  we  can aggregate detected BSOs to units of higher echelons (tanks can be assigned to platoons, platoons to companies, etc.). Additionally, we can identify further reconnaissance targets: for instance, let us assume that we detected a tank platoon. A tank platoon is always part of a tank company. Therefore, further tank platoons must be in the same area. Where are they located?  We can approximately identify  their  potential  locations  by  taking  into  consideration  the  maximal  area  that  can  be  covered by  a  company  as  well  as obstacles (for instance, rivers). Thereby, we can also identify potential unit boundaries. That is, we can both enrich the situational picture and determine further reconnaissance targets. Our paper describes unsupervised learning approaches for the clustering of BSOs (DBSCAN, k-means, among others) and relates them to a rule-based automatic aggregation. We discuss their application for supporting C2 staff in information enrichment and situation assessment. As proof of concept, we have implemented respective clustering and aggregation services within a C2IS framework.

Title: Predicting Success in Training of Navy Aviators
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: This project investigated patterns in the training data of U.S. Navy aviators in an attempt to predict their success in training. We assembled a database from many sources of training data. This database covered 18,596 pilot and Naval Flight Officer candidates through their pretesting, classroom instruction, training in generic aircraft, and training in specialized aircraft. This data was hard to organize because it had incompatible formats and missing data. After standardizing the formats and fixing errors in the data, and aggregating sparse training records to a smaller set of average scores, we had 301 features for the candidates. We then correlated their features using both numeric-correlation and nonnumeric-association (class-characterization) methods. We identified 38 measures of success in the program, and particularly focused on correlations involving those. We did confirm some early indicators of success and failure in the program that had not been noticed before. We conclude that the Navy is doing a good job of identifying candidates likely to be successful, but additional factors should be considered. with the degree of eventual candidate success.

Title: MIP Information Model 5 The Next Generation of the C2 Semantic Reference Model and its Eco-System
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: To ensure interoperability in multinational operations, coalition partners must interpret shared information in the same way. The MIP Information Model (MIM) defines a common semantics for the Command & Control (C2) domain that supports this. The MIM is not only the semantic foundation for future Land C2 information exchange in the Federated Mission Networking (FMN) but also acts as a semantic reference model in the NATO Core Data Framework (NCDF). Communities of Interest (COIs) can adopt the MIM for developing interoperability specifications in support of their specific processes. For that purpose, the MIM comes with a comprehensive tool suite. In this paper, we provide an overview of the concepts of the MIM and our approach to annotate the model with semantic information. We present the tools and services supporting the MIM - its eco-system. In detail, we describe a strict, toolsupported approach to derive messages from the MIM that are structurally and semantically compliant with the model. Finally, we explain how the MIM supports cross-COI information sharing, using the example of an exchange between the CIS and C2 domain.

Title: Speed vs thought
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The purpose of this paper is to discuss the conception of speed in command and control (C 2 ) systems' impact on human thought  and  to  introduce  the  notion  of  'Combat  Integrated  Learning'  (CIL).  In  a  context  of  increased  complexity, unpredictability and ambiguity, adaptation not only concerns agility in terms of speed, but through resilience, selfhealing and - in lack of calculable consequence ethics - an Aristotelian notion of virtue as a stable equilibrium of the soul as a basis for choice of action 'knowingly and for its own sake'. As humans we have many cognitive functions which impact how we perceive information i.e., sensemaking and situation awareness. But the essence of thinking is a philosophical issue where philosophers like Arendt previously explained the emergence of banal evil as ordinary men having lost their ability to think. C 2 systems that make us reactive to a piece of information, inciting speed to act, minimize human reflection and learning. Developing time effective C 2 systems could in fact lead to the creation of military stupidity. Authority, organizational and administrative procedures may lead to normalization of risk, morally questionable attitudes and actions, routinized humiliation and behavior that ultimately break down sensemaking and the sense of 'self' in degraded environments. C 2 systems and their uses may hamper intelligent action through the loss of an active agency of the individual, thus impede understanding and management of the battlespace, especially in the light of deception and disinformation, and unclear causal relations of events in constrained environments. This paper points at potential good practices in the face of ambiguity.

Title: Arabic Script Textual Information Extraction from Image Sources via Deep Learning
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The use of intelligence in the command and control process is vital in defining mission objectives, and ensuring mission accomplishment. Canadian Armed Forces (CAF) collect multitudes of images containing textual information as a source of intelligence prior, during, and post operations that range from scanned documents gathered on missions, to conversation screenshots in open source intelligence work. Automated search for target information in these resources require comprehension of their textual content via approaches such as Optical Character Recognition (OCR). While OCR technologies are relatively mature for Latin script languages such as English, their application for other languages remain challenging. Given CAF's operational requirements, languages using Arabic script are of particular interest, and yet no such image-to-text technology exists to achieve an acceptable baseline due to a small niche audience, and Arabic-specific difficulties such as cursive nature, ligatures, or significant font variations. This problem is especially hard for handwritten Arabic script which differs considerably from the typed format. In this work, we focus on state-of-the-art deep learning architecture for Arabic script recognition, with an emphasis on the Tesseract Engine. Building a complete pipeline, we aim to improve the recognition accuracy by the careful selection of pipeline components, such as enhanced pre-processing. The inference results are consequently compared to the state-of-the-art by Google Vision. The solution is analyzed when applied to CAF use-case scenarios, based on which recommendations for future improvements are provided.

Title: Cyber Risk to Mission:  Parametric Screening Model
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: To understand the appropriateness of a C2 Approach for endeavors undertaken in contested cyber environments requires the ability to observe, measure, or predict the level of Cyber Risk to Mission (CRM). This paper presents a simple CRM Parametric Screening Model that utilizes the key drivers of CRM. These drivers take the form of model parameters, the selection of which is based upon a CRM Assessment methodology and ELICIT experiments which have been previously reported on as well as analyses of cyber-related game play. The sensitivity of predicted CRM to changes in the model's parameters is discussed.  This parametric screening model is designed to help mission stakeholders better understand the mission risk they face, the options they each have in managing the risk, and the inter-dependencies among the decisions they make and the actions they take.  This initial version of the screening model will be revised as empirical evidence accumulates and more detailed models are developed.
Author Keywords: CYBER RISK TO MISSION; PARAMETRIC SCREENING MODEL; DEFENSE-IN-DEPTH; CYBER DEPENDENCY INDEX; REMEDIATION AND RESTORATION EFFECTIVENESS; RISK TAXONOMY; SENSITIVITY ANALYSIS; CYBER THREAT AND DAMAGE ASSESSMENT

Title: Psychology of Agile C2 in the Joint Space
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Command and control achieves subordinate adherence (control) to superior's intent (command). However a complex battlespace may frustrate the commander's capacity to respond accurately and with sufficient detail. Agile C2 assumes the HQ to adjust to meet increasing battlespace complexity by delegating to subordinates within the remit of a broader command intent. Joint is a particular arena that lends itself to Agile C2 because it, by definition, oversees multiple services co-acting and cooperating simultaneously, while also contending with growing battle space complexity. However the psychology necessary for commanders and echelons to enact Agile C2 is under-examined, and indeed may be problematic since higher ranks populate the Joint space, who are unfamiliar with shallow hierarchy. This conceptual work addresses a gap in the C2 literature related to psychology that varies by perceived power distance and its respective hierarchical or egalitarian culture. The ComPILAR model of the group (continuum Prospects, Involved, Liked, Agency, Respect) is proposed as one paradigm of psychology that varies with power distance. Teaching ComPILAR to Joint space actors is recommended to enable the rapid psychological adaptation required by Agile C2.

Title: Categorization of AI-Enabled Command and Control in Ballistic Missile Defense
Authors: Byun, M. H.; Moon, I. C.
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: In many fields of our society, AI technology is being rapidly developed and incorporated into various areas. These advances in AI bring the organizations to be automated by affecting their decision-making process. In particular, many countries endeavors applying AI to military operations. The objective of this paper is to analyze the impact of AI technology development on C2, related to Tactical Ballistic Missile Defense (TBMD) operation, and to suggest how C2 should be changed in an AI-driven environment. The levels of AI applied to TBMD operation are classified from zero to four according to the following criteria: the degree of AI technology in the TBMD operation process, the degree of decentralization of C2, and the degree of autonomy of subordinate units. Through the case study, this paper discusses the current TBMD level on the Iron Dome of Israel.
Author Keywords: AI; TBMD; C2

Title: Human-in-the-Loop Disinformation Detection: Stance, Sentiment, or Something Else?
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Both  politics  and  pandemics  have  recently  provided  ample  motivation  for  the  development  of  machine  learning-enabled disinformation (or 'fake news') detection algorithms. Existing literature has focused primarily on the fully-automated case, but the resulting  techniques  cannot  reliably  detect  disinformation  on  the  varied  topics,  sources,  and  time  scales  required  for  military applications. By leveraging an already-available analyst as a human-in-the-loop, however, the canonical machine learning techniques of  sentiment  analysis,  aspect-based  sentiment  analysis,  and  stance  detection  become  plausible  methods  to  use  for  a  partiallyautomated disinformation detection system. This paper aims to determine which of these techniques is best suited for this purpose and  how  each  technique  might  best  be  used  towards  this  end.  Training  datasets  of  the  same  size  and  nearly  identical  neural architectures (a BERT transformer as a word embedder with a single feed-forward layer thereafter) are used for each approach, which are then tested on sentiment- and stance-specific datasets to establish a baseline of how well each method can be used to do the other tasks. Four different datasets relating to COVID-19 disinformation are used to test the ability of each technique to detect disinformation on a topic that did not appear in the training data set. Quantitative and qualitative results from these tests are then used to provide insight into how best to employ these techniques in practice.
Author Keywords: HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP DISINFORMATION DETECTION; STANCE DETECTION; ASPECT-BASED SENTIMENT ANALYSIS; BERT-BASED TRANSFORMER MODELS; OPEN-DOMAIN MISINFORMATION CLASSIFICATION; COVID-19 MISINFORMATION DATASETS; HYBRID HUMAN-AI SYSTEMS; DEFENSE-ORIENTED INFORMATION ENVIRONMENT

Title: Command and control perspective of defence against intelligent hypersonic weapons
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Defence against smart hypersonic weapons (HWs) not only reduces available tactical response time, but also imposes deeper thinking about strategic posture for improving successful defence of allied infrastructure and mobile assets. Existing intercontinental ballistic missile (ICBM) defence approaches can address threats from HWs up to a certain point. Depending on situations, HWs may reduce flight path lengths and times to targets by about 10% over ICBMs. For targets within 10000 km range, an ICBM may take about 25 to 40 minutes to striking, for HWs it may take 22 to 36 minutes. A challenging aspect is HWs claimed ability to evade missile defence systems. The real game change is when HW launching platforms move closer to intended targets. Launching platforms could be submarines, ships or warplanes. Such an opposing force strategy may reduce flight paths from 10000 km to 1000 or 100 km, shortening times to intended targets to about 2 to 4 minutes or worst to 13 to 21 seconds, making target/area defence difficult. A strategic posture needs to reduce, as much as possible, the likelihood of launching platforms moving too close to potential intended targets. Consequently, defending against new HWs needs to address opposing forces launching platform moves. The tactical perspective includes the extreme time pressure for developing course-of-actions (CoAs) in a very short timeframe or compression of sensor-to-effector loop (StEL). Previously, we found that reducing human interventions in such response process, by using artificial intelligence and cognitive network-based enhancements, accelerates knowledge acquisition, sharing awareness, and timely development of effective CoAs toward the desired goal or end state. We identified this as the cognitive StEL (CStEL). Therefore, in order to defeat HW attacks, CStELs may prove to be an appropriate approach due to its accelerated response time with automatic threat recognition.

Title: Improving Decisions: Conceptualising the Antecedents and Impact of Unique Opinion Expression
Authors: Mr. William Tang*, Mr. Sebastian Tsui*, Mrs. Yoona Choi*, Dr. Wesley McTernan*
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: To confront the complex, unpredictable and dynamic scenarios of contemporary and future warfare, the Australian Defence Force recognises  the  requirement  for  its  operational  headquarters  to  achieve  Agile  Command  and  Control.  Our  investigation  aims  to increase our understanding between the relationship of team dynamics, unique opinion expression (dissent) and decision quality within inter-connected, complex teams in operational headquarters. Our conceptual model integrates social and organisational psychological theory and superordinates various constructs into three categories of disparity, culture and composition. We posit that a  fairer  distribution  of  power  and  resources  (disparity),  more  varied  teams  (composition)  and  feedback-seeking  environments (culture) increase a team member's willingness to express unique opinions. More unique opinion expression within military tea ms will ultimately benefit decision quality by lowering harmful cognitive constructs such as information cascades, group polarisation and shared information bias. We contribute a novel, psychological model of how team dynamics impact decisions within operational headquarters through unique opinion expression. The current paper is part of a multi-phasic project to measure, evaluate and provide advice to operational headquarters on beneficial and harmful team dynamics. Through identification and intervention of helpful or harmful team dynamics, research can enhance decision quality within operational headquarters and close the gap between current capabilities and contemporary and future warfare requirements.  The theoretical and practical implications of the conceptual model are discussed.
Author Keywords: ORGANIZATIONAL DISSENT; TEAM DECISION QUALITY; PSYCHOLOGICAL SAFETY; POWER DISPARITY; TEAM COMPOSITION DIVERSITY; GROUPTHINK MITIGATION; INFORMATION CASCADES; MILITARY COMMAND AND CONTROL

Title: A Corpus of Military Communication
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: A corpus of military communication is a large collection of texts from the military domain. It comprises a wide range of text types, including standardisation agreements, central service regulations, field manuals and other training material, collections  of  commands  and  reports  as  well  as  transcripts  of  radio  communication.  Ideally,  a  corpus  of  military communication is multilingual. It can serve as an empirical basis for investigations into military language use. Among these  are  lexico-semantic  investigations  that  contribute  to  the  proper  definition  of  military  concepts  in  semantic reference models for interoperability solutions. Furthermore, the corpus can provide training data for natural language processing solutions. For example, it can be used to create military word embeddings.  A comprehensive corpus of military communication is a desideratum. For the design of a military corpus, we can refer to existing large language corpora. However, the integration of classified material remains a challenge. In our paper, we will present an initiative for creating a military corpus along with a first prototype.
Author Keywords: MILITARY COMMUNICATION CORPUS; MULTILINGUAL TEXT RESOURCES; LEXICO-SEMANTIC ANALYSIS; DOMAIN-SPECIFIC WORD EMBEDDINGS; NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING FOR DEFENSE; METADATA STANDARDS (DUBLIN CORE); DATA SECURITY AND CLASSIFICATION; UNSUPERVISED LEARNING FOR INFORMATION MANAGEMENT

Title: Towards cross-validation of C2-Harmonisation Hypotheses: Comparing ELICIT simulation and Network Synchronisation modelling approaches to Command and Control
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: In this paper we compare alternative approaches to modelling C2, particularly with a longer term goal to understand the role of Artificial Intelligence and Autonomy within C2 systems. NATO-SAS-143 has developed the concept of C2 Harmonisation to test the consistency of C2 approaches across diverse organisational entities that come together for Multi-Domain missions. The ELICIT agent modelling approach has been used by this research to test hypotheses within this concept. One of the present authors has proposed an alternative modelling approach based on synchronising phase oscillators coupled on a network, which has itself been shown to map to a range of C2 contexts, from headquarters sociotechnical systems, adversarial C2 'Blue-v-Red' competition, and warfighting with both kinetic and non-kinetic effects. Here, C2 'actors' - and the information elements and artefacts they manipulate - are represented as interacting OODA loops; the system is assessed based on its capacity to achieve OODA-synchronisation. We adapt a scenario used in ELICIT by NATO-SAS-143 and map it to the synchronisation approach. We show how C2 Harmonisation can naturally be tested here and set up the means to compare results with those from ELICIT. We argue that, with the complexity of real-world C2 systems,  such  cross-validation  across  diverse  modelling  and  experimentation  approaches  can  provide  true  robust  evidence  for change in C2

Title: Artificial Intelligence Success for Mobility Nodes
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Mobility Nodes is a Joint Concept applying robotic and artificial intelligence (AI) technologies for SMET, MUM-T (Manned and Unmanned Teaming) and autonomous missions. Dismounted squads and Joint Terminal Air Controllers (JTAC/TACP) are enabled with unmanned systems requiring joint capability area attributes for combined J6 JADC2 and AD EOD missions. Success of AI applications employ machine learning / deep learning with predictive analysis to shape and persuade audiences. Tactical maneuver systems deliver ISR by way of Sensors for Situational Awareness and C2OTM is effective only with mobility. This is achieved when shape terrain obstacles are detected and if need be neutralized for dismounted to have assured mobility and protection when under fire while providing Situational Awareness to Commanders for cross-domain maneuvers.

Title: Possible consequences for command and control of shifting coordination form: Navigating the murky waters of adverse situations and complex missions
Authors: Stensrud, R.; Valaker, S.; Haugen, R.; Mikkelsen, B.
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: In this article we analyze the integration of activities in a joint combined military operation, where land, air, sea and special operations forces contribute with specific functions (e.g. planning) and activities (the use of kinetic and nonkinetic effectors, and sensors e.g. tasking authority (TA)) in order to efficiently strike targets. One challenges is: How to ensure coordination of capabilities is maintained and done in an agreed and efficient manner for the organization despite dynamic external and internal challenges? We elaborate on the influence of command and control (C2) states, i.e. coordination form, meaning who is enacting coordination functions. Prior research have emphasized that different coordination forms (e.g. centralized, decentralized, small world network) are appropriate under various external and internal contingencies. One of the significant challenges is congested, contested and denied environment such as in an A2AD context (e.g. cyber threats) which influences the complexity and dynamics of the missions. This should suggest a need to dynamically shift coordination form, yet few have examined how to make such shifts. We propose a model and framework where: 1) entities hold updated information about the current coordination form (e.g. information about who is delegated/claiming the responsibility to coordinate), and 2) use known criteria for shifts in coordination form (e.g. shifts from centralized to decentralized or small world network) systematically and based on feedback and feedforward. Such a coordination concept could reduce coordination costs (e.g. due to communication overload in the organizational structure leading to delays of communicating coordinating decisions) as well as ensure reliable shifts between who is responsible for coordination. We propose that shifts in coordination form could be an example of C2 agility. We sketch a framework, hypothesis and suggested methods. Lastly, we discuss briefly some implications related to multi-domain and joint all domain operations, as well as the role of technology.

Title: The eradication of barriers to organisational learning in a command and control environment.
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: A learning organisation is key to effective command and control (C2) operations. Recent reviews of organisational learning (OL) within UK Defence have identified some primary challenges for OL from an operational C2 perspective. Previously, the Defence Science and Technology Laboratory (Dstl) have briefed on the thematic analysis of data sources, where comparisons were made to analytical frameworks created by Dstl - the MARVEL model was used to explore interaction effects between themes identified that impede OL (M. Fewtrell et al, 25th ICCRTS, 2020). This paper builds upon the thematic analysis of qualitative data sources completed during the previous years of the study and highlights those factors which promote good OL practice in Defence and Government. The aim was to map real world issues with theoretical ones identified through various data sources including; interview summaries with military staff, insights from a focussed survey and academic literature on OL. This work has reviewed existing models which may aid direct organisational interventions and derived mitigating concepts in order to improve C2 OL capability. The study concluded that a number of OL best practises could be translated to an operational C2 environment with good effect, most critically refinement of effective knowledge management systems and OL tools. It also concluded that there is an unmet need for more effective OL forums, both face to face and on-line, for the sharing and discussion of information to allow for more productive OL in a C2 setting - this was highlighted as a follow-up action and the implementation of practical good practice through workshops is proposed to align UK Defence to a common OL standard.

Title: A Distributed Architecture for Tactical Data Link Interoperability
Authors: Botelho, T. de A. T.; Cecílio, E. L.; Oliveira, A. F. de
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Military forces rely on automatic data links (ADL) to securely share messages between air, ground, and naval platforms. Since different devices use different types of ADL for communications, a highly sophisticated ADL gateway is necessary to translate information between different types of links. The Multi Data Link Processor (MDLP) project team, sponsored by Brazilian Ministry of Defense, develops a set of gateways in a distributed architecture that will allow the exchange of messages between tactical networks using distinct ADL used during Brazilian Armed Forces Joint Operations, e.g., the Yb and STERNA naval ADL, the BR1 and BR2 Air Force ADL, and tactical networks using SDR and radars. Once we have the Interface Control Definition it is possible to translate messages from links 16 and 22 in operation by NATO. After a successful proof of concept developed in 2020, as published in ICCRTS '20, the first operational step will be building the set of prototype gateways needed to provide interoperability in real time between two Brazilian Navy links: Yb and STERNA. These two ADL have different syntaxes and semantics and also different time frames. Besides providing interoperability between them, the set of gateways will send information from these tactical ADL to strategic level, using JC2IEDM and ADEM messages. Data from radars not connected in any of these ADL will also be received by a gateway and be provided for them. This article presents the main results achieved in the MDLP first stage of development.

Title: Multi-Domain Battlespace Management Visualisation
Authors: Borras, C.; Carrier, B. S.; Guest, R.; Gillgren, J.
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: This paper describes the development of a Multi-Domain Battlespace Management (MDBM) Visualisation Capability Concept Demonstrator (CCD) undertaken by the UK Defence Science Technology Laboratory (Dstl) in collaboration with Industry. The aim of the CCD was to explore how current and future technologies and techniques would enable decision makers in Command and Control (C2) environments to maximise their situation awareness (SA) and improve decision making out to 2040. The CCD takes a human centred approach to provide a seamless means of human-system interaction that visually fuses data from a range of military and civilian domains and technologies to display the operating environment in multiple dimensions. The work focusses on innovative concepts to explore the art of the possible to enhance planning, SA and decision making for C2. The CCD will show how spatial and temporal displays can be combined, have a naturalistic look and feel and utilise virtual, augmented and mixed reality through an immersive interaction that is technology agnostic. This paper describes the CCD development process which includes a human centred performance assessment that captures feedback through consultation with Military stakeholders as well as scenario-based laboratory experimentation to quantify the impact of the novel interface design on C2 tasks. The CCD will integrate increased data capacity through technologies such as a single information environment whilst satisfying human cognitive capabilities and limitations. This work will advance C2 User Interface Design for systems that can be exploited in both the near and far term.

Title: Towards a framework for autonomous defensive cyber operations in a Network Operations Centre
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Network defenders continue to fend off the ever increasing and evolving cyber attacks that aim to disrupt ongoing missions. The defensive activities, which are mostly coordinated through network operations centres (NOCs), combine operator skill and many commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) tools suited for various defensive cyber operations (DCO) capabilities. A number of processes have been proposed to improve on these defensive activities. One recent approach is the functional mission analysis for cyber (FMAC) process. FMAC, which is based on system-engineering, is a three-phased process proposed by the USAF to meet their mission requirements. Although FMAC provides valuable insight into the mission-critical assets and the cyber key terrain, this promising methodology is very labour-intensive and manual, and in its current state would not be appropriate for fast-paced NOCs. To address this, we propose a conceptual framework for an autonomous FMAC process that could rapidly provide vital information for decision-making, execute DCO actions, and reduce operator burden. The proposed framework would aggregate and analyse data from various COTS tools and methodologies, providing the commander with near real-time information on the missions and the supporting information technology infrastructure (ITI). A combination of deep learning and reinforcement learning algorithms would provide rapid analysis of the data, allowing for the accurate identification of evolving threats (including zero-day) and then recommend or execute appropriate courses of action. The result would be a near real-time autonomous DCO process that could match the increasing sophistication of the adversary. The results could also be used for resource allocation and capital acquisitions.

Title: Rethinking the Perennial Problem of Generalists vs Specialists: Applications in a 5th Generation Headquarters
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract:
Operational C2 systems, as realised in military headquarters, will involve in the future a far greater diversity of knowledge domain specialists than the traditional paradigm, expanding to encompass experts in warfare and manoeuvre across non-traditional domains, emerging digital technologies, and multi-agency linkages as demanded  by  the  increasing complexity of the cooperation-competition-conflict environment. The integration of these elements will be critical to enable timely and accurate awareness and decision making to meet the challenges and threats of an increasingly complex geopolitical landscape in which military force is but one element of national power.

The  integration  of  these  specialists  is  not  without  its  challenges,  including  problems  with  cross-domain communication,  role  ambiguity  and  team  orientation.  Generalists,  individuals  with  a  broader  gambit  of knowledge but less depth than specialists, are frequently touted as the solution to multi-disciplinary teams. Generalists alone however lack the domain expertise to complete the same depth of tasks as specialists. Organisational theory provides a plethora of literature examining the generalist/ specialist composition of an organisation, which is known as the generalist vs specialist dilemma.

This paper will draw upon this body of research to build on the design of a 5th Generation Headquarters, a concept previously presented in ICCRTS. Included will be a model that enriches the binary conceptualisation of generalism vs specialism, to explore the broad spectrum of knowledge heterogeneity and depth. Changes in structure, staff training, and automation/autonomous technologies are discussed as possible parameters that may be modified to enhance organisational effectiveness and agility.

Title: Plan MURA: The pathways to transforming an Operational Headquarters
Authors: Kalloniatis, A.; Luketic, N.; Ali, I.; Cassar, G.; Ingham, I.
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: We present an overview of the vision and status of a journey to achieve significant transformation of an operational military headquarters. This is directed to the goal that Australia's Joint Operations Command (JOC) continue coordinating, with greater agility and resilience, Australia's global military activities. Plan MURA - meaning Pathways in the language of the traditional custodians of Australian Capital Territory land, the Ngunnawal people - covers all facets of modernisation of the headquarters as a socio-technical system. This is realised in four interacting Lines of Effort - around Organisation, Workforce, Information and Decision Support Technology, and Infrastructure. MURA builds on two decades of scientific engagement with the Australian Joint C2 system, including: - the Wilson Review of 2005 which defined Australia's current C2 arrangements, - numerous research studies of the functioning of the headquarters across its branches, - the scientific conceptualisation of the future headquarters, already published in this forum as the 'Fifth generation Headquarters Concept', and - the contributions to Plan MURA in its sequence of activities across all levels of headquarters staff.

Title: Human-AI Teaming for Exploring Wargame Strategies
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: While recent advances in AI make the prospect of AI-supported wargaming increasingly practical, it is likely that the most effective analysis of complex wargame models will continue to involve both humans and machines working together. We discuss interactions between human analyst, algorithm designer and machine in the context of exploring strategies for humanitarian assistance and disaster relief (HADR) scenarios. We present a simple HADR wargame, which was developed independently, and discuss the iterative process of human design and machine exploration which has led to increasingly strong solution methods to the task of evacuating refugees. We outline the process whereby human analysis informed early model simplifications and algorithm design. Machine exploration, in turn, led to further insights into how to shape parameters, action spaces and observation spaces. This process is then repeated. We present results for a range of different algorithms that were explored and refined, including deep learning methods, such as Proximal Policy Optimisation and more classical approaches, such as search and rule-based algorithms. We also present visualisations that were used to help humans understand the results of algorithmic exploration, thereby leading to further insights in algorithm choice and design.
Author Keywords: HUMAN-AI TEAMING; MILITARY WARGAMING; HUMANITARIAN ASSISTANCE AND DISASTER RELIEF (HADR) SIMULATION; DEEP REINFORCEMENT LEARNING; PROXIMAL POLICY OPTIMIZATION (PPO); HILL CLIMBING OPTIMIZATION; MONTE CARLO TREE SEARCH; AI ETHICS IN DEFENCE

Title: AI Networking Joint Concept for reliable asymmetrical teaming Agile C2 5G Operation Plan for Mobility Nodes
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: This concept paper explores strategic and tactical solutions for establishing reliable communication links in austere operational environments characterized by Denied, Intermittent, and Limited (DIL) connectivity and Anti-Access/Area Denial (A2/AD) conditions. To circumvent the complex cybersecurity risks and electronic warfare (EW) operations posed by near-peer adversaries, the author proposes the joint concept of 'Mobility Nodes.' This solution employs unmanned robotic platforms equipped with 5G Mobile Ad-hoc Networks (MANET) and AEHF communications to extend the Multi-Domain Command and Control (MDC2) grid Beyond Line-of-Sight (BLOS). The inherent mobility of these tactical nodes allows for the bypassing of physical terrain obstacles—such as Improvised Explosive Devices (IEDs) and minefields—while mitigating the risks of uplink and downlink signal interception, thereby safeguarding secure pathways for Intelligence, Surveillance, and Reconnaissance (ISR) data transmission. The implementation of this tactical architecture ensures Situational Awareness (SA) and provides an uninterrupted Common Operational Picture (COP) for dismounted teams, Joint Tactical Air Controllers (JTACs), and joint force commanders alike. The study concludes that the integration of mobile robotic platforms, directional 5G signals, and AI-supported processing drastically reduces the OODA loop (Observe, Orient, Decide, Act), enabling force survivability and the effectiveness of Joint All-Domain Command and Control (JADC2).
Author Keywords: MOBILITY NODES; COMMAND AND CONTROL (C2); C2 AGILITY; MULTI-DOMAIN COMMAND AND CONTROL (MDC2); ROBOTIC PLATFORMS; 5G/AEHF; DIL ENVIRONMENTS; JADC2.

Title: Discussion of Command and Control for Air MannedUnmanned Cooperation
Authors: Qian, N.; Li, H.; Zhu, H.
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: With the continuous improvement of autonomous capability of unmanned air systems, the cooperative operation of air mannedunmanned systems will become an important operational mode in the future. This paper studies the current status of cooperative operation of  air  manned-unmanned systems, analyzes  the  main  challenges  faced by  the command  and control of  air mannedunmanned systems in further complex battlefield environments. Further,  cooperative  issues  of air  manned-unmanned  systems such as  command  structure,  resilient  information  sharing,  collaborative  situation  awareness,  dynamic  mission  planning  are discussed.

Title: Perspectives on Command and Control: Implications for Capability Development and Assessment
Authors: Wikberg, P.; Granåsen, M.; Johansson, B. J. E.
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: This paper explores different ideas or perspectives in science and practice regarding command and control (C2). The purpose is to provide an overview of the multiperspectiveness of C2 in research and practice and to unveil assumptions and implications for assessment and development of capability related to the perspectives. Based on literature reviews and workshops, seven different C2 perspectives were identified - 1) C2 as structure and functions, 2) C2 as decision-making, 3) C2 as leadership, 4) C2 as expertise, 5) C2 as learning and change, 6) C2 as politics, power and conflict, and 7) C2 as a social construct. For each perspective, an overview of general research is presented, followed by examples from C2 research and C2 practice as well as a discussion about the implications on assessment and capability development. The conclusion is that no single perspective provides a correct and all-purpose view of C2. Although overlapping, all perspectives have their strengths and weaknesses. Choosing a perspective on C2, in research or practice, inevitably means desisting aspects not covered by the chosen perspective. Consequently, scientists and practitioners should aim to understand the underlying ideas and metaphors that form their understanding about C2. Further work on how different perspectives can be used to develop knowledge and practice of C2 is suggested.

Title: Assessing Distributed Sensemaking Performance in Command and Control: An Exploratory Study
Authors: Leggatt, A.; Baber, C.; Attfield, S.; Minocha, S.; Elliott, E.; Fields, B.; Hutton, R.; Cowell-Smith, P.; Harryman, J.
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The Future Operating Environment (FOE) is likely to be one in which operational combat units are required to be increasingly mobile and geographically dispersed with more decentralised Command and Control (C2) structures. One approach to the challenge of maintaining agility and resilience in the new context is for operational military units to be less dependent on C2 structures and to increasingly operate as semi-autonomous self-organising teams. The devolution of C2 functions, however, places a greater emphasis on the need for local and collaborative sensemaking capabilities to interpret information and assess potentially complex and unfolding situations so as to adapt and act effectively and within the constraints of a broad military intent. This paper describes a study to explore the concept of Distributed Sensemaking (DSM) in a military C2 context in order to help understand the implications for future operating concepts and to start to develop an understanding of how to assess DSM within military C2 organisations. The research team previously developed, from the academic literature, a series of nine principles of DSM (Elliott et al., 2020) presented at 25th ICCRTS. This paper describes the exploratory research due to be conducted to test two of these principles and a range of measures to assess distributed sensemaking during the study.

Title: NATO Federated Coalition Cloud with Kubernetes: A National Prototype Perspective
Authors: Andersen, H. M.; Flytør, B. F.; Onsøyen, E.; Haugan, T.; Gabrielsen, O.
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract:
Abstract -In NATO, the IST-168 research task group (RTG) 'Adaptive Information Processing and Distribution To Support Command and Control' aims to investigate cloud computing at the tactical edge from a coalition force perspective. From recent developments in cloud computing and DevSecOps stemming from civilian use, but also with increasing adoption for military applications, virtualization of infrastructure and containerization of software is currently a preferred approach to cloud computing. Containers provide a lightweight approach to packaging and orchestrating software components and services. Kubernetes is a tool for orchestrating and managing deployment, scaling and migration of such containers.

In the NATO IST-168 RTG, the emphasis is on interoperability and interconnecting different nations' Kubernetes clusters. The work described in this paper presents Norway's contribution to the RTG.

Title: Military Applications of Internet of Things: Operational Concerns Explored in Context of a Prototype Wearable
Authors: Langleite, R.; Griwodz, C.; Johnsen, F. T.
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Internet of Things (IoT), due to its inherently automated behavior and low development costs, coupled with the emergence of wireless technologies combined with small-sized hardware, has become one of the defining technologies of the last decade. IoT has therefore gained the attention of innovators of military technology, where its role could also prove to be central in gaining information dominance in the battle space. In this paper, a prototype Military IoT (MIoT) soldier wearable was built using commercially available software and hardware, supported by a private network and information-chain built solely out of free open-source software. The communication uses low-power Long Range Wide Area Network (LoRaWAN) communications independent of existing infrastructure, to showcase the ability to provide military deployments with a self-driven, ad-hoc network of sensors. This work was performed in the context of the NATO research task group IST-176 'Federated Interoperability of Military C2 and IoT Systems'. In developing the prototype, we interviewed serving military personnel in two rounds: First, to gain important insights on leadership approaches to various military missions, which aided the development of the prototype. Second, to collect feedback on the prototype to conclude whether or not such a system would help increase operational effectiveness. The findings show that increased battlespace awareness is possible through automated data acquisition using MIoT. It is therefore recommended that military organizations partaking in such scenarios further investigate the usage of MIoT approaches, specifically including wearables for automating processes that until now constitute fully or semi-manual processes.

Title: A Mixed Initiative Effects Planning Tool
Authors: Baber, C.; Morar, N.; Vance, C.; Yusuf, S. M.
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Mixed Initiative planning systems allow human decision makers to collaborate with automated decision support to develop and evaluate plans. In this paper we report an Effects Planning Tool which uses Bayesian Belief Networks to compare alternative plans that can be suggested to a Mission Planner and a simulation of the mission plan that can be used to evaluate likely issues as the plan is enacted.  The definition of the Effects Planning process is mapped to the Abstraction Hierarchy in Cognitive Work Analysis which provides a convenient approach to defining the mission, populating the Bayesian Belief Network and specifying the User Interface. This creates a shared vocabulary for the design team to discuss and evaluate the Effects Planning concept.

Title: Applying Commander's Information Requirements to Value of Information Modeling
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The multi-domain battlefield of the future will feature a staggering diversity and volume of information. Command, Control, Communication, and Information (C3I) based technologies aim to facilitate information processing and dissemination needs for Army operations, having an underlying objective of getting the right information to the right people at the right time. Towards addressing the information needs of personnel (dismounted, mounted, and those at command posts), C3I infrastructure constraints must be addressed, emerging from both constrained networks and data processing services. Addressing these constraints requires implementation of methods to effectively prioritize the management of discrete units of information (termed Information Objects) in-line with mission requirements. Towards addressing the problem of targeted Information Object management in C3I infrastructures, this work seeks to investigate the application of policy-based Value of Information (VoI) assessment in-line with records of Commander's Critical Information Requirements (CCIR). Towards addressing this objective, an information management model - termed CCIR VoI - is defined based on a review of CCIR usage in supporting command decisions. Following definition of the CCIR VoI model, discussion is provided on the applicability of CCIR-informed VoI to Information Object delivery systems for use in C3I infrastructures.

Title: A Unified Framework Combining Semantic Matchmaking and Multi-Objective Optimization Applied to Dynamic Collection and Effect Tasking Problems
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance (ISR) and joint fire operations in fast-paced military tactical environment require proper ISR and effect asset allocation to meet mission task demands. Given highly competing tasks, resources available, complexity and constraint diversity, automation and optimization are required to provide operators with suitable decision support deriving best collection/fire plans. In response to ISR collection requests for target detection, localization or tracking, collection tasking asks for the best available resources, by analyzing their capabilities and limitations, as well as factors such as timeliness, weather conditions, terrain, and adversarial risks, to determine assets representing potential collection options. Likewise, effect tasking such as weapon-target assignment triggered by call-for-fire requests similarly proceeds with multidimensional analysis according to equivalent factors. Both tasking problems heavily depend on these analyses to optimize constrained resource management. As a result, a novel Total ISR and Fire Asset Visibility (TIFAV) unified framework is proposed for ISR asset and effector management in time and resource-constrained uncertain environment. Assisting ISR/ Joint Fires enablers in the digitization of the Decision-Action Cycle, TIFAV is an automated Sense-and-Respond proof-ofconcept decision support capability, delivering optimized effector-sensor mix automation at the tactical edge, and paving the way toward a sensor-decider-shooter solution integration. It automates sensor-task /weapon-target matchmaking and optimization to derive best collection/fire plans. A matchmaking component based on a comprehensive ISR ontological model and multi-dimensional filters to derive potential collection/fire options is presented. Then, an open-loop with feedback decision model and optimization algorithms are proposed to maximize collection/effect value and recommend the best collection/fire options given multiple competing ISR requests and effect (call for fire) tasks.

Title: Improving C2 and Decision Making Using the Joint Operations Fusion Lab
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Command and Control (C2) is the accomplishment of a mission by a commander exercising authority and direction over assigned forces; and it is the most important activity within the combat theatre. Effective C2 depends on having good situational awareness. Current military capabilities allow for the collection of a large amount of various types of data (such as video, voice, geographical location,  thermal  imaging,  and  unstructured  live  reports)  for  situational  awareness  and  decision  making.  However,  processing, understanding, and fusing the data to support C2 can be painstaking for a human due to the shear amount of information. The Canadian Armed Forces (CAF) is looking to establish a Joint Operations Fusion Lab (JOpsFL) with the aim to improve and transform CAF's  C2  and  Joint  Intelligence,  Surveillance,  and  Reconnaissance  (JISR)  capabilities  by  developing  technological  solutions  for automated fusion of data and dissemination of intelligence products. This paper discusses the aim, vision, scope, and technical requirements of the CAF JOpsFL and provides an overview of the state-of-the-art information fusion techniques including Artificial Intelligence.

Title: Outlining Future C2 Doctrine Using the Cynefin Framework
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Jackson's (2013) review of the history of military doctrine shows that positivism, characterised by rationality and objectivity, has provided the epistemic basis to doctrine for 400 years. Since the beginning of the 21st century, criticisms of military doctrine based on positivism have grown. Positivist doctrine failed in Afghanistan, Iraq, and Libya, in part because of the complexity of orchestrating international 'coalitions of the willing'. When faced by chaos and complexity, particularly in the social domain, anti-positivist thinking exploiting participants' subjective experience and naturalistic decision-making was needed. A survey of C2 doctrine (Grant, 2020) shows that the evolution of doctrine over the past 25 years to cope with chaos and complexity has been weaker than might be expected. Although doctrinal manuals increasingly mention terms like tempo, uncertainty, complexity, chaos, agility, and resilience since 2012, they offer no guidance in coping with them. Only MoD(UK)'s Joint Concept Note 2/17 on the future of C2 suggests a model that could assist commanders in understanding chaos and complexity: the Cynefin framework (Snowdon, 2010). The purpose of this paper is to apply the Cynefin framework in outlining future C2 doctrine that will cope with chaos and complexity. Cynefin can contribute to identifying the nature of the problem, selecting appropriate decision processes and command structures, and dealing with pitfalls. Cynefin contexts, connection strengths, and dynamics are mapped to related concepts in the C2 literature, such as the C2 Endeavour and Approach spaces and command structure. Existing doctrinal publications are surveyed to identify the topics commonly found. These topics are then used to form a canonical Table of Contents for C2 doctrine. The contributions that Cynefin can make to a canonical C2 doctrine publication are identified, and these contributions are further worked out. Finally, conclusions are drawn and recommendations are made for further work.
Author Keywords: CYNEFIN FRAMEWORK; COMMAND AND CONTROL (C2) DOCTRINE; COMPLEXITY AND CHAOS THEORY; DECISION-MAKING PROCESSES; OODA LOOP ADAPTATION; HIERARCHICAL AND EDGE ORGANIZATION; EMERGENCY MANAGEMENT AND MILITARY OPERATIONS

Title: Towards a multi-level theory of agility
Authors: Bjurström, E.; Ivari, J.; Tarnawska, A.
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: A multilevel theory of agility for C2 is absent. This paper uses an organizing paradigm to reinterpret the existing body of research in order to explore the multilevel self-organizing dynamics that underpin the emergence of organizational agility. Following social theory's practice-turn, we outline in section 2 the essence of the organizing paradigm and map some of its implications. We reflect on the routines, creativity and complexity inherent in human organizing, and how organizing practices manifest new collective C2 capabilities for unanticipated, unexpected and unfamiliar circumstances. Then we consider the role and limits of individual agency and rationality within the process of organizing, placing the emphasis on a collective process of structuring that imposes change and order in structure. In section 3 we present an illustration of some organizing principles and practices that may feature within a multi-level theory of agility. We explore how collective sensemaking is intertwined with collective organizing, and briefly reflect on a conceptual model of agility in the organizing paradigm. In the concluding section 4 we discuss the usefulness of an organizing paradigm for C2 agility research and invite collaboration in researching cross-level organizing processes and practices.

Title: Command and Control Across Air, Space and Cyber Domains at Speed and Scale for Dynamic Targets
Authors: Guo, J. K.; Champagne, V.; Lautenschlager, J.; Webb, E.; Williams, P.; Van Brackle, D.; Ritz, B.
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Because Dynamic Targets (DT) are typically fleeting and have significant strategic value to an overall military campaign, the warfighter must identify available all-domain (air, space, cyber) assets with the right capabilities and perform cost/benefit assessment in time to achieve desired effects in severely compressed decision cycles.  While all-domain assets offer more flexibility and redundancy to military operations, they create more challenges to a real-time resource allocation problem:  more constraints, relationships, and dependencies than conventional air operations alone.  To address these challenges, we present a dynamic planning/re-planning system that allocates and schedules dynamic targets in real-time, optimizing and balancing the tradeoffs of multiple objectives at speed and scale. Our algorithm provides near-optimal solutions for dynamic target resourcing while minimizing ripple effects (e.g., changes to existing tasks in execution) and maximizing the diversity of Integrated Tasking Order (ITO) change options across all domains. We define an Integrated Tasking Order as similar to an air tasking order but including integrated air, space, and cyber domains. Using a realistic planning scenario developed and validated by Subject Matter Experts, our results show diverse Integrated Tasking Order change options that utilize all-domain assets, automatically tasking air, space, and cyber capabilities against dynamic targets.  Our system provides quantitative analysis over speed, allocation percentage, collateral damage, attribution, desired effects, and changes to the executing Integrated Tasking Order and then presents the Integrated Tasking Order change options with these metrics to the warfighters for cost/benefit analysis and comparison.

Title: Level 11 Automation 1
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: As technology advances into the Cognitive Era, military Command and Control (C2) systems must evolve to balance efficiency with the ability to respond to unexpected shock events. While human operators and current automated systems both possess critical failure modes—demonstrated by incidents like the Air France Flight 447 crash and the Ariane 5 missile launch failure—the future of C2 relies on an increasingly symbiotic human-machine relationship. To address the limitations of human perception, such as spatial disorientation, and the brittleness of tightly coupled systems, this paper explores the concept of \Level 11 automation
DOI: \" defined as a state where the computer overrides the human operator. Achieving this safely requires moving beyond current pattern-matching Machine Learning (ML) techniques toward systems capable of cause and effect modeling. Integrating Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AI/ML) with a high degree of domain cognition will mitigate both human and machine vulnerabilities
Cited by: creating a partnership where the entity that best understands reality is empowered to make the best decision."

Title: Challenges in understanding AI
Authors: Staffas, K.; Bjurström, E.; Roxström, G.
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is a concept that triggers many pictures and visions in all kinds of fields of application. There are many ways of explaining what AI is and what can be expected of this technology in the coming decades. Our examination of the challenges inherent in notions of AI in the context of C2 refer back to its thought applications, thus assuming that meaning, purpose, function and usefulness are interrelated concepts. Further assuming that AI in its basic nature is a mathematical phenomenon within the realm of what Simon called 'the sciences of the artificial', now taking on a certain degree of autonomy and self-designing logic, we address the question of what the challenges of understanding AI may be in the context of C2. The answer to the question has implications not only for the use of AI in decision support, but also for the education of a profession which traditionally has been defined by its decision-making functions and abilities. - What do future officers need to understand about AI in C2 to remain, or emerge as even more potent, in their art of making decisions for decisive advantage? - Which are the crucial challenges to the education of AI-savvy decision-makers? Which are the greatest challenges to the actual realization of the full potential of AI-supported C2? In this way, this paper extends e.g. Gigerenzer's call for the education of risk-savvy decision-makers.

Title: Kick the Cann: Modeling Effect of Cannibalization Maintenance Actions on Naval FA-18s
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Naval aircraft cannibalization actions, where one aircraft consumes the part of another aircraft in maintenance, is a common yet under evaluated maintenance action. Decisions to cannibalize an aircraft are based on domain knowledge and need. While an improvement in performance is expected in the aircraft receiving a part, there is no method to quantify the increase, nor predict if future cannibalizations will have the same improvement. This presents an opportunity to implement a novel metric to quantify aircraft performance and train a machine learning model to predict the 'return' of cannibalizing a part for a pairwise combination of FA-18 E/F Super Hornets. Here, we propose a novel return-on-investment (ROI) metric, a composite of flight data, financial records, and work orders, and its utility as a surrogate for aircraft performance and to evaluate the outcome of cannibalization actions. In addition, we train an associated gradient boosting model that predicts the change in ROI. Finally, we perform an analysis of feature importance to identify features that most affect outcome. This model is a command and control decision-making tool that can assist in utilizing resources more effectively in support of mission capability. Furthermore, the analysis of feature importance can help maintenance administrators understand the factors that affect aircraft performance and provide guidance in making more informed cannibalization action decisions.

Title: IMPROVING SITUATION AWARENESS BY RECOGNIZING OBJECTS OF INTEREST
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: To enable today's armed forces to operate in a complex and dynamic environment, increasing Situation Awareness  (SA)  for  both  personnel  in  the  field  and  for  those  higher  up  in  the  chain of command  is important. SA is defined as the knowledge of the elements of the operating environment necessary to make informed decisions. In urban operations, an important element to consider when working on increasing SA is the complexity of the operating environment, and in particular the physical environment which includes the natural terrain and man-made structures. This document focusses on the understanding of 3D scenes by detecting and classifying objects of interest for decision aids. These 3D scenes represent vast urban areas and are mainly lidar data. Aerial and terrestrial lidar data are investigated. A brief literature review is done, followed by the presentation of three use-cases and by the discussion of the detection and classification results. The document concludes with closing remarks.

Title: Relating the Cultural Iceberg to Organizations and Information Warfare
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The Iceberg as initially proposed by Schein is often used when considering organizational culture and to try to understand what leads to visible behaviors and actions through surfacing the assumptions, values and beliefs that led to the selection of  strategies,  goals  etc.  When  considering  C2  in  a  contested  environment,  decision-making  should  never  take  place without fully understanding the context within which the decisions are being made. From all that is observable, we select those aspects that interest us and interpret them in our personal context and give them meaning accordingly. We will draw conclusions having applied our existing assumptions, frequently without acknowledging them, and develop beliefs based on these conclusions. Finally, we take actions that seem right in our context. The actions we may take will be from our personal repertoire according to the means available to us, the strength of our drive (motivation), and any constraints or restraints imposed on us. However, we need to see the 'other' by standing in their shoes and seek to understand their beliefs,  values,  motivations,  and  drives.  We  need  to  understand  the  impact  of  their  organizational  structures  and technology on their courses of action. However, we also need to recognize they are doing the same to us, so we also need to  look  within  to  assess  as  best  as  possible  those  actions  being  taken  against  us  to  influence  our  beliefs,  values, motivations,  structures,  and  technological  enablers.  Finally,  we  explore  what  the  impact  on  organization  and  C2 approaches and the challenges that need to be addressed. This is the critical understanding we need to achieve success.

Title: Application of Blockchain for Improving Future Canadian Armed Forces Supply Chain Efficiency
Authors: Qasrawi, Y.; Boukhtouta, A.; Soeanu, A.
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The widespread availability and cost effectiveness of information technologies and digital services have reshaped supply chains and logistics, enabling hybrid forms of cooperation and competition. This increased fragmentation and complexity of supply networks presents an opportunity for blockchain adoption. Blockchain enables logistics operations to be more efficient and transparent by securing the information exchange between individuals, companies, and units of an institution. It has the potential to be used within a military context during coalition operations, such as disaster relief or peace keeping, by connecting forces and reducing inter-party mistrust, while maintaining privacy and data confidentiality and enabling Command and Control. The contribution of this paper is threefold;  it  first  answers  the  question  'how  can  blockchain  be  used  within  logistics to  improve  command  and control  and  the decision  making  process.  Second,  it  presents  a  use  case  of  the  implementation  of  blockchain  for  military  coalition  operations involving information sharing to coordinate logistics activities and connect battlespaces and forces. Finally, it presents the challenges and the future potential of the technology for logistics applications.

Title: Understanding And Predicting The Future Cost Of Readiness For An Aging Naval F/A-18 Fleet
Authors: Michlin, B.; Rorie, J.; Wied, J.; Williams, G.
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract:
Recent budget constraints require the Naval Aviation Enterprise to maximize readiness while minimizing cost. We propose a data-driven approach to help decision makers identify which aircraft are the most expensive to keep in a mission capable (MC) state and what factors affect this cost.

An AI/ML model is developed as an extension to the Digital Aviation Readiness Technology Engine (DARTE) family of models to determine the factors that lead to a high average cost per day of MC for an aircraft in the Naval F/A-18 fleet. This model then predicts if the average cost per day of MC will be above a fixed cost threshold in the future.

Various model architectures are explored, including tree-based ensemble methods and long short-term memory (LSTM) networks. These models are trained on a combination of traditional readiness datasets (manning, training, equipping) and financial datasets.

The resulting models are then examined with explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) techniques to obtain a rigorous and comprehensive feature importance which may be used to determine past and predict future aircraft status.
