Title: The UK's Intelligent Ship Project
Authors: Cox, J.; Webster, K.; Tate, A.; Cowell-Butler, J.; Pottrill, E.
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract:
The complexity and quantity of data available to be utilised in the battlespace is ever growing, causing strain and potential overload on the traditional systems & decision-making processes used in complex systems and organisations. As capability, diversity of threats and utilisation of technology grows; decisions will need to be faster and exploit this increasing volume of data. This will mean greater automation, utilisation of technologies such as artificial intelligence, understanding how humans will interact with the technology as part of a team and changes to platform design to support the information and data flows required.

Dstl has initiated the Intelligent Ship project  with a goal to investigate human-autonomy teaming on an intelligent platform by considering  team  structures,  human  machine  interfaces,  intelligent  agents,  agent-to-agent  communication,  interactions  and necessary enablers. The project aims to investigate, how:

- to manage, integrate and organise teams of multiple humans and intelligent agents
- to manage workflows & workload, measure performance and manage 'conflict' between agents and humans
- can AI and humans work collaboratively utilising respective strengths
- to  distribute  Command  and  Control  more  flexibly  than  current  processes  to  allow  concepts  such  as  adaptive automation

The first phase focused on the integration environment, the enablers to support integration and testing with intelligent agents. The project is currently in its second phase with integration & agent development continuing with evaluation plans and metrics. A series of evaluations are planned and the phase will culminate in a demonstration in spring 2022. Whilst the use case of this project is a maritime platform, the lessons learnt and architectures developed will be applicable to any system where humans and intelligent agents are working together and will be relevant across a force or distributed platform.

This paper will cover the aims and objectives of the project, the phases of work and the demonstrations planned.
Author Keywords: HUMAN-AI TEAMING; AUTONOMOUS MARITIME SYSTEMS; COMMAND AND CONTROL ARCHITECTURE; INTELLIGENT SHIP AI NETWORK (ISAIN); ADAPTIVE AUTOMATION; MULTI-AGENT DECISION SUPPORT; HUMAN-MACHINE INTERFACE EVALUATION; DISTRIBUTED REINFORCEMENT LEARNING; SITUATIONAL AWARENESS METRICS

Title: Topic 4: C2 and human-AI/autonomy teaming Human Machine Interactions as Partnership
Authors: Gallup, S. P.; Nissen, M. E.
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Although the technologic capabilities of autonomous systems continue to accelerate, and systems in some domains have reached the technical point of total autonomy, the focus on human-machine teaming is gaining increased attention. The concept human-machine team remains simplistic and incomplete, however. The next step in human-machine evolution-not design-involves partnership , which differs from teaming in several important ways, and which can enable human-machine capabilities that can advance in a quantum way beyond our current design trajectory. In this article we discuss the differences between teaming and partnership, along with emergent implications, and we describe the essential role of dynamic C2 knowledge to enable and support human-machine partnership. We then build upon and integrate such discussion to advocate new thinking about how people and machines can interact as future partners. We conclude in turn with suggestions for further research and advanced thinking on this topic.

Title: Agile, Antifragile, Artificial-Intelligence-Enabled Command and Control
Authors: Simpson, J.; El Sawah, S.; Oosthuizen, R.; Abbass, H.
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is rapidly becoming integrated into military Command and Control (C2) systems as a strategic priority for many defence forces. The successful implementation of AI is promising to herald a significant leap in C2 agility through automation. However, realistic expectations need to be set on what AI can achieve in the foreseeable future. This paper will argue that AI could lead to a fragility trap, whereby the delegation of C2 functions to an AI could increase the fragility of C2, resulting in catastrophic strategic failures. This calls for a new framework for AI in C2 to avoid this trap. We will argue that 'antifragility' along with agility should form the core design principles for AI-enabled C2 systems. This duality is termed Agile, Antifragile, AI-Enabled Command and Control (A3IC2). An A3IC2 system continuously improves its capacity to perform in the face of shocks and surprises through overcompensation from feedback during the C2 decision-making cycle. An A3IC2 system will not only be able to survive within a complex operational environment, it will also thrive, benefiting from the inevitable shocks and volatility of war.

Title: Soft Actor-Critic Solution to a Security Game With Deception and an Informant
Authors: Guo, J. K.; Satya, S.; Hofmann, M. O.; Veldhuizen, C.; Champagne, V.
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Peer competition requires fast Command and Control (C2) decisions that anticipate intelligent, potentially deceptive opponent actions. Deception provides a method for enticing an opponent into taking suboptimal actions. We describe an application of game theory to course of action (COA) development in a competition among two opponents, an attacked and a defender, in the presence of a third party. Proximity to the third party provides cover for the attacker, reducing the likelihood of being detected by the defender, but the third party may also work as an informant, negating this advantage. We model the competition as an incomplete information, extensive form, security game with continuous actions. We approximate the action policies of the two opponent agents in equilibrium using a meta strategy-guided Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) algorithm which yields robust, explainable decision support and reduces the risk of surprise due to unexpected adversary COAs. We performed pairwise experiments with agents trained with varying degrees of awareness of the possibility of deceptive hiding, i.e., blending in with the third party, and informing. Our results show how the defender optimizes its win rate by taking these deceptive actions into account in its COAs. In our pilot project we restricted the action policies to movement in two dimensions in an area populated with five targets of varying values, but our approach generalizes to additional dimensions.
Author Keywords: SECURITY GAMES; DECEPTIVE HIDING; MULTI-AGENT DEEP REINFORCEMENT LEARNING; SOFT ACTOR-CRITIC; META-STRATEGY GUIDED LEARNING; CONTINUOUS-ACTION GAME THEORY; INFORMANT MODELING; COMMAND AND CONTROL DECISION SUPPORT

Title: A Knowledge-Based Approach to Defining Decider Types for Decider Collaboration Implementing Agile Control
Authors: O'Neill, J.; Newbold, R.; McDonald, J.; Milas, T.; Brown, P.
Source title: 26th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The structured logic methodology for generating options for how to deciders may collaborate across Defence Elements is presented in this paper. The Australian Future C2 concept 'Hierarchical Command, Agile Control' recognises that there are multiple deciders collaboratively adapting to a contested and congested environment to ensure the commander's intent is achieved. The Sense-DecideEffect Concept is analysed for the key interactions required across Defence Elements and the three types of deciders required for decider collaboration. A design space is articulated that generates 25 options (modes) for how deciders may collaborate across Defence Elements. Advice is provided for how force designers may select appropriate decider collaboration modes to enable joint force integration.
Author Keywords: AGILE CONTROL; DECIDER COLLABORATION; SENSE-DECIDE-EFFECT; EMANATION CONTROL; SOFT-KILL AND HARD-KILL DECIDERS; JOINT TASK FORCE (JTF) OPERATIONS; KNOWLEDGE-AUGMENTED INFORMATION EXCHANGE; PLAYBOOKS AS CODE

Title: Formal intelligence produced by mathematical deductive reasoning as a complement to knowledge produced by inductive inference from patterns in data (machine learning)
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: At its heart, machine learning (ML) is a sophisticated pattern-recognition system trained by an algorithm. Advances in ML offer promising ways to help analysts in command and control find new insights (patterns from data) necessary for decision making. Applying ML methods to sensing and processing could have a significant impact on intelligence analysis, especially on intelligence functions that develop knowledge, enable understanding, and produce predictive assessments. However, developing knowledge and enabling understanding can also be obtained using deductive reasoning, which is a complementary approach to ML. Formal mathematical (deductive) reasoning-namely, logical rules for automated deductions similar to ones currently used for proving mathematical theorems-can augment or replace common-sense reasoning used to analyze data lakes. Formal deductive reasoning, a process that does not add uncertainty, can generate new knowledge, which we call formal intelligence. This type of intelligence is free of the cognitive biases introduced by human operators in the analysis process and can be used directly for decision making or indirectly as an input for ML methods. The goal of this concept paper is to motivate the introduction of the formal intelligence and to present a few examples.

Title: Security First Approach for Developing Machine Learning Applications in Multi-Domain Operations
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Effective Command and Control (C2) for multi-domain operations require good situational awareness (SA) across all domains. Good SA is achieved through the collection of large volumes of data that are of different varieties (such as video, voice, geographical data, thermal imaging, and unstructured live reports) and the production of reliable intelligence products through data analysis. Due to the volume and variety of data, the analysis need to be semi-automated. Machine Learning (ML) has been employed to automate the data processing, analysis, and fusion to enable effective C2. However, ML is vulnerable to manipulation leading to untrustworthy results and unreliable intelligence which can influence the decision making process in favour of the opposing forces. A ML based system can be attacked (known as adversarial machine learning) at any stage during its development or operation, such as poisoning the training data (as an example, introducing false samples as positive samples) or model extraction (as an example, learning a model's hyperparameters). This paper provides an overview and discusses the types of attacks that an opposing force can mount on a ML based system, including white box and black box attacks, and proposes Machine Learning Security Operations (MLSecOps), a security  first  approach  for  developing  ML  based  systems,  as  means  to  mitigating  attacks  and  improving  the  resilience  of  these  systems.
Author Keywords: MULTI-DOMAIN OPERATIONS; COMMAND AND CONTROL (C2); ADVERSARIAL MACHINE LEARNING; MACHINE LEARNING OPERATIONS (MLOPS); MACHINE LEARNING SECURITY OPERATIONS (MLSECOPS); DATA POISONING ATTACKS; EVASION AND MODEL EXTRACTION ATTACKS; RESPONSIBLE AI GOVERNANCE

Title: An integrated framework and taxonomy for cyber damage assessment
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The ever increasing and evolving cyber attacks continue to threaten cyberspace, which is a critical enabler to the conduct of current and future military missions. It is therefore necessary to understand and assess the damage due to cyber breaches on organisational assets. Such a Cyber Damage Assessment (CDA) would develop a disciplined, measurable approach to assess the costs of cyber incidents and the organisation's remediation performance. This allows an organisation to benchmark its defensive cyber operations (DCO) performance and return on investment (ROI), providing decision makers with regular input on the organisation's value proposition. This work proposes a new CDA framework, which could enable DCO stakeholders to obtain regular situational awareness (SA) of the damage to their operations in cyberspace following cyber breaches. We propose an integration framework that is based on a new taxonomy of metrics that we derived from the characterisation of key performance indicators (KPIs) of cyber damage. Such a framework, which could have modules added or modified as new information and methodologies become available, would ingest internal and external data that is analysed through a variety of algorithms that determine KPI metrics and measures for CDA. The results of our work address the lack of a taxonomy covering the complete spectrum of cyber damages, and provide a framework proposal for periodic SA updates to stakeholders following cyber breaches. Preliminary simulations of the framework provides promising results, showing representative damage estimates to decision makers.

Title: Assessing Distributed Sensemaking Performance in Command and Control: Results from an Exploratory Study
Authors: Leggatt, A.; Baber, C.; Attfield, S.; Minocha, S.; Elliott, E.; Fields, B.; Hutton, R.; Cowell-Smith, P.; McNeillie, S.
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract:
The Future Operating Environment (FOE) is likely to be one in which operational combat units are required to be increasingly mobile and geographically dispersed with more decentralised Command and Control (C2) structures. One approach to the challenge of maintaining agility and resilience in the new context is for operational military units to be less dependent on C2 structures and to increasingly operate as semi-autonomous self-organising teams. The  devolution  of  C2  functions,  however,  places  a  greater  emphasis  on  the  need  for  local  and  collaborative sensemaking capabilities to interpret information and assess potentially complex and unfolding situations so as to adapt and act effectively and within the constraints of a broad military intent.

This paper describes a study which explored the concept of Distributed Sensemaking (DSM) in a military C2 context in order to help understand the implications for future operating concepts and to start to develop an understanding of how to assess DSM within military C2 organisations. The research team previously developed, from the academic literature, a series of nine principles of DSM (Elliott et al., 2020) presented at the 25th ICCRTS. This paper describes the exploratory research conducted to investigate these principles and a range of measures to assess distributed sensemaking. Findings from the exploratory research including analysis of participant-interactions, questionnaires and interviews are presented in detail.

Title: Connecting the Battlespace: C2 and IoT Technical Interoperability in Tactical Federated Environments
Authors: Manso, M.; Furtak, J.; Guerra, B.; Johnsen, F. T.; Michaelis, J.; Ota, D.; Suri, N.; Wrona, K.
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: With the emergence of available wireless technologies in combination with small-sized hardware, the Internet of Things (IoT) has become one of the defining technology trends of the last decade. It has additionally gained the attention of military technology innovators as a means to gain information dominance in the battlespace through enhanced situational awareness. Conducted as part of the NATO research task group IST-176 on “Federated Interoperability of Military C2 and IoT Systems”, this research investigates a secure approach to connect heterogeneous assets that rely on widely used and standardized technologies. To demonstrate the approach, a set of planned experiments are presented in which systems from different nations are connected in a federated environment. The results of the experiments aim to demonstrate the feasibility of integrating battlefield assets, including soldier systems and IoT devices, in supporting collective C2.

Title: Readiness Predictions for the MH-60 Seahawk with the Digital Aviation Readiness Technology Engine
Authors: Rorie, J.; Michlin, B.; Duclos, J.; Lee, D.; Williams, G.
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The Digital Aviation Readiness Technology Engine (DARTE) paradigm, proven highly successful with fixed-wing aircraft such as the F/A-18 Super Hornet and E-2 Hawkeye, has had the MC model expanded to rotary wing aircraft for the first time with predictions for the MH-60 Seahawk. Readiness datasets for the MH-60 are explored and AI/ML models are created to predict future Mission Capable (MC) aircraft in squadrons. New challenges, including different squadron structures and deployment profiles, are addressed. Additionally, new insights are accessible with the addition of new readiness datasets and interrogation of model predictions with eXplainable AI (XAI).

Title: Actualizing the Emergent in Military Decision-Making: Toward an Operationally Oriented Understanding
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The aim of this paper is to provide a conceptual framework that helps explicate a productive relation between the construction of an operational problem (knowledge) and the possibilities, on an operational level (organization), to conduct operational art (decision-making). Our discussion evolves by way of three successive parts. First, we allege that an operational problem is not in fact an object, but a concept, and we invoke Michel Foucault’s notion of “problematization” to explain what this means and why it matters. Next, we draw on insights from phenomenology to remediate the epistemological interstice between “empiricist” (Situation Awareness) and “intellectualist” (“planning”) approaches to knowledge by shifting the methodological focus from representation to emergence. Finally, we introduce the concept “operationally oriented understanding” to bring these insights together within a single framework in an attempt to facilitate a more productive mode of thinking in relation to military decision-making.

Title: Procedural knowledge loss due to military posting cycles: The need for better understanding its impact on organizational performance
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The main goal of this paper is to draw attention to and promote a discussion around management of implicit procedural knowledge (undocumented knowledge of how things are done) in military organizations, including headquarters (HQ). HQs are the backbone of the command and control (C2) enterprise, and their smooth operation is essential to efficient C2. Regular personnel rotations resulting from posting cycles have a somewhat conflicting effect on organizational knowledge capital—a seeming growth of the collective knowledge capital at the global Departmental level is coupled with knowledge loss at the local unit-level, which may have a negative impact on organizational performance, and may affect the HQ’s ability to support C2. Due to the often-insufficient knowledge management (KM) at the local unit-level, organizations reportedly find themselves in a perpetual cycle of re-discovering how to operate, and some may even have to re-establish clarity around their mandates and expectations with their external partners. An apparent solution to this problem may lie in improving KM practices at the local level; however, any such initiative requires expenditure of already limited resources. Some authors noted a general reluctance of HQs to invest resources into procedural KM, which may be due to different motivations, including a lack of unequivocal evidence of the problem. This paper highlights the need for a better understanding of the nature and extent of the procedural knowledge loss problem in military organizations and proposes an empirical approach to establishing the impact of knowledge loss resulting from personnel rotations on organizational performance.

Title: A Data Collection Approach for Evaluating National Security Collective Training Events
Authors: Jarmasz, J.; Thomson, M.; Fugelsang, J.; Risko, E.
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Producing coordinated plans  and expedited responses across governmental agencies in the face of national security (NS) crisis events presents considerable challenges. A unique opportunity to practice responding to NS crises is afforded by a program of Collective Training Events (CTEs) organized by the Canadian Special Operations Forces Command (CANSOFCOM). The challenges with evaluating the success and effectiveness of such events are well-documented. In 2020, we collaborated with the CANSOFCOM Lessons Learned (LL) cell to develop a set of measures to evaluate the most recent CTE, Global Archer 2020 (GA20). These were based on the literature on Command and Control, naturalistic decision making, and inter-agency collaboration and communication. We adopted a multipronged  approach,  including  informal  observations,  an  adapted  social  network  analysis  methodology,  interviews  with  select participants,  and  a postexercise  survey on  participants' perceptions  of the exercise. Due to the exploratory nature of the data collection, limited inferences can be drawn. Nevertheless, we were able to provide some analytical rigour to support the general observation by the CANSOFCOM LL team that, despite certain challenges, GA20 provided a valuable opportunity for Canadian Armed Forces and governmental partners alike to foster effective collaboration and coordination in a NS crisis setting. This initial effort showed that it is feasible to collect systematic exercise performance and perception data at complex 'whole of government' fi eld exercises. Given CANSOFC OM's plans to conduct further CTEs in concert with its Canadian and Allied NS partners, next steps in developing and refining data collection for future events are discussed.

Title: Information Theoretic Measures for Command and Control: Influenceability, Understanding and Intentionality
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Command and control (C2) can been decomposed in terms of capability, understanding and intent. Without all three elements, one cannot exercise C2. As the future of warfare becomes increasingly mediated via information systems, it shall become increasingly necessary for machine-speed C2, i.e. for autonomous agents to act on behalf of the human command element in order to digest and act upon the high volume, velocity and variety of information that flows within and between these information systems. These agents will require the ability to reason about cause and effect and to quantify their ability to influence both own and adversarial systems. Information theory provides a mathematical framework for quantifying uncertainty and correlations between random variables. The application of information theory to control or even C2 is not novel. However, past applications have focussed on control purely in terms of ability to influence without recognising the importance of understanding and intentionality. This paper considers control in terms of the prototypical anatomy of a decision, and presents information theoretic measures for quantifying capability, understanding and control. Capability shall be recast as the ability to exercise influence and a measure of influence will be described in terms of mutual information. Understanding shall be recast as an agent's ability to describe system dynamics in probabilistic terms and the measure of misunderstanding shall be expressed in terms of Kullback-Leibler divergence. Finally, control shall be characterised in terms of a novel information theoretic construct, referred to as loss of control, which measures an agent's inability to achieve it's intended outcome. Each measure shall be demonstrated with a motivating example. These measures constitute a computable theory of (directed, informed) influence, which can be used to support decisions about investment of resources and the ranking of systems by influenceability and controllability.

Title: Intelligent Situational Awareness - Experimental Framework for Command and Control Technology
Authors: Bau, N.; Cawalla, J.; Gökgöz, F.; Panda, M.; Schmitz, H.-C.; Stucken, S.
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The military has to function effectively in diverse mission types (peace enforcement, peace keeping, war, etc.) and it has to operate in various dimensions, including land, sea, air, space and cyber. The military must both be able to apply new (weapon) technologies and react to them. To this end, it has to adapt and further develop its tactics and processes. Complexity and constant development of military affairs are also challenges for command and control (C2). C2 has to be efficient and effective under heterogeneous and often adverse conditions. Therefore, C2 needs technological support. Respective research and development (R&D) is a permanent endeavor.  Developers  and  military  subject-matter  experts  (SME)  must  cooperate  closely  in  order  to  constantly  elicit  new requirements and ensure that solutions meet these requirements. For enabling an iterative, human-centered design process, an experimental framework is needed that enables the quick development and evaluation of demonstrators. The framework must provide the core functionalities of a C2 Information System (C2IS), including its basic 'look and feel'. It must allow for the integration of new functionalities easily and quickly. The framework should be independent of a specific C2IS on the market, as its role is not to favor a certain product, but rather to enable the bridging of research and military product development as well as deployment. An experimental C2IS framework is at the core of a C2 technologies lab. In this paper, we will introduce the Intelligent Situational Awareness (INSANE) framework as an experimental environment for C2IS R&D.

Title: Augmented and Virtual Reality (AR/VR) and Artificial Intelligence (AI) Technology in Systematic Inter-Professional Crisis Management Training
Authors: Bakken, B. T.; Lund-Kordahl, I.; Sandberg, I.
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The technological transformation has enabled innovative changes in operational processes and services. Immersive technologies, specifically Augmented Reality (AR) and Virtual Reality (VR) training has been used for many years with excellent results to train e.g. astronauts, pilots, paramedics, and military personnel. Emerging technologies, such as Artificial Intelligence (AI), present even greater opportunities when it comes to making our crisis management systems more intelligent, secure, and effective. At present, the crisis management field is dominated by conventional exercise methods, for example when participants gather to discuss or simulate a full-scale scenario (such as table-top exercises). Consequently, exercises that present participants with realistic challenges under time pressure are rare, if they even exist. Other problems identified in earlier research are, for example: inadequate assessment of the effectiveness of training; difficulties in defining a suitable training context or content; providing timely and relevant feedback to participants; and transfer of 'lessons learnt' to future incidents. With this study we aim to design an experimental testbed for the purpose of conducting systematic comparisons of various configurations of simulation exercises, using novel tools and procedures (such as AI, VR, AR). The baseline for comparison will be traditional time- and space-dependent (i.e., field) exercises. Measured variables fall into the categories of situational awareness (SA), decision-making, communication, inter- and intra-team dynamics, and leadership. The main dependent variable will be learning outcomes. Our main hypothesis is that using novel tools and technologies to support systematic crisis training and exercises on crisis management will have a positive effect on intermediary processes as well as learning outcomes.

Title: Characterizing Novelty in the Military Domain
Authors: Chadwick, T.; Chao, J.; Izumigawa, C.; Galdorisi, G.; Ortiz-Peña, H.; Loup, E.; Soultanian, N.; Manzanares, M.; Mai, A.; Yen, R.; Lange, D. S.
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: A critical factor in utilizing agents with Artificial Intelligence (AI) is their robustness to novelty. AI agents include models that are either engineered or trained. Engineered models include knowledge of those aspects of the environment that are known and considered important by the engineers. Learned models form embeddings of aspects of the environment based on connections made through the training data. In operation, however, a rich environment is likely to present challenges not seen in training sets or accounted for in engineered models. Worse still, adversarial environments are subject to change by opponents. A program at the Defense Advanced Research Project Agency (DARPA) seeks to develop the science necessary to develop and evaluate agents that are robust to novelty. This capability will be required, before AI has the role envisioned within mission critical environments. As part of the Science of AI and Learning for Open-world Novelty (SAIL-ON), we are mapping possible military domain novelty types to a domain-independent ontology developed as part of a theory of novelty. Characterizing the possible space of novelty mathematically and ontologically will allow us to experiment with agent designs that are coming from the DARPA SAIL-ON program in relevant military environments. Utilizing the same techniques as being used in laboratory experiments, we will be able to measure agent ability to detect, characterize, and accommodate novelty.

Title: A Human Factors Perspective on Information Fusion, Data Analytics and Decision Support for Command-and-Control Modernization Concepts
Authors: Fang, S.; Ghajar-Khosravi, S.; Ho, G.; Zotov, V.; Collins, R.; Hou, M.
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: In support of the modernization of the North American Aerospace Defense Command (NORAD), several new initiatives have been proposed for future NORAD multi-domain Command and Control (C2) operations.  The research works at Defence Research and Development (DRDC) Toronto Research Centre (TRC) aims to identify key Human Factors (HF) challenges and provide advice and recommendations for the development and improvement of future NORAD C2 systems and technologies. The work will focus on investigating the Human System Integration (HSI) issues related to Artificial Intelligence (AI)-enabled information management, decision support, and training systems to support NORAD’s C2 teams through track validation, track identification, threat assessment, mitigation solution, and weapon engagement activities. With the advancements of NORAD C2 systems in coming years, the resulting fast-paced complexity of employed AI technologies may surpass operators’ cognitive abilities in C2 decision-making. Therefore, these C2 systems must be designed and evaluated using HF measures, methodologies, and best practices. Over the next four years, in collaboration with DRDC Valcartier Research Centre, DRDC TRC will provide recommendations and strategies to facilitate improving C2 operational performance and effectiveness through enhanced situation awareness (SA), improved decision-making, reduced operator workload, and increased trust in AI. Following the related literature reviews and interviews with subject matter experts in the areas of HAT and C2 operations, a summary of key findings and recommendations are provided in this paper.

Title: Shaping Partner Battle Rhythm Design through Visualisation
Authors: McLennan-Smith, T.; La, P.; Kalloniatis, A.
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Headquarters at high tempo use a formalised approach known as battle rhythm to plan and execute military operations based on typically dense meeting schedules. Doctrinally, battle rhythm design is commonly built around the established staffing, timing, and reporting  requirements  of  Boards,  Bureaus,  Centers,  Cells  and  Working  Groups  (B2C2WGs).  The  design  of  such  a  structure  is particularly challenging with partners, either peer (in a coalition) and higher or lower headquarters. During heightened operational tempo where personnel may face back-to-back meetings, designing a battle rhythm framework around a partner's schedule needs to avoid cramming and allow for the actual conduct of work, whilst remaining agile to meet dynamic operational requirements. Application of the meeting schedule problem has been applied in the Command and Control (C2) context and further extended to incorporate time-dependent fatigue factors. In this work we explore a hypothetical exercise between two partners in which one partner seeks to design a battle rhythm framework around the established battle rhythm of the other partner. The dataset of B2C2WGs created for this hypothetical exercise consists of an artificial collection of partner B2C2WGs run over the course of a 5 day period with their own staffing, timing, and reporting requirements. Given the partner B2C2WGs, we consider an initial schedule of internal B2C2WGs. We then explore the research questions: 'Where are the issues with the current battle rhythm that result in crammed meetings?' and 'How can an internal battle rhythm be designed around an existing partner schedule?' Through a series of visualisations that highlight staffing, timing, and other requirements across internal and partner B2C2WGs, initial design issues are identified. Using insights obtained from these visualisations, we establish constraints upon the design that can be directly fed into a scheduling procedure. This new procedure is then used to generate new battle rhythm designs, reflecting constraints from the partner's schedule. Finally we compare the output of our optimisation to the initial scheduling and establish what improvements have been made for the headquarters to work with its partner's schedule.

Title: Augmented C2 Assessment Using Digital Data
Authors: Alston, N.; Kalloniatis, A.; Bowles, C.
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The adoption of digital systems in Command and Control (C2) organisations presents many opportunities to automate and streamline the assessment of C2. In addition, while digital C2 systems continue to be rapidly deployed and evolved, there is an ongoing challenge in assessing the effect a new digital system or process has on C2 performance. Historically, data for C2 assessment has been gathered directly from humans in the form of observations, interviews and surveys. While measurement of human to human interactions remain key to a well-functioning C2 organisation, the growing importance and wealth of digital data available presents an opportunity to assess C2  in hitherto unexplored ways. By using digital C2 data we can automate and expand the breadth and volume of information gathered for C2 assessment while also collecting data that was previously impractical to gather from human observations. This paper presents a generalised methodology for gathering digital data from C2 systems and applying it in conjunction with human observable data to assess C2 across the entire sociotechnical spectrum. This methodology has been tested on the Australian Defence Force (ADF) operational planning process in the context of an ADF Command Post Exercise (CPX).

Title: An information architecture supported by ontologies to provide reporting of incidents related to public safety
Authors: Cruz, J.; Prandel, P. C.; Victorino, M. C.; Ishikawa, E.
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: In Brazil, several large-scale events are held, directly involving the need to maintain public order, such as major sporting events, elections, public manifestations, among others. Technological advances have uncovered a natural trend to digitalize the means of obtaining data and providing information, improving the planning and monitoring of operations aimed at public safety. The nature of  current  conflicts  allows  the  perception  of  relevance  in  obtaining  data  through  human observers.  In  the  information  systems currently employed in events involving public safety, the description of incidents is usually performed by field agents in natural language,  without  standardized  structuring,  which  makes  it  difficult  to  further  extract  useful  knowledge.  The  formation  of  a semantically represented information base becomes very valuable for optimized recovery of knowledge. Considering this context, the objective of this work is to provide Information Architecture focused on obtaining data through field observers, in an assisted way,  as  well  as  to  provide  availability  of  the  generated  information,  having  as  parameters  the  organization  and  structuring  of information, employing ontologies for its semantic representation. In order to materialize the proposed Information Architecture, a software prototype was constructed that uses the data entry by users, guided by an ontological model, to enable the structuring of reports regarding observed occurrences. As contributions of this work highlight an assisted model to enable the recording of data from events, as well as the availability of structured reports to provide support for analyzing the information obtained.

Title: An Empirical Test of the C2 Agility Model in a Crisis Management Situation
Authors: Turcotte, I.; Jobidon, M.; Farrell, P.; Tremblay, S.
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: High and fluctuating demands of defence and security situations require teams to adopt the appropriate command and control (C2) approach in order to achieve their operational goals. C2 agility is defined as the ability to transition from one approach to another as required by situation complexity (SAS-065, 2010; SAS-085, 2014). It can be posited that transition could be facilitated or inhibited by factors both within and outside the organization. The objectives of this study were to examine transitions between C2 approaches and the impact of team size, resistance and variations in situation complexity on the transition processes and the performance of crisis management teams. The C3Fire microworld was used to create various conditions under which a need for transitioning between C2 approaches could arise. All teams received training for the de-conflicted and collaborative C2 approaches prior to completing two experimental scenarios. They could use the approach that they deemed fit to deal with the crisis situation, and change their approach over the course of a scenario if they wanted. Resistance was manipulated with the help of confederates either resisting or promoting transitions. Results showed that teams transitioned between C2 approaches as a function of situation complexity, but that resistance and team size had a limited impact on C2 agility. Moreover, transitioning to a more network-enabled C2 approach when facing an increase in situation complexity was associated with better team performance. The findings are discussed in the context of C2 approach transitions and the C2 Agility model.

Title: MURA Snapshot: Development of a Sociotechnical Survey Tool for Headquarters Assessment
Authors: McTernan, W.; Kalloniatis, A.; Tsui, S.
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Modern joint military headquarters are fast paced and dynamic environments that act as the nexus of information for multidomain operations. To understand the complex sociotechnical system of a joint operations headquarters, a sophisticated assessment framework and tool isrequired. This paper discusses the development of the ' MURA Snapshot ' : a survey instrument designed to capture key sociotechnical dimensions critical to the success of a modern joint operations headquarters. The MURA Snapshot is proposed as part of the longitudinal tracking of improvement under the Australian headquarters modernisation project: Plan MURA. In this paper we begin by providing an overview of the evolution of joint headquarters within Australia, contextualising the military environment the MURA Snapshot aims to assess. Then we discuss a history of assessment in military headquarters and models of assessment. Finally we discuss our process for the development of the MURA Snapshot. In describing the development of the MURA Snapshot, we explore the literature on survey design, its principles, and key survey design considerations including the use of single and multi-item measures, the use of established versus bespoke survey measures, scale composition and compatibility with extant models. The discussion of this literature may provide useful insights for military practitioners when developing future surveys.

Title: Big Data Solutions on Tactical Infrastructure
Authors: Langleite, R.; Opland, E. A. F.; Johnsen, F. T.
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: In this paper, we discuss the need for explainable artificial intelligence (AI) in defense systems. Further, we elaborate on the need for Big Data solutions to support AI on tactical infrastructure, and discuss an architectural approach to address this need. Finally, we present our proof of concept implementation of this architecture, instantiated to support the human concepts of fast and slow thinking. The proof of concept was built using free and open source software to allow the solution to be shared, and our approach to be repeatable for others.

Title: Bi-Temporality in a Military Information Model
Authors: Stucken, S.; Lena, P.; Gerz, M.; Bau, N.
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Command and control information systems exchange and process battlefield information with temporal reference which changes over time. This opens the possibility of raising questions based on the available information in the past, present, and future. For instance, we might ask: 'where was the convoy on 04/08/1999 at 08:43?', 'what was known about the location of the convoy on 04/18/1999 at 08:45?', or 'was it known on 03/23/2009 that the network service would be unavailable on 03/26/2009?' In order to answer these types of questions, a system has to be based on uniform data modelling incorporating temporal dimensions. There are two main timelines: Effective Time and Record Time . Effective time denotes the time when a battlefield event happens. Record time, as the second timeline, denotes the time when the system of the operator got notice of it. The understanding of these two concepts is one of the most difficult problems to solve while designing information systems. Applications that use both time dimensions (bi-temporal) are not a marginal phenomenon, but there is a great need for effective and efficient support. Our work and use cases are based on the MIP (Multilateral Interoperability Programme) Information Model (MIM), which contains concepts with a large number of attributes for specifying various time aspects. In some specific scenarios, such specifications may cause ambiguities. Different temporal design patterns are investigated with respect to their applicability. Additionally, we propose a consistent and canonical representation of different time dimensions in the MIM model. Only bi-/multi-temporal data allows to retrieve what and when we knew particular information. Our aim with this work is to avoid ambiguities and enable the answering to the initial questions.

Title: Robust and Trusted Artificial Intelligence through Refined Processes and Metrics for Military Applications
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Artificial  Intelligence  (AI)  and  Machine  Learning  (ML)  technologies  are  being  increasingly  used  in  commercial  and  military applications. Their successful operationalization will be critical for competitive advantage in future warfare. While they offer great promise in support of decision-making in the command and control domain, e.g., for enhanced situational awareness, resource planning and allocation optimization, the development and deployment of robust and trusted AI-enabled applications in large-scale and real-world context remains challenging. In particular, this requires rigorous design, development and validation processes that incorporate additional properties exhibited in AI frameworks, including robustness, risk, explainability, trust, uncertainty, as well as legal and ethics.  We consider each of them and present the requirements, the methods proposed to address them, and evaluation metrics  that  are  applicable.  These  elements  are  analyzed  from  the  perspective  of  military-related  problems,  in  particular  for perception and decision problems.
Author Keywords: ROBUST ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE; TRUSTWORTHY AI ASSURANCE; AI SYSTEM TESTING, EVALUATION, VERIFICATION (TEVV); RISK-AWARE MACHINE LEARNING; EXPLAINABLE AI (XAI) AND INTERPRETABILITY; UNCERTAINTY QUANTIFICATION IN DEEP LEARNING; REINFORCEMENT LEARNING OPERATIONS (RLOPS); MULTIMODAL DATA FUSION; LEGAL AND ETHICAL GOVERNANCE OF MILITARY AI

Title: Can crisis management be seen as harmonization of efforts?
Authors: Herkevall, J.; Johansson, B. J. E.
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: This paper argues that the term crisis management often is misleading when discussing inter-organizational crisis response operations. Many countries lack centralized mechanisms for managing crises and rely upon cooperation as the main method for creating focus and convergence. Furthermore, the inherent uncertainty of crises makes it impossible to rely on pre-planned structures . Research on real-world crises rather suggests that crisis response only partly depends upon pre-planned management structures and that actual response systems emerge and evolve as the situation unfolds. Often, multiple government agencies, municipal rescue services, healthcare services, and volunteer organizations become involved in an endeavor, all with their individual objectives in relation to the crisis as a whole. This paper presents a framework for describing and discussing the processes by which crisis response organizations cooperate, achieve common intent, and coordinate activities and resources as harmonization of efforts . Examples from the COVID-19 pandemic response are provided to illustrate how harmonization  was achieved in the context of regional healthcare in Sweden.

Title: Exploring Command and Control of Semi-Autonomous Units: Co-constructing the future battlefield using a tactical board game
Authors: Saleh, D.; Pestrea, A.; Johansson, B. J.
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The introduction of semi-autonomous units on the battlefield raises many questions related to command and control (C2). Some of these involve command responsibilities and tactical decision making concerning whether the usage of unmanned ground vehicles (UGV) or unmanned aerial vehicles (UAV) can potentially be allocated to a number of different roles within the organizational structure of a battalion. Due to the differences between the purposes and operating conditions for UGVs and UAVs, different individuals are likely to command them. However, such units can be used in a coordinated fashion, suggesting that these commanders need to collaborate. Two workshops have been conducted with personnel from the Swedish Land Warfare Centre. During these, a tactical board game was used as a basis for discussing possibilities and limitations of using semi-autonomous UGVs and UAVs in ground warfare scenarios. Both workshops were video recorded and transcribed. The transcriptions showed that an understanding of the autonomous units, and possible ways of using them (attack/defense), was developed during the workshops. A thematic analysis revealed different perspectives on how semi-autonomous units can be used, what roles they can undertake on the battlefield, in what ways they could be incorporated into existing command structures, and what kind of capabilities they should have to be useful.
Author Keywords: COMMAND AND CONTROL (C2); SEMI-AUTONOMOUS UNMANNED GROUND VEHICLES (UGV); UNMANNED AERIAL VEHICLES (UAV); TACTICAL BOARD GAME METHODOLOGY; HUMAN-AUTONOMY TEAMING; MILITARY WARGAMING; AUTONOMY LEVEL TAXONOMY; THEMATIC ANALYSIS OF C2 IMPLICATIONS

Title: Implications of Future Security Environment Trends on C2 Governance
Authors: Jobidon, M.; Bryant, D.; Davies, G. M.; Dekker, R.; Gaertner, P.; Granåsen, M.; Hardie, B.; Houghton, P.; Kalloniatis, A.; Wikberg, P.; Wikström, M.
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Despite many initiatives among NATO and allied nations to transform command and control (C2), progress in adapting C2 to the needs of the current and future security environment (FSE) has been limited [1]. As a result, traditional methods and structures have been retained despite significant technological advances, which raises concerns that our overall C2 capability will be inadequate to address the challenges of the FSE. A first key step to developing best practices for C2 transformation is to identify what future C2 requirements might be. These requirements will be largely determined by the FSE, which encompasses the broad set of expectations concerning  the  environments  in  which  militaries  will  operate  in  the  future.  Under  NATO  HFM-342  'C2  Capability  Lifecycle Governance',  we  explore  the  ways  in  which  NATO  nations  will  have  to  change  their  approaches  to  governing  C2  capability development. An analysis of NATO and allied documents was conducted to identify and synthesize the major forecasting trends of the FSE that represent a core set of challenges and requirements for future C2. This paper presents an overview of this work, including a description of themes that emerged from the analysis of the literature and of the implications of these themes for future C2. Generally, forecasts suggest that the FSE will entail greater conflict among both nation states and non-state actors, occurring in a broad  range  of  physical,  social,  economic,  and  informational  domains,  in  complex  environments,  with  adversaries  employing inventive, non-traditional strategies and tactics. The paper ends with a discussion of the implications of the FSE for C2 capability and the challenges entailed regarding the transformation of C2 governance.

Title: Tactical Data Link Interoperability: MDLP Phase 2
Authors: Botelho, T. A. T.; Cecílio, E. L.; Oliveira, A. F.
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The Multi Data Link Processor (MDLP) project team, sponsored by Brazilian Ministry of Defense, develops a set of gateways in a distributed architecture that will allow the exchange of messages among tactical networks using distinct Tactical data links (TDL) used during  Brazilian  Armed  Forces Joint  Operations,  e.g.,  the  Yb  and  STERNA  naval  TDL,  the  BR1  and  BR2  Air  Force  TDL,  and tactical networks using SDR and radars. During this first phase of development, the team has achieved successful results carrying out  a  field  interoperability  test  between  link  Yb  and  the  new  Navy  link  STERNA,  last  September.  Results  will  be  shown  in  this article. There has been an evolutionary focus over the protocol, new performance tests are in place and two ways of interaction via proxy or via capture/insertion into the network are now available. Project team is now coding the necessary software to achieve interoperability between the air force link BR2 and the navy links cited above. In this second phase the team is researching  to achieve interoperability among the already cited links and the link Adelis A, which will be installed on the new Tamandaré class Frigates.

Title: Multi-Criteria Decision-Making for Uncertainty of Information
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Decision-making is a very complex task that considers a wide range of factors, particularly for Command and Control (C2). While information is critical to C2 decisions, there is the realization that there are levels of uncertainty tied to any information. Thus, this becomes another factor in the decision-making process. Multi-Criteria Decision-Making (MCDM) methods provide an algorithmic approach for evaluating, prioritizing and selecting the most optimal alternative decision from a set of available options. Therefore, MCDM can help address the challenges of multiple factors and potential conflicting choices. There are definitely more than one MCDM method that can be used to aid in decision-making particularly for dynamic and complex environments, each with its pros and cons in generating a solution. A hybrid method that combines multiple methods that leverages the strengths and minimize weaknesses across methods would be ideal. For this work two approaches were selected, AHP and TOPSIS. They were coupled with dynamic calculations, to form a novel Dynamic MCDM (DMCDM) method, which is the focus of this research effort. As mentioned previously one of the key factors to decision-making is uncertainty, thus the concept of Uncertainty of Information (UoI) is an approach that takes a different perspective for addressing this issue. From a recent experimental user study, we have asked soldiers to complete a questionnaire based on the UoI taxonomy for various data source types. We will apply the new DMCDM to the UoI related data for Army relevant scenarios and perform an analysis to uncover prioritization of UoI that maybe relevant for supporting C2 decisions.

Title: Planning for Safety when Preparing for War: Competition Brings Out the Wickedness of the Military
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The study investigates how two-sided military exercises affect learning and participating personnel's ability to perform tasks safely when confronted with an equally matched opponent. Data were collected from two separate events the Northern Wind 2019 Final Planning Conference and the Northern Wind 2019 exercise using participatory observations and shadowing. The outline of the critical incident technique was used as a guide when designing the data collection and the activity theory framework was used for coding and analyzing the data. The results indicate that a driven desire to win two-sided military exercises can create unsafe work practices by performing work tasks without regard for occupational-, electrical- or fire safety. The results also indicate a need for reshaping the conception of a two-sided military exercise by treating the exercise as a learning opportunity and recognizing the impact of the participating unit on each other's learning. The study is based on the results of one army exercise and requires further research to understand the activities that take place and how they affect learning and safety. However, it gives some insight into the learning problems occurring during military field exercises.

Title: Continuing the debate on Russian command approach: Are we seeing a mission command approach?
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Recent years have seen some speculation amongst command researchers as to whether the Russian military may be looking to augment its modernization process by adopting more decentralized approaches to command and control. This paper examines some of the available published evidence and also considers this question in light of the limited information available in news reports and expert commentaries associated with the February 2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine. At present it is too soon to conclude whether the Russians are currently pursuing a more ‘mission-command’ approach and evidence from Ukraine suggests that, given the substantial challenges faced in that theatre, especially in the early phases of the war, a more traditional approach has been adopted in general.

Title: Pulse and Pace: Two Desirable Attributes Extending Legacy Systems Effectiveness
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: There are many announcements of the immediate obsolescence of several weapons systems managed by C2 systems.  The tank currently is the most popular candidate for the warfighting scrap heap.  Their alleged obsolescence seems foreordained by their centralized and vulnerable C2 systems (human commanders) which are certain to be aboard any given armored platform.  However, there is systems evidence that we may be throwing the baby out with the bathwater.  Tanks, like manned aircraft or manned vessels of any type (air, ground or sea), have a common C2 characteristic: a centralized C2 function or process (brain.)  Incapacitate the brain, incapacitate the weapon and its capabilities.   This paper proposes an alternative for land systems: distributed AI with human in the loop C2. The proposed system creates a information “fog” that can be adjusted and adapted to the relevant operational environment.  The system is well within known computational and algorithmic limits, and can be quickly attached.   What should follow are simulation experiments to identify plausibility and possibility of the efficiency and effectiveness of such systems.

Title: The Variety Calculus Planning Methodology An alternative planning concept for complex operations
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Many of the deterministic assumptions inherent in conventional military planning become less valid under complex circumstances. This paper describes an alternative approach that is designed for tasks and situations that are complex, unpredictable, uncertain and ill-defined. The Variety Calculus Planning Methodology is based on a cybernetic approach to operations. It addresses complexity by working iteratively, starting at a high level of abstraction and progressing systematically towards greater detail. A novel enquiry framework is used to generate a narrative that expresses an understanding of the situation and the operation purpose. The tasks required to deliver the operation are identified from the narrative and a model that maps the dependencies between tasks is constructed. The plan thus takes the form of a network of interdependent tasks rather than distinct lines of effort. Each task can be subjected to its own equivalent analysis to generate further detail with each iteration. The dependency map informs the relationships required between actors or activities and thus the organisational structure and command & control of the enterprise. Activity planning is therefore integrated with organisational design. Complex situations demand a less structured and certain approach with the application of multiple disparate instruments. This method can be thought of as a shift from an algorithmic approach (specific actions to deliver defined results), to a heuristic approach (creating a system capable of generating appropriate solutions).

Title: The Dimension Assessment: A Battlespace Management approach to Multi-Domain Integration
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The UK MOD seeks to achieve greater levels of integration between the military and its non-military partners as well as between the five military operational domains: maritime, land, air, space, and cyber. This aspiration is highlighted in JCN 1/20, where Multi-Domain Integration is defined as: ‘the posturing of military capabilities in concert with other instruments of national power, allies and partners; configured to sense, understand and orchestrate effects at the optimal tempo, across the operational domains and levels of warfare.’ To enable better integration, there needs to be consideration of the numerous seams of responsibility, influence and interest that currently exist within the military and between military and non-military actors. These seams may form the windows of vulnerability that adversaries will continue to seek to exploit, both above and below the threshold of conflict. The identification of such windows is obscured by the lack of a common codified language. This paper offers a conceptual tool, based on logical concepts and underpinning terminology presented during ICCRTS 2020, to support the consideration of aspects outside traditional Defence related activity. The tool therefore is designed to help define the areas that form these seams. It is expected that improving understanding of these domain boundaries will enable effective efficient and resilient integration. The tool facilitates the identification of the core Dimensions that are relevant to the Environment in which a Multi-Domain force may be deployed. It is intended to support the identification of the force capabilities required, and to assist in identifying what factors are important to the local audiences (civilian, host nation, adversarial, etc.) within that Environment.

Title: Machine Learning Empowered LoRaWAN for Command and Control (C2) Agility
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: In  this  paper,  a  quantitative  analysis  has  been  devised  for  normalized  readiness  (NR),  which  is  the  resultant  vector  of  three perpendiculars  of  each  other  vectors: throughput  performance,  delay  performance  and  threat-hazard  resiliency  performance. Providing  long  range  connectivity  for  Internet  of  Battlefield  Thing  (IoBT)  devices  from  all  military  domains-air,  ground,  naval, space,  and  cyber  forces  is  one  of  the  important  multi-domain  operations  applications.  The  long  range  low  power  wide  area network (LoRaWAN) wireless  technology may deliver  the  solution  for  connecting  a  large  number  of  IoBT  devices.  The  original LoRaWAN is pure ALOHA based, which is very simple, easy to implement and excellent for connecting a large number of distributed IoBT  devices.  The  throughput  performance  of  pure  ALOHA  may  collapse,  if  the  coverage  number  of  IoBT  devices  is  very  large and/or  packets  transmissions  occur  more  often.  Command  and  Control  (C2)  Agility  case  studies  and  experiment  studies  have manifested that there is no single best C2 Approach or most efficient communications algorithm that worked well for all missions and circumstances. Consequently, pure ALOHA may be most appropriate communications algorithm for low endeavour complex (complexity and the dynamics of the 'mission space') missions/circumstances. The use of Slotted ALOHA instead of pure ALOHA can solve this throughput performance collapse problem by doubling the throughput performance. However, the use of Slotted ALOHA cannot increase the delay performance and threat-hazard resiliency performance. Consequently, NR increases from 31.9% to 36.8% only; and thus may be appropriate for the medium endeavour complex missions and circumstances, but may not be appropriate for medium-high complex missions and circumstances. The medium-high complex missions are becoming very common nowadays and  those  need  higher  threat-hazard  resiliency  performance  along  with  higher  throughput  performance  and  higher  delay performance. In this paper, we have added the machine learning empowered fast retransmissions trials accompanying with the Slotted ALOHA (MLeFRSA) instead of pure ALOHA in LoRaWAN. Results show that the proposed communications algorithm not only makes the throughput performance more than triple, but also significantly increases the delay performance and threat-hazard resiliency performance; and thus outperform two other choices in all dimensions simultaneously. As a result, NR increases from 31.9% to 60.04%; and thus may be appropriate for medium-high endeavour complex missions and circumstances. C2 agility and a hypothetical  relationship  between  the  appropriate  communications  algorithms,  appropriate  C2  Approaches  and  endeavour complex missions/circumstances have also been discussed in this paper.

Title: Graph-based Representation of Military Action Spaces
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Let us consider a land-based battle space scenario where the adversary's intent is to attack towards a specific geographic area and our mission is to conduct a delaying operation. We assess the terrain within our area of interest, define time phase lines and identify the critical points, including the decision points for the adversary's actions. By linking these points, we arrive at a graph with the critical points as nodes and the links between these nodes as edges. Both nodes and edges can be further specified by attributes describing capacity, average speed, required capabilities etc. The graph can be considered a representation of the adversary's action space. Graph traversals define specific courses of action (CoA). The graph can be extended with additional nodes and edges that are not immediately derivable from the surface map. This is useful in order to represent supporting operations within other dimensions, e.g. aerial operations. The graph representation is suitable for automatic analysis, such as the assessment of adversary CoAs, the determination of the most dangerous and the most likely CoA, the assessment of countermeasures and the respective planning as well as control. The graph can be mapped back to the original situational picture, for assessing whether the preconditions of CoAs are fulfilled, among other analyses.  We can determine missing information needed for identifying the actual CoA and, thus, support reconnaissance planning.

Title: Edge computing for disaster relief operations
Authors: Vatne, N.; Griwodz, C.; Johnsen, F. T.
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Humanitarian Assistance and Disaster Relief (HADR) operations are needed to alleviate the effects of natural disasters for the local population. In HADR operations, military, first responders, and possibly non-governmental organizations all need to collaborate towards the same goals. Hence, there is a need for collective C2, to ensure that these partners are coordinated and have sufficient situational awareness to fulfill their tasks. In support of collective C2, it is paramount to prepare and build capacity and ensure interoperability between the needed ad-hoc deployable communications solutions. In addition to communications, there is a need to provide flexible computing and storage resources in such environments where existing infrastructure is damaged or destroyed. In this paper, we investigate Kubernetes as the resource framework for Edge computing in HADR operations. We consider the extreme case in which we operate independently from existing infrastructure.

Title: Bridging the gap between desktop-based C2 research and large-scale C2 experimentation
Authors: Turner, P.; Leggatt, A.; Smith, G.
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The maturation of C2 concepts, from initiation to validation, relies upon C2 research, experimentation and assessment activities that generate evidence and military stakeholder confidence. The success of such activities requires: An understanding of benefits and costs of potential C2 research, experimentation and assessment methods. The ability to select the most appropriate method for C2 concept maturation studies.The availability of tools to enact methods. The UK MOD funded research from 2020-22, as part of Dstl’s Agile C2 project, to address these requirements. The key outputs from the research were the C2 Research, Experimentation and Assessment Tool Estimate (CREATE) and the C2 Experimentation Laboratory (C2EL). CREATE provides a handrail for C2 researchers in selecting appropriate C2 research, experimentation and assessment methods and tools. The C2EL was designed to provide flexible support to Limited Objective Experimentation (LOE) and Microworld Experimentation. These two methods support concept development, provide intermediate levels of ecological validity and generate intermediate levels of evidence, thereby bridging the gap between desktop-based C2 research and large-scale C2 experimentation. This paper describes both CREATE and the C2EL, draws conclusions regarding the benefits they offer to C2 concept maturation and includes discussion of the relevance of the research to the international C2 S&T community.

Title: Baseline Assessment of Object Detection Models on Partially Occluded Objects and their Parts
Authors: Jefferson, D. E.; Harrison, A. V.
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Object detection models are an important element in the research and development of systems that aim to provide continuous, adaptive,  and  autonomous  situational  awareness  of  the  battlefield.  There  is  a  long  history  or  work  that  has  gone  into  the development of object recognition models and the datasets they are assessed against.  However, most datasets, and as a result the models  developed  from  them,  focus  on  straightforward  scenarios  (well-lit  environments,  unoccluded  objects,  no  atmospheric interference, etc.).  But this poses a problem in more challenging battlefield scenarios.  For instance, many current object models flounder when they encounter partially occluded objects. This is because the models may not recognize the objects while parts of them are covered. Additionally, occlusion is not a condition that many object detection models are specifically designed to handle. Overcoming this obstacle is of utmost priority, considering the high likelihood of intentionally occluded objects on the battlefield. The main objective of this work was to perform a baseline assessment of the Gonzalez-Garcia model compared with the Faster RCNN model and YOLOv5 using the PASCAL VOC 2010 and PASCAL-Part datasets. Of course, these datasets contain many examples of partially  occluded  objects  and  their  parts.  The  results  from  each  evaluation  were  then  compared  to  determine  their  overall effectiveness and accuracy on partially occluded objects. This paper will present an overview of each model, the VOC 2010 and PASCAL-Part datasets, and discuss the results from training/validating them on these datasets.

Title: Modelling command and control: potential negative effects of transferring features of the individual to the organization
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract:
Both academics and professionals often claim that individuals in defense enterprises with access to the most relevant information and who possess the best situation awareness should decide upon what to do. This claim is typically interpreted as the person closest to the situation should decide, inferring that closeness automatically provides the best situation awareness. This interpretation is problematical however, partly because the term 'situation' is undefined and partly because the individual would have to always be provided with information regarding the whole enterprise and also have the possibility to assimilate this information.

This interpretation is probably influenced by prevalent descriptions or models of command and control (C2). The field of C2 research has developed models of C2 covering several different perspectives. Two distinct classes of models are represented in this paper. First, models stemming from an individual perspective, typically focused on decision-making are highlighted. Second, models focused on a systemic and activities perspective on C2 are presented. We refer to these as reference models. This paper provides a relational analysis between the models of individual decision making and the C2 reference models. The analysis relates key features from the models of individual decision making to cornerstone aspects of the C2 reference models, especially aspects such as centralized/decentralized decision-rights. The discussion covers arguments to why it is important to avoid unreflected and noncritical transfer of features from one class of models to the other.

Title: Updating the Concept and Execution of Narrative-Led Operations
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Narrative-led operations is a command-and-control concept that calls for operations to be initiated with the formulation of a narrative that thereupon becomes the basis for both military action and communication pertaining to and contextualizing those actions (Nissen, 2015). Since the concept has been developed, the challenges for militaries to maintain narrative consistency and narrative authority in the information environment have, however, only multiplied with the greater proliferation of social media and instantaneous participative broadcasting as well as adversarial information operations and amplification of hostile narratives being continually perpetrated on social media. This article proposes a set of models and planning concepts and tools that democratic militaries can use to become more competitive in the IE by allowing them to develop a more coordinated approach to creating operational narratives and augmenting them with real-time narrative intelligence and narrative targeting procedures. These include the Narrative Pyramid Model for creating a synchronized mission-command approach to generating voluminous narrative as well as a basis in literary theory for underpinning operational narratives with classically engaging narrative templates and effects.

Title: Development and Deployment of a Headquarters Maturity Model - A Case Study
Authors: Ellis, R.; Wise, P.; Hill, J.
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Maturity Models are common tools for capability development and monitoring across commerce and industry.  Such models typically define a number of organisational features, each with several levels of maturity.  An organisation can use a Maturity Model to assess its areas of strength and weakness to develop and prioritise actions needed for self-improvement.  The UK MOD’s Joint Concept Note 2/17 (“Future of Command and Control”) introduced an initial HQ Maturity Model (HQMM) in 2017. Since then, Dstl has continued to explore the use of maturity models to support development of Command and Control (C2) capability by Operational level military headquarters (HQs).  Working in partnership with six UK HQs, notably UK Strategic Command Permanent Joint Headquarters (PJHQ), a generic HQMM was developed for wide application across the UK Defence enterprise. The current model has eighteen so-called ‘Dimensions’, each with five maturity levels, and examples of measures and metrics as a starting point for organisations to develop a specific model that is tailored to their needs. This paper describes the design and evolution of the HQMM and discusses ways it might be used by military organisations as a tool to support continuous improvement efforts.

Title: Shifting between coordination forms: The role of competencies and organizational structure
Authors: Sigmund Valaker; Rune Stensrud; Arne Cato Jenssen; Walther Åsen; Astri Maria Ravnå Nyaas; Tarjei Andersen
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: How do one make sure that an organization is capable of coordinating under changing adverse circumstances? And How could one be able to shift who is responsible for coordinating, if needed? These are critical questions for military and civilian organizations that strive to fulfill complex goals. Examples include: integration of military land air and sea assets or  agencies such as police, health, fire departments. In this article we explore  what characterize and what may be the influence of competencies to coordinate and the available forms of coordinating, i.e. who can be designated to coordinate. We report findings from a military organization, using a survey to shed light on competencies needed and a communication log  from an exercise to shed light on coordination forms used. Regarding competencies we found that at the joint level being able to hold authority to coordinate is emphasized, at the tactical level understanding the various teams and at the sub-tactical level specific expertise in tactical tasks. Regarding coordination form we found that the organization shifted its coordination form from hierarchical to more delegated and back to hierarchical during the exercise. Our findings shed light on what types of competencies may be distributed among the organizations members in order to coordinate. Furthermore the enacted coordination forms, while useful, may be too restrictive to enable shifts to more decentralized coordination forms. We point to further direction for operational analysis testing of our hypothesis such as through more extensive logging of exercises (e.g. the common operational picture) and performance.
Author Keywords: MULTITEAM SYSTEMS; COORDINATION COMPETENCE; COMMAND AND CONTROL (C2) STRUCTURES; HIERARCHICAL VS DECENTRALIZED COORDINATION FORMS; SOCIAL NETWORK ANALYSIS; GRAPH-THEORETIC METRICS (ASSORTATIVITY, CENTRALIZATION); BOUNDARY SPANNING AND TRANSACTIVE MEMORY; CONTINGENCY THEORY IN ORGANIZATIONAL DESIGN
