Title: Agent Based Modelling to demonstrate self-synchronisation at the Battle of Trafalgar: a demonstration of computational methods for teaching Command and Control
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Command and Control (C2) research demonstrates the value of decentralised decision making and agility.  However, the complex situational factors that lead to self-organisation could be a barrier to teaching and understanding *power to the edge.* This paper explores the use of Agent Based Modelling (ABM), as an educational technique to visually demonstrate the effect of decentralised decision making at the Battle of Trafalgar.  Using Net Logo simulation software, the movement of two fleets of warships can replicated.  By using a few simple rules, agents simulating the Royal Navy Grand Fleet clearly demonstrate self-organisation, collaboration, and situational awareness.  This shows the effect of emergence from complexity theory, where simple rules at one level result in self-organised and complex behaviour at another.  Whilst the agents in the model cannot share information, their behaviour closely simulates self-synchronisation.  The simulation allows levels of autonomy and situational awareness to be adjusted with the results demonstrating the efficacy of decentralised decision making in specific situations.   The result of demonstrating the agility of independent agents makes a striking and straightforward affirmation of Edge C2 theory, through the historically well-known example of a famous battle.  The paper will demonstrate how the simulation was created and the features which show self-organisation, agility, collaborative action, and situational awareness.  As well as describing the basic operation of the programme, the paper will link this with C2 and complexity theory, showing how to use the model for educational purposes.
Author Keywords: AGENT-BASED MODELING; SELF-SYNCHRONISATION; EDGE COMMAND AND CONTROL; COMPLEXITY THEORY; EMERGENT BEHAVIOR; NETLOGO SIMULATION; DECENTRALISED DECISION MAKING; SELF-ORGANISING CRITICALITY; MILITARY EDUCATION

Title: Focus on Commander’s Intent to Improve C2
Authors: Shigley, P.; Carreño, J.; Galdorisi, G.; Morton, C.; Tanner, R.
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Effectively developed and conveyed Commander’s intent compensates for imperfect command and control (C2) systems. With the recent Russian invasion of Ukraine, Grant’s (1995) distillation of Coase’s Theory of Firm [organization] (1931) is operationalized in living color. Coase’s theory states that the critical resources required for an organization’s success are held within the boundaries of the organization. Grant focuses further, and concludes that the definition of the organization is encapsulated within the specific knowledge that it holds. In short, the defining strength of an organization is the people and their specific knowledge and skills. Dierickx & Cool (1989) define these as the organization’s unique stocks that provide competitive advantage. Recent events in Ukraine appear to illustrate these theories. Much has been made of Russia’s logistics debacle, but that might be taken as one symptom of a more fundamental flaw: the inability to flow the needed knowledge to those who must organize and execute the desired mission. Simply stated, a complete lack of knowledge of what the commander’s intent is, and an inability to move the knowledge required throughout their forces to effectively execute their campaign. This paper considers the movement of knowledge through an organization from the theoretical perspective of Knowledge Flow Theory as captured in Shigley’s 2021 dissertation. It connects this theory operationally through the Joint Planning Guide 5-0, with specific consideration on the importance of the Commander’s Intent guidance. Finally, it considers how analogy could play a critical role in ensuring that knowledge (guidance) is effectively transferred across an organization so that it can effectively execute its mission(s).
Author Keywords: COMMANDER’S INTENT; COMMAND AND CONTROL (C2); KNOWLEDGE FLOW THEORY; KNOWLEDGE FRICTION FACTORS; ANALOGICAL REASONING IN MILITARY PLANNING; ORGANIZATIONAL KNOWLEDGE MANAGEMENT; COASE'S THEORY OF THE FIRM IN DEFENSE CONTEXTS; JOINT PLANNING GUIDE (JP) 5-0

Title: The C2 Agility Model in a Crisis Management Situation: An Empirical Test
Authors: Turcotte, I.; Jobidon, M.-E.; Farrell, P. S. E.; Tremblay, S.
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: High and fluctuating demands of defence and security situations require teams to adopt the appropriate command and control (C2) approach in order to achieve their operational goals. C2 agility is defined as the ability to transition from one approach to another as required by situation complexity (SAS-065, 2010; SAS-085, 2014). It can be posited that transition could be facilitated or inhibited by factors both within and outside the organization. The objectives of this study were to examine transitions between C2 approaches and the impact of team size, resistance to changing C2 approaches, and variations in situation complexity on the transition processes and the performance of crisis management teams. The C3Fire microworld was used to create various conditions under which a need for transitioning between C2 approaches could arise. All teams received training for the de-conflicted and collaborative C2 approaches prior to completing two experimental scenarios. They could use the approach that they deemed fit to deal with the crisis situation, and change their approach over the course of a scenario if they wanted. Resistance to transitioning was manipulated with the help of confederates either resisting or promoting transitions. Results showed that teams transitioned between C2 approaches as a function of situation complexity, but that resistance and team size had a limited impact on C2 agility. Moreover, transitioning to a more network-enabled C2 approach when facing an increase in situation complexity was associated with better team performance. The findings are discussed in the context of C2 approach transitions and the C2 Agility model.
Author Keywords: C2 AGILITY; COMMAND AND CONTROL (C2) APPROACHES; CRISIS MANAGEMENT TEAMS; SITUATION COMPLEXITY; TEAM SIZE AND RESISTANCE; MICROWORLD SIMULATION; C3FIRE FIREFIGHTING SIMULATION; ORGANIZATIONAL AGILITY; PERFORMANCE AND RESOURCE REDISTRIBUTION

Title: Blurring the lines: Merging aspects of human cognition and artificial intelligence
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The purpose of this article is to discuss the transformation of Twitter from a usable tool for spreading information to a space for learning. When we talk about different software, we in many cases can’t phantom their outreach and connectivity. Specific software solutions have been integrated into our lives making them a part of ourselves. Specific tools can be designed to enhance specific functions within the software such as automatic accounts spreading keywords users write. Users are humans and perceive their cyber environment in the same way as they perceive human interaction in real life. The transformation changed the tool to an area of operation where different stakeholders can interact with each other. From an intelligence perspective, the distinction between technology and HUMINT no longer serves its purposes of classification – it has merged into one and the same. From the perspective of C2, information technology in itself is still emphasized at the cost of other dimensions, hence obscuring the very mechanisms of how what is only seen as social media in fact is a dimension of itself. While the human dimension is sometimes addressed, there is a dearth of research exploring its workings.

Title: Position Paper: Expanding Deception Detection in Social Media from a Military Perspective
Authors: Devereaux, Z. P.; Forrester, B.; Lecocq, R.; Cinq-Mars, P.; Labrie, M.-A.
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Since 2016, western democracies have seen large increases of influence campaigns within social media (SM) in an effort to elect right leaning governments, disrupt western countries and NATO, and to undermine democracy through exploitation of ideological division and promotion of anarchy. While it is widely accepted knowledge who was behind such deceptive attacks, attribution remains a problem, making it difficult to clearly understand the pursued objectives and to set appropriate responses. To address the challenges of attribution and intent, a deception detection framework was presented by Forrester and Von Franke (2020) at ICCRTS 25 designed to guide research on deception within SM. This current paper moves this framework forward. The paper starts with a circumscribed literature review, then an algorithm is proposed that automates the identification of non-authentic actors (often referred to as Bots) found in the earlier work (Forrester et al., 2019). This position paper argues that automated actors will soon be able to exploit the disruptive power of recent developments in Language-Learning-Models (LLM) and Foundational Models (FM), allowing  artificial  intelligence  (AI)  generated  disinformation  in  addition  to  the  current  ability  of  non-human propagation. These developments are concerning. Not only is there a pressing need to automatically identify nonhuman actors but there is also a need to understand the impact of this deceptive activity. Indeed, the degree of intent to deceive, as well as the level of harm Bot-led narratives can produce, requires automations for detection and a framework for understanding by analysts. Such impact can be determined though analysis of additional components within the deception detection framework which will direct future research and algorithm developments.

Title: Agile C2 Inspired Approaches:  Bridging Theory to Practice
Source title: 27th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Applying Agile C2 theory in practice can be challenging.  The author notes from his own attempts to apply (operationalize) Agile C2 theory in the US military during that last 12 years, that the resistance encountered is seldom due to a lack of understanding.  Most embrace the Agile C2 concept for its simplicity.  However, nearly all fail to follow-through with implementing the theory into practice due either to uncertainty of specific actions to take or uncertainty of success related to the risk of adopting a central idea that is not yet doctrine into practice.  An alternative implementation approach that the author has begun to use, abandons the Agile C2 label and borrows the ideas of Agile C2 in formulating Agile C2 Inspired approaches to C2.  Two such formulations explored in this paper involve the author's Defense Institution Building work in Lebanon and Uruguay.  This paper discusses two Agile C2 Inspired approaches:  1) Implementing headquarters governance over the totality of Lebanese Armed Forces staff actions as they update their Capability Development Plan; and 2) Constructing a C2 Supporting Concept for Joint Border Security in Uruguay.  In the Lebanese case, headquarters decision making, while improving, remains highly centralized and processes to support decision making are either non-existent or underdeveloped.  Internal lines of communication are slow and exclusive, defaulting to hoarding of useful information, recorded on paper within ledgers and only shared upon validated, often formal request.  In Uruguay, ea the military services operates separately, lacking operational unity of command and effort, seldom coordinating with each other during  operations.  This  separation  creates  capability  redundancies  resulting  from  demand-based  oversubscriptions  upon  very limited  resources  while  failing  to  harvest  the  added  effectiveness  that  can  result  from  planning  and  operating  jointly.    Both formulations  illustrate  a  possible  best  practice  of  starting  out  with  an  Agile  C2  Inspired  approach,  reinforced  with  hands-on experiences  that  can  then  lead  to  exploring  further  practical  implications  of  C2  Agility  theory  and  concepts  with  a  receptive audience.
Author Keywords: AGILE C2; COMMAND AND CONTROL (C2) THEORY; DEFENSE INSTITUTION BUILDING; CAPABILITY DEVELOPMENT PLANNING; JOINT BORDER SECURITY; DECISION‑MAKING FRAMEWORK; C2 AGILITY AND ADAPTABILITY; MILITARY STAFF COORDINATION; OPERATIONALIZATION OF C2 CONCEPTS

Title: Cyber Warfare Metrics
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Metrics are at the heart of any analysis. This paper presents a set of metrics designed to support a variety of analyses and assessments related to both the ability to successfully operate in a contested cyberspace environment and to identify an appropriate approach to multi-domain C2 for endeavors that include cyberspace operations. The metrics identified include those necessary to characterize: 1) a set of initial conditions for cyberspace operations; 2) the state of the portion of cyberspace that supports both kinetic and cyberspace operations; 3) losses of cyber capability; 4) the mission impacts and their significance; and 5) the resulting Cyber Risk to Mission. Finally, this paper discusses the relationships between and among these metrics that need to be incorporated into models to better understand the drivers of risk and perform sensitivity analyses.

Title: The Future of Command and Control – Evolution or Revolution?
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: This paper presents a hypothesis based on Cybernetic Control Theory that command and control (C2) cannot continue to develop as a directive hierarchical system to meet the challenges presented by the increasing complexity of military tasks and situations.  A paradigm shift in C2 is therefore required to ensure that operations remain effective in the future operating environment.  We use the cybernetic concepts of Variety and The Law of Requisite Variety to explore ideas of how the command system and the force co-evolved with the increasing sophistication of warfare.  We conclude that complex circumstances create an inherent tension between the conflicting requirements of the force and the C2 system and that the capacity of directive command systems to control the force in complex circumstances may have been reached.  We suggest that it may be more relevant in complex circumstances to concentrate less on control of the force and more on maintaining purposefulness among diverse networks of actors and actions that can be more responsive to localised needs and factors.

Title: SPRITE: A Plan Template Learning Framework
Authors: Gervasio, M.; Myers, K.; Tam, L.; Szatkowski, C.
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Operational planning remains a time-consuming process, with much effort devoted to documenting routine activities despite the availability of increasingly powerful support tools. In particular, similar tasks occur in many plans, often differing only in the resources they use or their time or location. SPRITE (Specialized Plan Recommendation through Intelligent Template Extraction) is a template learning  framework  for  managing  the  creation,  use,  and  adaptation  of  plan  templates  within  planning  tools.  Through  SPRITE, planners can build a library of reusable templates from examples that serve as building blocks for creating new plans. We discuss SPRITE's application to AMPT ( Advanced Multi-echelon Planning Tool), a state-of-the-art, interactive Navy planning environment. Utilizing  AMPT's  machine -readable,  structured  plan  representation,  SPRITE  provides  the  ability  to  learn  plan  templates  by generalizing observed values and relations in example plans along multiple dimensions. SPRITE is designed to combine analytic learning to enable learning in small data settings with statistical learning to benefit from more voluminous usage data over time. We present the generalization algorithms that enable template learning, along with theoretical and empirical analyses of the associated costs and benefits. SPRITE's template management functionality and user experience were developed and refined with feedback gathered from a series of user engagement sessions, which we also discuss in this paper. SPRITE enables better utilization of modern operational planning support tools, allowing planners to focus on more cognitively demanding tasks, fostering expertise sharing and adoption of best practices, and more fully realizing the potential benefits of advanced technology for command and control.

Title: Decision Support for Commanders: An AI Approach to Wargaming
Authors: Abdelazez, M.; Garber, R.; Ghanmi, A.
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Effective Command and Control (C2) requires the commander to make informed decisions quickly to achieve the mission objectives. The commander would have to explore the possible courses of action (COAs) by analyzing their potential outcomes, risks, and required resources, an involved analysis that is further complicated by the pressure of making a timely response. Using wargaming, the possible COAs could be pre-emptively explored and taught to commanders before deployment. Wargaming is a broad term that could be used in multiple contexts; however, for the sake of this paper, it will be defined as warfare simulation. The utility of wargaming has been shown throughout history, such as the development of AirLand Battle, the principal US warfighting doctrine of the latter stages of the Cold War, by US General Donn Starry. However, as multi-domain operations complicate the battle space, setting up a wargame became a resource and time-intensive activity. Artificial Intelligence (AI), the ability of machines to imitate human cognitive skills, can potentially simplify the setup and operation of wargames. While staff have long used wargaming to develop and evaluate COAs for the commander, AI promises to enhance and accelerate this evaluation process, mainly through running multiple iterations in ways that manual wargaming could not provide. Additionally, AI has been shown to generate new tactics, which humans have not previously used, in strategy games such as chess, Go, and StarCraft, indicating that AI could generate potential COAs unknown to commanders and military strategists. This paper provides an overview of AI in wargaming, the potential benefits and challenges of using AI in wargaming, and the various publicly available national perspectives on AI for wargaming.

Title: Multi-Domain Targeting through Battle Rhythm Modelling for Superior Decision Making
Authors: Adams, T.; Bennett, M.; Francis, M.; McLennan-Smith, T.; Davis, J.
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: One of  the  key  functions  of  a  military  headquarters  is  to  undertake  'targeting'  which  invo lves  the  planning,  coordination  and application of effects against legal targets to achieve operational objectives. This targeting process is governed by decisions made through a headquarters' battle rhythm, which refers to the Boards, Bureaus, Centres, Cells and Working Groups (B2C2WGs). Beyond decision-making, these battle rhythm events harmonize headquarters' staff and subordinate entities while also synchronizing the multi-domain nature of an operational headquarters. We extend our previous work in battle rhythm optimization by introducing a multi-objective framework to quantify and evaluate the knowledge flow through sub-processes -specifically the targeting process. In this mathematical model of a headquarters' battle rhythm, B2C2 WGs generate and pass ' knowledge '  to subsequent B2C2WGs where targets are prosecuted by a central decision-making authority subject to sufficient knowledge being generated. In this paper, we utilize publicly available targeting doctrine from the North Atlantic Treaty Organization and the Australian Defence Force to develop hypothetical battle rhythms that adhere to processes such as Find, Fix, Track, Target, Engage, Exploit, and Assess. We then conduct numerical experiments where we measure the quantitative metrics of throughput, bandwidth, and knowledge utilization to establish the limits of decision-making cycles and identify the optimal timing of B2C2WGs. Finally, we compare the optimal battle rhythm from a knowledge flow perspective with previous iterations of battle rhythm optimization.

Title: King of the Hill: C2 for Next Generation Swarm Warfare
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: As the reliability of cheap, off-the-shelf autonomous platforms increases, so does the risk posed by intelligent multi-agent systems to military operations. In the contemporary context of the Russo-Ukrainian war alone, we have seen autonomous aerial vehicles and surface vessels deployed both individually and in multitude to deliver critical effects to both sides. While there is a large body of literature on tactical level communications and interactions between agents, the exploration of high-level command and control (C2) structures that will underpin future autonomous multi-agent military operations is a less explored area of research. We propose a quantitative  game-theoretic  framework  to  study  effective  C2  structures  in  cooperative  and  competitive  multi-agent  swarming scenarios. To test our framework, we construct a virtual environment where two adversarial swarms compete to achieve outcomes comparable to real-world scenarios. The framework we present in this paper enables us to quickly test and interrogate different C2 configurations in multi-agent systems to explore C2 as a force multiplier when at a force disadvantage.

Title: Navigating in the zoo
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Machine learning (ML) and artificial intelligence (AI) are taking the world by storm. Armed forces around the world are thinking about new concepts or adaptations to integrate ML and AI in different functions affecting the military work environment. The purpose of this article is to broaden the perspective and challenge the perception of a military ML or AI integration. Military exercises are important tools for armed forces throughout the world. Learning to fight wars in a safe environment which emphasizes learning, saves lives on the battlefield. Through the theoretical framework of human activity, it is possible to understand the challenges soldiers  and  officers  face  when  conducting  double-sided  live  field  exercises.  The  introduction  of  ML  and  AI  in  the  exercise environment also creates a new learning environment with two more learning participants. By understanding the human zone of proximal development, we can also better understand the learning limitations and constraints an integrated ML or AI must consider before adjusting the algorithm. Simply accepting every collected data stream from a double-sided live field exercise might lead to learned faults and errors endangering the lives of soldiers and officers. To mediate the risk a new type of exercise needs to be developed with a focus on all participants learning opportunities both human and machine.

Title: Censeo: A Scenario-based Approach to C2 Capability Assessment and Experimentation
Authors: Krisler, B.; Myers, J.; Rock, C.
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The  process  of  identifying  opportunities  for  and  incorporating  new  functionality  into  Command  and  Control  (C2)  is  often  time consuming and expensive. In this paper we introduce our research on a methodology we call Censeo inspired from DevOps software delivery. Developed as part of the AFRL Flyleaf program, Censeo is designed for adopting new technologies into critical infrastructures by providing assessment capabilities to software developers earlier in the development cycle. The Censeo methodology uses process and data models to scope and define operational scenarios. These models are then used to drive the development, assessment and evaluation of new functionality without compromising the integrity of critical infrastructures.

Title: Organizational Structure and Consensus Emergence of Team Trust Perceptions within Command and Control (C2) Systems
Authors: Chapman, M. T.; Corsin, J.; Ye, M.; Gagne, M.; Riddell, H.; Wilson, M.; Brade, C.; Jorritsma, K.; Gucciardi, D. F.; McTernan, W.; Strickland, L.; Griffin, M.; Gedeon, T.
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract:
Background .  Adaptive and shared perceptions of team trust are necessary for successful performance within command and control (C2) systems. Team trust perceptions are often dynamic, with salient determinants situated across multiple layers of organisational structures (e.g., leadership), yet the knowledge base is constructed primarily from cross-sectional snap shots and single level approaches. We aim to contribute new knowledge on team trust dynamics via an examination of the emergence of shared perceptions of trust and factors that might augment or undermine increased similarity among team member perceptions over time. Recent methodological advancements afford opportunities to model team trust consensus emergence within a multilevel framework and explore the role of characteristics that may predict this dynamic process.

Aim. Within the context of newly formed C2 teams working on a computer simulation in a laboratory setting, we estimated the overall magnitude of consensus emergence in team trust perceptions, and the average between-group effect of C2 structure upon consensus emergence perceptions.

Methods .  Design:  We  implemented  a  randomised  laboratory  experimental  design . Participants  &  Procedure:  155 participants  across  thirty-one  5-person  teams  (74  Male,  79  Female,  2  Transgender,  MAge=24.52±6.31)  were  randomly assigned to either an edge or classic C2 structure condition and then completed three 15-minute fire-fighting simulations using  the  C3Fire  software.  C2  structure  was  operationalised  via  the manipulation  of  communication  channels  and distribution of graphical information within the software environment. Team trust was measured via self-report at four points  during  the  experiment;  prior  to  the  first  simulation  task  and  thereafter  upon  completion  of  each  of  the  three simulations. Data Analysis: We utilised consensus emergence models via a 3-level mixed-effects model, with simulation tasks (Level 1) nested within individuals (Level 2) who are nested within teams (Level 3). All models were fit using the nlme package in R.

Title: Network Flow Interdiction for Graph-based Course of Action Analysis
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: In land-based operations, assessing the terrain is critical for the success of a mission. While well-established representations -maps, tactical graphics and overlays -help operators to make sense of the situation, they are not ideal for automatic analyses. To enable the  application  of  artificial  intelligence  methods,  Cawalla  and  Schmitz  [1]  have  introduced  the  concept  of  a  graph-based representation of action spaces. Based on mobility corridors, critical points, and points of interest, a graph is constructed. This graph encodes  important  aspects  of  the  terrain  in  its  nodes  and  edges.  In  this  paper,  we  will  further  elaborate  on  the  concept  and demonstrate automated analyses using network flow and network interdiction approaches. The former determines optimal courses of action (CoAs) of one side without considering the other, the latter calculates the most dangerous red CoAs and blue means of intervention, depending on each other. Finally, we will discuss the applicability of reinforcement learning for developing successful policies within such graphs.

Title: Coalface Perspectives on C2 Capability Governance: An Interview Study
Authors: Wikberg, P.; Herkevall, J.; Gaertner, P.; Dekker, R.; Bryant, D.; Jobidon, M.-E.; Granåsen, M.; Cooke, R.; Kalloniatis, A.
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Command and Control (C2) remains a critical capability for NATO and needs to transform in response to the current and future challenges of the security environment. Over the years, many C2 transformation programs have been initiated and, while it is relatively easy to find references from the early phases of these programs, evidence of actual transformation having  taken  place  is  rare.  The  NATO  HFM  RTG-342  'C2  Capability  Lifecycle  Governance'  is  formed  to  address  this observation. A part of this work includes interviews with individuals from various entities that are involved in development or maintenance of C2 Capability. The study aimed to explore experiences and collect insights related to C2 governance and the processes required to transform current C2 practices to meet future requirements. This paper summarizes and analyzes the interviewees' opinions and experiences regarding four broad themes: the future security environment, C2 capability,  challenges  for  ensuring  C2  capability,  and  the  way  forward  towards  future  C2  capability.  The  interviews represent the case of the current rearrangement of the NATO C2 structure as a response to the conflict in Ukraine and also the planned inclusion of new NATO members. The  discussion includes topics regarding potential solutions such as the importance of addressing both technology and the human perspective and that commander skills include both the art of war and the trade of governing C2 capability. In order to accomplish change, simultaneous activities need to be conducted across all time horizons, where change is a process of negotiation. Finally there is need for operationalizing concepts early within a vision of possible futures.

Title: Autonomous information processing at the tactical level
Authors: Langleite, R.; Andersen, E. P.; Opland, A. F.; Johnsen, F. T.
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: In this paper, we address the challenge of autonomous processing at the tactical level. With an increase in available communications and sensor capabilities, we need digital approaches to automate data gathering, processing, and information exploitation. We contend that the future of C2 will rely heavily on leveraging artificial intelligence and that this technology will be crucial to all aspects of military C2. In our work, we address the challenge of supporting autonomous processing of big data on tactical nodes. Our main motivation behind the work is to investigate approaches to realize automated processing, and the impact this will have on future tactical information technology infrastructure needs. The paper presents an architectural approach, the free/libre open-source software we use to realize this architecture, as well as the use case we're currently pursuing for leveraging the implementation. In this paper, we discuss crowd-sourced radio spectrum detection and classification as our use case. However, we contend that this approach can be used for a variety of data, though domain specific knowledge will be needed to implement the automated reasoning functionality.

Title: Using C2 Problem Space to Integrate Cyber and Kinetic Operations
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract:
Ever since the military used the telegraph in the American Civil War, Command & Control (C2) has taken place through cyberspace. When the USA declared cyberspace as the fifth domain of warfare in 2011, this formally recognised that operations can also take place within cyberspace. This recognition brought with it the need to integrate cyber operations with operations in the four physical domains of land, sea, air, and space. However, there are major differences between the domains. Land, sea, air, and space are natural domains containing physical entities located in a continuous, three-dimensional space, while cyberspace is a man-made domain containing inertia-less entities that can move close to the speed of light through a discrete, multi-dimensional space. A physical entity can be in only one location at a time, but cyber entities can be in multiple locations at once. Because physical entities have inertia, their operations, actions, and effects in physical domains are termed kinetic .

Despite these differences, cyber entities can only exist within physical entities, typically a computer system or a communications medium. Moreover, cyber entities may also represent physical entities, as when a C2 system represents a tank on the battlefield by data describing the type of tank, its location, and whether it is friendly, hostile, or neutral. Both cyber and kinetic operations can be represented in C2 problem space, in which the characteristics of the operating environment are plotted in multiple dimensions. The five domains occupy different places in this space, complicating their integration in all-domain operations.

This paper focuses on the issues involved in integrating offensive operations in cyberspace with kinetic operations, viewed from the perspectives of theory, experience, and doctrine. Relevant theory covers layered conceptualisation of cyberspace, the cyber kill chain, and all-domain operations. Information in the public domain about operational experience is scarce. Case studies from past operations combining cyber and kinetic action suggest that cyber action may be best suited to deception and kinetic action to destruction. A recent paper from practitioners emphasises the important of gaining access to the target. Doctrine will be drawn from US DoD publications in the public domain, covering information operations, cyberspace operations, and targeting. Mapping the findings onto C2 problem space is a step towards finding the appropriate C2 approach(es) for integrated cyber-kinetic operations.

Title: Dynamic Effects of Social Media Information on Command and Control Performance
Authors: Hu, X.; Bui, L.; Park, G.; Sweetser, P.; McTernan, W.; Stanley, S.; Newman, E.; Caldwell, S.
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The success of military and civilian Command and Control (C2) depends on the ability of C2 entities to adapt to dynamic changes in situations. With increased exposure to the public and social media, it is critical to understand the way C2 teams use social media information to increase their situation awareness (SA) and team performance (TP). This means that we require a new understanding of the structure of distribution of information in C2. This study used a simulated firefighting task and integrated information (social media) into a tactical (firefighting team) response to investigate the effects of high (team members receive all social media information) or low (each team member only receives social media information specifically relevant to their role) distribution of information on team SA and TP. We recruited 150 participants (30 five-member teams of four tactical team members and one information officer) to participate in three rounds of firefighting scenarios in C3Fire. The results from 22 teams indicate that distributing social media information to all tactical team members improves team SA and TP in general, though these effects were not consistently found. We discussed the implications for C2 agility in managing contingent military and civilian mission in the Information Age.

Title: The Concept of Non-Human Intelligent Cooperation with No Inter-Communication: Goore Game for Centralized Command and Distributed Control of Sensors
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Is it possible to obtain cooperation between agents with no communication among them and without knowledge of the central authority's  goal?  Goore  Game,  introduced  by  M.  L.  Tsetlin,  proposes  an  answer  to  this  question;  the  game  has  the fascinating property that it can be solved in a completely distributed manner with no inter-communication between the players.  Motivated by the current interest in integrating Artificial Intelligence technologies into Command and Control capabilities across All-Domains to exploit AI-enhanced remote sensors, intelligence assets, and open source to sense and integrate information so that the decision cycle operates faster relative to adversary abilities, our intention is to explore potential implementations of the Goore Game for power  control,  uniform  coverage  in  sensor  networks,  uniform  energy  expenditure  in  sensor  networks,  redeployment  of  scarce resources, dynamic configuration of swarms, and multi-access communications.  Command and Control is essential for achieving mission success, but it can also hinder the cooperation if it is too rigid or hierarchical. In our research setting involving sensors, effective cooperation requires a certain degree of autonomy and flexibility.  The findings obtained from the world of sensors can be transferred to humans' Command and Control in the future. Our exploratory work uses implementations with Learning Automata, modelled with Linear Reward-Inaction scheme.

Title: MURA Snapshot Survey: Validation of Culture and C2 Agility Scales
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Under Plan MURA, Australia’s Headquarters Joint Operations Command (HQJOC) is undergoing a series of modernisation enhancements to ready itself for the challenges of tomorrow. To support this modernisation, an annual headquarters survey was developed: The MURA Snapshot. In this paper we conducted Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA) on two new multi-item measures implemented within the 2022 MURA Snapshot survey: a four item measure of Culture and seven item measure of Command and Control (C2) Agility. We found good fit indices for a reduced three item measure of Culture, and for the full seven item measure of C2 Agility. We believe this is the first published and validated measure of C2 Agility, and therefore offers a useful and rigorous tool for the C2 community. We conclude the paper by providing a discussion of possible directions for future research including approaches for making improvements to each scale.

Title: Endeavor complexity across command levels: Defining and communicating attributes of an endeavor
Authors: Sigmund Valaker ; Else Helene Feet ; Rune Stensrud
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: What are the essential elements in the threat environment? In addition: How can they be communicated across strategic, operational and tactical levels? These two related questions are of importance practically, theoretically and methodically. The development of novel military capabilities, e.g. effectors, should ideally answer needs identified in strategic scenario construction.  In practice, however, a drift may occur between the command levels if the link is lost between strategic, operational and tactical levels i.e. “disconnect” that may leads to dysfunction in applying resources on a military problem. For example, the necessary resources, e.g. expertise put into developing certain capabilities, may not be provided if there are different understanding of scenarios across levels. A long tradition of C2 research have focused on leading and coordinating military activities (e.g. operations and missions) and described in detail the control dilemma that a higher level (e.g. strategic) may have in adapting to feedback from lower levels (e.g. operational and tactical) (Johansson, Carlerby & Alberts, 2018). However, the C2 research to date have little to contribute to the specific needs that long-term and mid-term military capability development is facing in horizons beyond operations and missions (McKelvey, B., & Boisot, M., 2009). In this paper, we suggest that the control dilemma may be reduced in military capability development by defining common environmental attributes across levels. We draw on the elements of endeavor complexity as suggested by Johansson et al. (2018): who proposed Dynamics, Dependencies, Interactions for the Endeavor Space dimensions. These attributes create a set of dependencies that need to be successfully managed to achieve the objectives of a Complex Endeavor established by participating entities (SAS-143, 2023, p.44). To further increase relevance, we add the attributes related to enemy capabilities, intent, courses of action as well as the elements of our (blue side) intent, desired effects could be valuable as common attributes, more specifically availability and composition of forces (blue side, red side), update times, technology level, need for tactical coordination, and geography. We argue that the higher granularity of our suggested attributes is needed to be able to communicate relevant scenario and vignette information, and deviations, across command levels. Essentially care should be taken of the boundaries between environment and the organization, boundaries that are complex due to the hierarchical structure of most military organizations. We illustrate our reasoning through a capability development case. We sketch some trajectories such development may take given discrepancies in understanding of the crucial scenarios and vignettes between levels. We suggest ways such discrepancies may be reduced with certain scenario and vignette attributes. Finally, some directions for further research is suggested as well as practical implications and advice.

Title: Hybrid Cognitive Capabilities in EDGE Operations
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract:
Contemporary and future defense and security operations are increasingly time-critical, resource-critical and safety-critical, requiring vigilance, awareness and determination. Commanders and operators frequently encounter uncertainty, risk, timecriticality and resource shortages while mastering the challenges of distributed, complex systems, strong functional coupling and interdependencies. Operational characteristics are highly dynamic and non-linear; Minor events, decisions and actions may have serious and irreversible consequences for the entire mission. This requires adaptive and versatile principles and concepts for Emergent, Dynamic, Global and Evolutionary operations (EDGE) operations, along with high-performance human and technological (i.e. hybrid) cognitive capabilities.

Additionally, operating in a hazardous operational environment requires comprehensive operational awareness - shaped, supported and utilized by human and machine agents, in joint capabilities. Joint human-machine agents constitute hybrid capability components, able to accurately and rapidly sense, perceive and interpret relevant events and circumstances in order to sustain and improve decision-making and action, enabling every commander and operator to develop a wide-ranging appreciation of the situation.

We propose a future-oriented Essence of Multi-Domain Cognitive Command, comprising strategic capability elements with equal relevance and applicability on human and autonomous agents.

Title: Virtual Testbed for Cost-Effective Evaluation of C2 Systems: Practical Examples from the Swedish Armed Forces
Authors: Albinsson, P.-A.; Granåsen, D.; Hermelin, J.; Thorstensson, M.
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: We present a testbed for recreating and analyzing realistic C2 scenarios. The testbed enables the examination of actual C2 system components in large constellations using virtual machines. We give  examples of practical insights from its use on evaluating C2 products used in the Swedish Armed Forces. The testbed is built upon a cyber range platform, configurable through user interfaces, where C2 system installations and network topologies are defined and automatically instantiated. The testbed enables rapid scenario development for repeated and revised simulations using realistic connectivity scenarios and environmental constraints.  Dynamic scenarios with timed events represent real-world actions such as changes in network quality, geographical movement of units and system operator interaction. With the seamless automation, from mass-instantiation of virtual machines to simulation and data collection,  repeated  testing  can  identify  and  resolve  system  issues,  as  well  as  reveal  the  system-wide  effects  of  tweaking  the C2 system's parameters. To exemplify, we present insights from three studies where we used the testbed for understanding system parameters, gauging system upgrade implications, and recreating field trial phenomena. The testbed presented has proven to be a valuable  tool  for  the  Swedish  Armed  Forces,  enabling  cost-effective  evaluations  and  optimizations  of  deployed  systems  and assessments of alternatives. We believe that the insights from developing and using this testbed show that armed forces in general can demand that defense contractors ensure similar capabilities to evaluate the full scope of the systems procured.

Title: Command and Control of Cyberspace during Multidomain Operations (MDO)
Authors: Teske, K. D.; Miller, M. E.
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract:
This paper on Command and Control of Cyberspace during Multidomain Operations (MDO) is devoted to a discussion of how both the need to operate in a Contested Cyber Environment and the need to conduct a variety of Cyberspace Operations affect the appropriateness of C2-H Arrangements and the relationship between these arrangements and mission success. Contested Cyber Environment is routinely observed in Regions B, D, and F of the Endeavors Space, are the most dynamic and require the greatest amount of communication and collaboration. Because one operates in a Contested Cyber Environment due to one's adversaries now routinely employ cyberattacks across the 'Competition Continuum' (to prepare the battlespace for subsequent attacks, as means unto themselves below armed conflict, and in coordination with attacks that can be classified as armed conflict).

Humans operating in a Contested Cyber Environment can be enhanced with the help of intelligent systems such as a Non-Human Intelligent Collaborators (NICs).  The appropriate accesses to information and the delegation of necessary decision rights to NICs which will provide them some degree of autonomy to take the actions needed for Defense Cyber Operations (DCO) enabling mission accomplished in a timely manner.

Entities conducting CSO in MDO must maintain cross-domain relationships with all other entities involved in a mission to better identify and understand the risks to missions that are generated while operating in a contested cyber environment.  Whether in a supporting or supported role, entities must be able to manage the risks they present to other entities while mitigating the risk that the other entities present to them.

Title: Constructing a Game of Games to Expedite Command and Control Capability Development
Authors: Veldhuizen, C.; Czajkowski, M.; Guo, J. K.; Widjaja, M.
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Command and Control (C2) decisions that anticipate intelligent, potentially deceptive opponent actions may benefit from Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) capabilities. Game theory has gained momentum in applications such as all domain Course of Action (COA) recommendation. However, the high complexity of C2 operational domains and the increasingly inter-domain nature of decision making present major challenges to the application of ML algorithms to COA selection. Moreover, existing AI testbeds do not adequately support assessing how decisions made in one domain impact outcomes present in another over long time horizons. AI-driven solutions to complex game-theoretic COA recommendation have been hindered by this inability to rapidly iterate on models that handle domain interdependencies. In this paper, we present a generic framework called Game of Games (GoG) that lowers the barrier to prototyping RL for evaluating game mechanics and understanding cooperative play. We assemble game solvers, game environments in an ecosystem to train strategies among multiple collaborative and adversarial players. GoG orchestrates communication between any number of domain modeling environments and unspecialized Reinforcement Learning (RL) algorithms which model -and seek to optimize the actions of -agents in those environments. In this way, we make tractable the problem of long-term, interdependent outcomes in complex environments by allowing the solution-seeking process to be 1.) decomposed into a set of smaller, less challenging subdomains, 2.) organized into a hierarchical structure by making explicit the dependencies between those domains, and 3.) assessed for long-term consequences by training RL algorithms on those consequences over many generations.

Title: Methodology for Assessment of C2 systems - A literature review
Authors: Oskarsson, P. A.; Stenius, C.; Melbi, A.
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Command and Control (C2) systems are central for efficient military operations. In order to ensure adequate C2 capability, several capabilities are needed. Making sure that these capabilities are achieved requires procedures for systematic C2 assessments. In order to assist such procedures, we have initiated research to define a framework to support assessment of C2 systems. Focus for the assessment is the sociotechnical C2 system, which means that assessments should not be restricted to technical C2 systems. This paper presents initial research in the form of a literature review with the objective to map assessment methods, described in the scientific literature, with applicability for assessment of C2 systems. Sixty-three methods with potential for assessment of C2 systems were identified. The main result of this work was a categorization of assessment methods into 14 focus areas for C2 assessment. This can be used as a foundation for a framework for C2 assessment methodology.

Title: Applying a Mission-Engineering Thread Approach Using Sense-Decide-Effect to the Problem of a Common C2 System for UxS
Authors: O'Neill, J.; McDonald, J.; Heaton, B.; Thotahewa, K.; Augur, C.; Brown, K.
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The 2023 Defence Strategic Review reinforced that the Australian Defence Force (ADF) will fight as an 'integrated force'. Current uncrewed systems (UxS) solutions tend to operate with bespoke, tightly coupled C2 systems, making it difficult to contribute to force-level behaviours and effects and operate as an integrated force. The Sense-Decide-Effect construct is used to reason at the logical level about the different requirements and approaches for an UxS Common C2 system in order to inform the development of an UxS Common C2 Reference Architecture. This paper steps through how the Sense-Decide-Effect activity model enables scoping of the problem domain, supports the generation of UxS Common C2 configuration options, and the development of UxS Common C2 Use Cases that detail for projects which aspects of the UxS Common C2 Reference Architecture they need to conform with. This is the third in a series of ICCRTS papers that have developed the Sense-Decide-Effect mission engineering approach. Based on this work and the development of the knowledge acquisition information exchange approach, we make four key findings for the C2 research community for the C2 Approach Space and C2 Endeavour Space.

Title: Cross Layer Optimization in Tactical Networks: Multi-agent Deep Reinforcement Learning
Authors: Möhlenhof, T.; Jansen, N.; Rachid, W.
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Providing situational awareness is a crucial requirement and a challenging task in the tactical domain. Tactical networks can be characterized as Disconnected, Intermittent and Limited (DIL) networks. The use of Cross-Layer approaches in DIL networks can help to better utilize the tactical communications resources and thus improve the overall situational awareness perceived by the user. The specification of suitable Cross-Layer strategies (heuristics) which describe the rules for optimizing the applications remains a challenging task. In this paper, we present a high-level architecture for a learning environment aimed at training decentralized MultiAgent Deep Reinforcement Learning (MARL) agents. These are designed to improve the efficient and fair use of network resources in DIL networks by exploiting cross-layer information. This approach shall lead to more sophisticated strategies which are learned autonomously  by  the  agents.  Furthermore,  we  present  a  reinforcement  learning  architecture  with  MARL  agents  placed  in  an  emulated radio environment focused on optimizing the traffic of a distributed Blue-Force-Tracking (BFT) service in a cross-layer fashion. The agents (re-)configure the instances of the BFT service to enhance and to reach the best possible Quality of Experience (QoE) from a user's perspective dependent on the current network state in a collaborative manner. Therefor e, we employed and adapted Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) [1] in combination with Graph Neural Networks (GNN) [2] to solve the underlying cooperative multi-agent communication network problem. Furthermore, we evaluated our approach on a set of different common crucial network states to ensure the usability and describe future work regarding a deeper evaluation with more realistic and challenging scenarios.

Title: Acquisition Management as Mission Command - C2 Systems Acquisition Reimagined
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Acquisition of technical command and control systems (C2 systems) for armed forces is a challenging task. Projects responsible for acquiring such systems often fail to deliver on time, on budget and with a satisfactory outcome for the users. In order to improve effectiveness  and  efficiency  during  the  acquisition  process,  a  study  was  carried  out  in  two  parts.  First,  a  literature  study  was conducted, aiming at identifying success factors when acquiring C2 systems. Second, interviews were carried out with 13 people who worked with acquisition for the Swedish Armed Forces to test whether the identified success factors could be applied in a military context. The results from the study show that (1) acquisition of technical C2 systems should be treated like a complex and wicked problem, (2) a healthy organisational culture with trust-based management needs to be developed, (3) clear goals for the acquisition need to be defined when acquiring C2 systems, (4) uncertainties need to be respected when planning the acquisition project, (5) close collaboration with stakeholders should be developed, (6) it should be ensured that the project team possesses the required competence over time, (7) follow-ups should focus on factors relevant for the success of the acquisition, and (8) an ability to respond to changes should be developed. The outcome of the study shows great similarities with mission command and thus, increased effectiveness and efficiency should be achieved through acquisition management based on mission command rather than prescribed processes.

Title: Bridging the Gap between Humans and AI in Military Planning: Officers' Response to a Text-Based AI Assistant
Authors: Lindholm, V.; Alenljung, Z.; Karlsson, J.
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: With recent technological breakthroughs in natural language processing tools, such as the launch of OpenAI's ChatGPT, new possibilities have emerged. Initial studies have shown an increase in productivity for written tasks when using generative AI. For C2-systems, text-based generative AI can be beneficial to organizations by streamlining routine work. The proposed study described in this paper will evaluate the concept of using a textbased AI assistant when following a standardized planning method. The aim of the study is to evaluate how interaction with an AI assistant affects officers' performance, e.g. efficiency, productivity and workload, compared to the current method where several officers work in small teams. The study will follow a withinsubject design, where the participating officers are divided into teams that are assigned tasks to be solved using a Swedish Armed Forces standardized planning method. For each task, one team will use an AI assistant, while the other teams will solve the task following the standardized planning method without AI assistance.

Title: A Future C2 Concept Designed to Guide Capability Development
Authors: Magdalena Granåsen; Niklas Hallberg;  Anders Josefsson; Johan Ivari
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The society, and consequently the security environment, is in constant change, characterized by increased interconnectedness, rapid technological development and competition. Advances are made within the air, sea and land domains at the same time as the space and cyber domains are increasingly contested. Command and Control (C2) needs to enable and facilitate the effective and efficient accomplishment  of  assigned  missions  or  tasks  and  respond  to  unanticipated  adversary  strategies.  Military  C2  needs  to  utilize available resources including technology and individuals' competencies and creativity. The objective of the study presented in this paper is a concept that can guide the development of future military C2. The concept was developed iteratively in concurrent activities: (1) review of seminal C2 literature, (2) exploration of future trends and concept initiatives, (3) C2 needs analysis, (4) C2 requirements analysis, and (5) concept design. The resulting concept prescribes effect-focused, agile and resilient C2, providing the conditions for acting solitarily and with partners, to meet the future challenges of military operations. The concept includes eight guiding principles that ought to be considered in development of C2 systems. The C2 concept is expected to augment the transformation of military C2 to correspond to future demands in the security environment. During the concept design activity, the C2 concept was presented to individuals involved in C2 development as well as operations. Feedback from these occasions gives at hand that the C2 concept provides a solid framework for exploration of future C2. Additional validation of the concept has been performed through development of scenarios describing future C2 situations. These scenarios specify how the concept can be manifested. The concept has further been used to develop an analytic framework for exploring the impact of future C2 technology on C2 capability.
Author Keywords: FUTURE COMMAND AND CONTROL (C2) CONCEPT; MULTI-DOMAIN OPERATIONS (MDO); AGILE AND RESILIENT C2; INTEROPERABILITY AND FEDERATED MISSION NETWORKING (FMN); MISSION COMMAND PHILOSOPHY; SOCIOTECHNICAL SYSTEMS ENGINEERING; REQUIREMENTS ENGINEERING FOR C2; AI-ENABLED DECISION SUPPORT IN C2

Title: Variety versus speed: how variety in competence within teams may affect performance in a dynamic decision-making task
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Command and control (C2) activities are conducted in various domains such as defence, emergency response, police and crises management. The problems in these domains are often characterized by complexity, i.e. having a high degree of variety. According to  cybernetic  theory,  the  variety  of  the  controller  (the  C2  system)  must  equal  or  exceed  that  of  the  controlled  system  in  the operational environment. The degree of variety sufficient to control a particular system is defined as requisite variety. In this paper we aim to operationalize both external and internal variety, closing the gap between high level abstract descriptions and concrete solutions, in order to suggest practical propositions when designing C2 systems. C2 systems are composed by methods, technology, personnel and organization. In this work we focus on the aspects of personnel and organization. A particular interest is dedicated to the competence variable within the personnel component. We discuss, based on previous research on e.g. diversity, what dimensions of the competence variable may be of most importance when performing C2 activities to cope with complexity. However, a substantial amount of C2 research, also suggests that making fast decisions is important to cope with problems stemming from an adversary. We elaborate on the potential cost of high internal variety in that it may delay decisions in the C2 team because of a raised need for team communication. We conclude by presenting an investigative method, which includes simulated external complexity requiring dynamic decision-making that is handled by C2 teams with different types of competence (internal variety).

Title: A New Perspective on the C2 Maturity Model Approach Space: Can Evolving Complex Situations Produce an Inverted U Trajectory in the C2 Approach Space?
Authors: Jobidon, M.; Farrell, P. S. E.
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: In this paper, we propose a new perspective on the C2 Maturity Model and the relationship between endeavour complexity and C2 Approach. It is recognized by various allied countries that the Future Security Environment (FSE) will be characterized by continuous competition  and  conflict  in  complex,  volatile,  and  unfamiliar  environments,  increasingly  contested  cyber  and  information ecosystems, with many varied (state and non-state) well-equipped and creative adversaries (see, e.g., Jobidon et al., 2022, for a review). Arguably, the future is already here. One critical requirement to deal with the FSE will be Command and Control (C2) agility. The C2 Maturity Model (SAS-065, 2010) describes a C2 Approach Space that includes five different C2 approaches, from conflicted to edge, that can be adopted to match the complexity of the endeavour or situation. Work conducted under SAS-065 and investigated further in SAS-085 (2014) propose that as the endeavour becomes more complex, an entity should move toward less constrained, more net-enabled  collaborative  and  edge  approaches.  The  relationship  is  assumed  to  be  a  direct  relationship.  But  what  if  the relationship between situation complexity and C2 approach was not a linear phenomenon? What if, at some point, complexity reached a level where deconflicted or coordinated approaches were a better, most efficient way to manage the situation? There are phenomena  in  psychology  (e.g.,  the  relationship  between  stress  or  motivation  and  performance),  among  other  domains,  that manifest in an inverted U shape. We hypothesize that a similar curve could apply to the C2 Approach Space with regards to endeavour complexity, where beyond a certain threshold more constrained C2 approaches are better suited. Drawing on relevant phenomena, we  will  discuss  this  new  perspective  on  the  relationship  between  situation  complexity  and  the  C2  Approach  Space,  and  the implications it may have on selecting and transitioning between C2 approaches. Considering the significant resources, in all forms, entailed both in functioning in a less constrained C2 approach and in transitioning C2 approaches, using these resources efficiently by adopting the most appropriate C2 approach to face a situation is key.
Author Keywords: COMMAND AND CONTROL (C2) MATURITY MODEL; C2 AGILITY AND MANOEUVRE; INVERTED U-SHAPED RELATIONSHIP IN COMPLEX SYSTEMS; SITUATION COMPLEXITY AND C2 APPROACH SELECTION; MULTI-DOMAIN OPERATIONS (MDO); NONLINEAR DYNAMICS AND CONTROL THEORY IN C2; DISASTER RESPONSE CASE STUDIES; NETWORK-ENABLED C2 APPROACHES

Title: Exploring the Effects of Future Technologies on C2
Authors: Granåsen, M.; Herkevall, J.; Tolt, G.; Johansson, B.
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The  global  security  environment  is  characterized  by  rapid  technological  development  and  increasing  interconnectedness. Technological advancements drive innovative business and management solutions, and the dominating view is that military C2 needs a  similar  evolution  through the  exploitation of emerging technologies.  Defence research organisations try to forecast how new technologies within areas such as sensor technology, communications and machine learning will transform military C2 capability in terms of quality and speed of decision making, adaptability and Headquarters design - potentially revolutionizing how future military operations are conducted. The present study aims to contribute in this effort. As part of a long-term study plan inspired by the NATO Concept Development & Experimentation methodology,  a  game-based  study  was  conducted  to  explore  the implications  of technological innovations on future C2 capability. A set of technology cards were developed and used during workshop sessions, where  experienced  military  personnel  discussed  implications  for  future  C2  capability  from  methodological,  doctrinal,  and organizational perspectives. To focus and facilitate discussions, an analytical framework was developed based on the Command and Control Concept for the Swedish Armed Forces analysing the time-horizon to the year 2045. Findings imply that the technology cards effectively inspire discussion and give insights regarding possibilities, related concerns and willingness to implement new technology. The results of the study provide input to a roadmap for future C2 capability development for the Swedish Armed Forces. Furthermore, the results provide a basis for more advanced scenario-based wargaming approaches as a tool for technology prioritization, concept development and capability development.

Title: A Layered Architecture for Adaptive Autonomy in Human-Robotic Team Interaction
Authors: Allison, M.; Gaspard, A.; Parks, M.; Jefferson, F.
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Shared autonomy has become increasingly relevant in robotic applications operating within environments with implicit uncertainty. In this cooperative model, a human and a robotic team collaborate towards a shared objective. The respective roles and contributions are dictated by their capabilities with respect to activities required to complete the mission. This paper reviews the distinguishing characteristics of these two team elements to derive a layered framework for shared autonomy. The framework proposed supports a separation of concerns at strategic, tactical, and operational levels. We assess the framework based on scaling up the number of targets within a search scenario for full autonomy, and contrast the performance against executing tasks upon human approval. While we found significant runtime latency introduced by the difference in decision times for humans and robots, it should be considered that the type (highlevel versus low-level) and quality (correctness) of the decision is a significant factor that demands further scrutiny.

Title: A Risk-Aware Leadership Structure for Heterogeneous Robotic Teams
Authors: Allison, M.; Parks, M.; Duncan, J.
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Heterogeneous robots collaborating in larger-sized teams towards a collective goal possess tremendous potential for significant effectiveness in problem domains of a distributed nature. However, increasing the team size amplifies challenges related to coordination complexity, communication latency, and in situ risk assessment. In this paper, we propose a leadership framework that is risk-aware to address such challenges. Furthermore, we address how this leadership structure may persist under failure or the requirement for the robots to replenish power in prolonged missions. A preliminary assessment of the reified framework shows that dissemination is enhanced considerably as the team size scales up despite the computational overhead.

Title: AI Simulations and MILDEC
Authors: Devereaux, Z. P.; Lecocq, R.; Forrester, B.; Labrie, M.-A.
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Initial  testing of AI generative models is important to establish an early understanding of the deceptive capabilities of this new technology from a military perspective. This paper presents results about tests performed on AI generative models using military use cases.  Before  militaries  engage  in  the  widespread  usage  or  analysis  of  images  and  texts  from  AI  generative  models  (AI-IMTXT) research needs to be conducted laying the groundwork for a baseline understanding of the relationship between these technologies and military priorities, such as the capacity for deceiving with these new technologies. In the context of defense and security matters, this paper revisits systematically, the hypothesis that commercially available off the shelf (COTS) generative AI programs can produce social media posts that will be difficult to discern from human-generated content. Subsequently, discussions address both, the useful as well as the potential risks from the perspective of such military deception as part of command, control and intelligence (MILDEC & C2I).

Title: Team up for success: harnessing Participatory Sense-Making with the Harmonization Emergence Model
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: This paper introduces the Harmonization Emergence Model (HEM), a model that integrates two key dimensions of harmony: Einheit (coalescing unity) and Stimmigkeit (congruent shared meaning). The HEM, in conjunction with John Boyd's OODA-loop, provides a valuable tool for comprehending how harmonization can emerge within complex endeavors that span organizational, linguistic, and cultural boundaries. By employing these models, our aim is to broaden the understanding of the imperative to shift the focus of Command and Control (C2), beyond mere direction and control towards a function for catalyzing and modulating collaboration among organizations and actors within a specific context. Our objective with the HEM, is to better understand the 'power to the edge' concept and how C2 could facilitate assemblages (teams of teams) in actively engaging with and influencing the environment. This is guided by the pursuit of Congruence, which represents the metastable shared meaning we seek through Participatory SenseMaking.

Title: Adaptability of Computer Vision at the Tactical Edge: Addressing Environmental Uncertainty
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Computer Vision (CV) systems are increasingly being adopted into Command and Control (C2) systems to improve intelligence analysis on the battlefield, the tactical edge. CV systems leverage Artificial Intelligence (AI) algorithms to help visualize and interpret the environment, enhancing situational awareness. However, the adaptability of CV systems at the tactical edge remains challenging due to rapidly changing environments and objects which can confuse the deployed models. A CV model leveraged at the tactical edge can become uncertain in its predictions, as the environment and the objects existing in the environment begin to change. Additionally, mission objectives can rapidly change at the tactical edge leading to adjustments in technology, camera angles, and image resolutions. All of which can negatively affect the performance of and potentially introduce uncertainty into the system. When the training environment and/or technology differs from the deployment environment, CV models can perform unexpectedly. Unfortunately, most scenarios at the tactical edge do not incorporate Uncertainty Quantification (UQ) into their deployed C2 and CV systems. This paper explores the idea of synchronizing robust data operations and model fine-tuning driven by UQ all at the tactical edge. Specifically, curating datasets and training child models based on the residuals of predictions, using these child models to calculate prediction intervals (PI), and then using these PI to calibrate the models deployed at the tactical edge. By incorporating UQ into the core operations surrounding C2 and CV systems at the tactical edge, we can help drive purposeful adaptability on the battlefield.

Title: A Utilitarian Approach to the Structure of Human-AI Teams
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: With the current progress and limitations of artificial intelligence (AI), optimal outcomes in any complex problem-solving scenario will involve humans and AI working together. Yet, there is no accepted standard or guidance to configure the structure of human-AI teams for optimizing performance. We propose to use principles of economics and Fitts’ list 1 to provide this guidance. The study of the allocation of scarce resources provides the ideal model for mapping out the allocation of team members to accomplish an objective. Tasks can be allocated on an absolute basis, or relative to other tasks and their allocation. Some economic measures to consider are error rate, output time, and cost per unit time, in addition to conceptual measures, including trust or confidence, AI processing power, and human cognitive capacity. Although there may be more specific considerations for any human-AI team structure, two dimensions emerge as critical for an understanding of how to achieve optimality following a cognitive task analysis. First, cognitive task analysis will help separate the distinct tasks from one another. Those constituent parts should lead to an assessment of which tasks are best performed by humans and AI after consulting Fitts’ list 1 . Second, an overall understanding of the task’s computational complexity will help determine the ideal level of automation (i.e., the overall amount of the task allocated to the human teammates versus the AI teammates). We conclude that economic principles can help guide optimal human-AI teaming configurations. If done successfully, team-structure assessments can aid AI developers and save money and resources from pursuing AI capabilities that can remain with human teammates.

Title: AI in Future C2 – Who’s in Command When AI Takes Control?
Authors: Bjørn Tallak Bakken;Inger Lund-Kordahl; Inger Lund-Kordahl;Erik Bjurström
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) will become an increasingly dominant element of future Command and Control (C2) systems and organizations. In this paper we discuss how to make effective use of this technology in time-critical decision-making situations, as we classify the relationships between human decision maker; the AI system; and the task or situation at hand, along two dimensions: Automation and Autonomy. The former concerns the employment of AI to standardize and speed up the decision-making process (efficiency), without necessarily sacrificing accuracy or precision. The latter concerns the influence an AI system will have on the determination of the utility of the outcome of a decision-making process (effectiveness), relative to a human decision maker within the same process. Our research question is whether there is a trade-off between automation and autonomy when using AI to support decision-making in time-critical situations. In other words, can an AI system be trusted to make decisions that are both efficient and effective in time-critical situations, such as encountered by C2 systems?

Title: Modelling Multi-Domain C2 with network synchronisation: testing a NATO-SAS-143 hypothesis
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: I test one of the NATO-SAS-143 hypotheses using a mathematical model of C2 developed over many years within the ICCRTS forum. Specifically, the hypothesis is that for increased networking there is improved C2 Harmonisation between the different organisations across a collective within a multi-agency/multi-domain scenario but nevertheless at increasing cost. Here cost is measured in terms of  human cognitive effort  in  interaction  between  agents  in  a  C2  system  and  C2  Harmonisation  is  measured  as  the  consistency between levels of synchronisation of decision-making achieved within each of the organisations in the multi-agency collective. In this paper, building on a multi-domain scenario presented at ICCRTS in 2019, I test the hypothesis by adding additional random links to the  baseline  case.  I  demonstrate  that  within  this  model,  and  its  associated  simplifications,  the  evidence  is  consistent  with  the hypothesis.

Title: Challenges to Governing C2 Lifecycle Capability
Authors: Kalloniatis, A.; Wikberg, P.; Gaertner, P.; Jobidon, M.; Cooke, R.; Bryant, D.; Wikstrom, M.; Dekker, R. A.; Herkevall, J.; Granasen, M.; Houghton, P.
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: This paper presents an overview to the challenges in governing C2 holistically as a capability. It is written as part of the NATO-HFM342 study panel, itself the successor to the exploratory activity, NATO-HFM-ET-184. Under the aphorism 'physician heal thyself', the paper  presents  the  different  dimensions  to  how  Conway's  Law  hinders  hierarchical  C2  systems  and  their  wider  ecosystem  to transform themselves into anything other than a reproduction of themselves. Structurally this breaks down into two aspects of the dilemma of Who Owns the C2 system, the bureaucratic nature of the Capability Development (CAPDEV) system, the traditional stovepiping of projects in the CAPDEV system, and the stovepiping of Science & Technology skills in the organisations that support CAPDEV. Lastly, the strong role of institutional culture which envelopes all these systems, is explained and how it hinders progress to more agile C2 arrangements.
