Title: Case Study in Transforming C2: Australia and Plan MURA, an update
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: As a contribution to the NATO-HFM-342 study panel on C2 Capability Lifecycle Governance we present a case study of how Science and Technology has been brought to bear on achieving a level of change to C2 in Australia. Plan MURA is the modernisation and assurance endeavour of the Australian Defence Force's Joint Operations Command. Organised under four Lines of Effort, across organisation, infrastructure, ICT and workforce, MURA seeks to cover the range of dimensions of C2 as a sociotechnical system. In 2021 an ICCRTS paper explained MURA, its origins and progress to date. This paper extends the story further to the present day, reflecting on the successes and continuing  challenges in achieving measurably effective change to C2, using the framework of  the NATO study panel.

Title: Artificial Reasoning Approach utilizing Pharmacological Computational Model Information Mapping
Authors: Raglin, A.; Lott, D. A.; Metu, S.
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: A multidisciplinary approach to artificial reasoning includes exploring all type of processes that can be leveraged for new information system computational models. We are looking into concepts in the biological processes that can be effectively mapped with information handling and its utilization within an information system. The goal would be to create new reasoning agents that can handle information in similar ways. Pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics are the main components of pharmacology. Pharmacology is the study of interactions between a drug(s) and the individual constituents of a biological organism. Specifically, pharmacokinetics is the study of what the body’s action or alteration of a drug by the organism whereas pharmacodynamics is the study of what the drug does to the organism. In this research, we seek to model the process of conveying, receiving and processing information by modeling them via these two methods. Here, the information is modeled like a drug where the rate of transformation and the rate of absorption represent the rates at which information is passed or received from host to host. The constituents could represent either decision makers (hosts) or the process of making a decision. In this novel approach, this allows us to determine how breakdown of information might affect the decision-making process and how the decision-making process might then affect the transformation of information in a system.

Title: Data quality and analysts’ role in AI enhanced C2
Authors: Bjurström, E.; Schüler, M.; Strömberg, A.; Roxström, G.
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) in Command and Control (C2) raises questions about the interaction between operators on different levels on the one hand and AI-supported information systems on the other. For some purposes, the aggregation and analysis of large “big data”-sets creates potential for prediction and calculations of probabilities, while for other purposes human heuristics may be as promising depending on the situation. What has been more rarely discussed, is the very quality of data underpinning such calculations, and thus also operators’ awareness of the validity of predictions in relation to any specific situation. This is an urgent debate, considering the fact that full transparency may be impossible and underpinning data may be based on exercises, simulations, real-time data, or a mix thereof. Based on a fundamental classification of different kinds of uncertainties, this article discusses how data of different origins and quality can be managed and communicated to allow for operators to assess on what basis predictions are made. The article further suggests that looking at other fields of research may be useful for exploring unconventional ways of highlighting the existence and quality of different kinds of data of different origins in order to assess its predictive power. Finally, the article discusses how AI may change the role of analysts with regard to such issues.

Title: A framework for understanding the relationship between C2 theories, military long-term planning, and empirical studies
Authors: Johansson, B. J. E.; Lantz, M.; Herkevall, J.
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: This paper presents a three-level framework for exploring the relationship between theoretical approaches to command and control, doctrine and policy, and the operationalization of research on the level of experiments or evaluations. A fundamental assumption in this framework is that C2 exists to control processes within the own entity (the self) as well as processes outside the own entity (enemies or other external events that need to be handled to reach goals). The law of requisite variety is used as a conceptual tool for understanding the relationship between, on the one hand, C2 efforts and, on the other hand, scenarios that these efforts are intended to handle. On the top level, the suggested framework connects theoretical and conceptual work on C2 with similar approaches for modelling the problem to be controlled. On the intermediate level, the framework focuses on doctrine and management concepts and how these relate to the scenarios they are intended to handle, described in documents like, for example, 'future force concepts.' Finally, there is the empirical level, on which experiments and studies are conducted. On the empirical level, all assumptions from theory, doctrine, and the challenges to be controlled come into play, sometimes explicitly stated, sometimes not. Thus, the framework will serve as the basis for two important, but sometimes neglected, relationships in C2 research: 1) the relation (or lack of relations) between C2 theory/concepts/doctrines and theories/concepts/doctrines about the challenges intended to be controlled by the C2 theory, and 2) the relationship between such theories/concepts/doctrines and empirical research. The framework can be used as a tool for understanding the limitations and possibilities of different C2 theories, concepts and doctrines from both an applied and conceptual perspective. It can also be used as a tool to highlight differences between theory and practice in the field of C2.

Title: Adapting To Novelty Will Drive Future Operations
Authors: Chadwick, T.; Chao, J.; Izumigawa, C.; Manzanares, M.; Soultanian, N.; Lange, D. S.
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Change often has a significant impact on military operations. Historical examples include the introduction of steam propulsion for ships, the V2 Rocket, the Atomic Bomb, and Drones. The introduction of novel capabilities is accelerating, with the war in Ukraine providing multiple examples. Artificial Intelligence applications can be brittle with respect to novelty. Some research attempts to develop models robust to change in the operational domain. In the DARPA program tilted 'Science of Artificial Intelligence (A I) and Learning for Open-world Novelty (SAIL-ON), a different approach was taken. Agents were developed that can reason about novelty independent of the application domain. As part of SAIL-ON, experiments were conducted in a number of domains developed to allow the injection of novelty in a manner that supported scientific research. Additionally, a capstone experiment was conducted using more realistic military domains generated using the Advanced Framework for Simulation, Integration, and Modeling (AFSIM) environment, and by bringing capabilities in to live force-on-force exercises where the messiness of the real world could not be avoided. Both environments provided valuable lessons on how novelty affects AI in a realistic military domain, and suggest future research to enable AI agents to operate in domains that can be affected by novelty.

Title: Experimentation on C2 and IoT Technical Interoperability in a Tactical Federated Environment
Authors: Manso, M.; Guerra, B.; Freire, F.; Suri, N.; Fronteddu, R.; Di Caro, E.; Claus, R.; Ota, D.; Johnsen, F. T.; Furtak, J.; Glebocki, P.
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Modern military forces are highly interconnected and deal with a wide range of devices and data originated from internal and external systems. In this regard, IoT has gained the attention of military technology innovators as a means to gain information dominance in the battlespace, through enhanced and augmented situational  awareness.  Produced  as  part  of  the  NATO  research  task  group  IST-176  on  'Federated Interoperability of Military C2 and IoT Systems', this paper describes performed experiments whose aim was to explore novel approaches for a federated tactical deployment in a coalition environment, involving different C2 systems and IoT infrastructures with heterogeneous capabilities and ownership. The conducted experiments had several runs with different manipulations, including high datarates and high number of tracks, demonstrating unprecedent scalability and flexibility, as well as the capability to generate a high success rate in message delivery (above 99%), small message delivery delays (median values between 127 ms and 154 ms, with minimum median of 4ms) and a consistent situational picture across different C2 systems.  Importantly,  the  experiments  demonstrated  robust  technical  effectiveness  for  multi-national operational deployments, supporting collective C2, whilst nations operate their own tactical infrastructure.

Title: Integrating Wargaming into the Canadian Operational Planning Process: An Experimental Approach
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The rapidly evolving modern combat space presents military institutions with increasing uncertainty as to the success or failure of their force concepts and designs. Emerging and novel capabilities such as autonomous systems, sophisticated information related capabilities (IRCs), and artificial intelligence, in addition to an ever more multivariate operating environment, requires re-evaluation of the planning process and the need for rigorous and informed debate on the conduct of modern operations. Wargaming is a crucial tool by which militaries assess their decision-making concerning conduct of tactics, techniques, and procedures, but remains underutilized for harmonizing modern Command and Control (C2). In contrast to other uses of wargaming, experimental wargames focus on hypothesis testing, enabling the creation of concrete examples and case studies of future operating environments. These assist in indicating what the employment of new capabilities on the modern battlefield will look like and ground discussion under a set of common rules. This paper will explore the theoretical background of experimental wargaming and describe the benefits it holds for the modern joint planning process. It proposes that forces be open to experimental wargaming as a tool to explore future scenarios and new approaches, foster healthy debate across ranks and services, and encourage increased involvement of military members in the too often obscured wargame design process.

Title: What Mission Command is Not
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The mission command philosophy is commonly associated with empowering subordinate commanders to solve problems creatively within boundaries set by the commander’s intent [1]. Mission command is regarded as the most promising method for future command [2]. Still, there is a tendency to associate this command approach solely with authority delegation level, disregarding the organization’s structure and other factors. Additional elements associated with mission command, such as trust, should be part of command and control studies in general rather than the command approach. Furthermore, command features such as disciplined initiative, agile and adaptive leadership, a clear and concise statement of intent, building trust with subordinates, or accepting mistakes and failures require further research. The possible command and control approaches are not definitively known because only a small subset of potential approaches have been explored by military organizations. Some organizations accept mission command as a permanent part of their command and control system without questioning its fitness for every situation. Accepting mission command in various capacities is partly due to organizations’ and individuals’ perceptions of mission command. Despite the current tendency to see mission command as the cure-all for complex operational challenges, mission command is not a fully developed and successfully exercised approach [2], [3]. To get back on track, defining what mission command is or what it is not is an essential step in establishing a basis for future studies. This paper aims to develop such a foundation.

Title: The Sweet Spot: Between Agility and Standardization
Authors: Johansson, B.; Herkevall, J.; Kalloniatis, A.; Granasen, M.
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: This paper describes an ongoing bi-lateral Swedish-Australian research effort aiming to better understand interoperability in the context of non-NATO countries in the light of Federated Mission Networking. Interoperability is an increasingly complex issue as the spectrum  of  conflict  involve  multi-domain  operations  beyond  the  traditional  military  domains.  Even  with  frameworks  such  as Federated  Mission  Networking,  considerations  must  be  taken  to  avoid  design  decisions  that  may  hamper  C2  agility  or  inhibit interoperability for partners outside of the Federated Mission Networking community. The joint project seeks to explore and work to resolve the paradox between standardisation, as a means of enabling interoperability between diverse partners, and flexibility as an underpinning to Agile C2. The project aims to develop a proposal for a meta-design for interoperability to empower both NATO and non-NATO users to employ solutions that allow them to combine and utilize capabilities jointly.

Title: Defining Cyber Warfare Capability Attributes and Characteristics for African Cyber Missions
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The domains of warfare have become complex, where cyberspace is declared as the fifth domain. The complexity of cyber space is that it is borderless, and war could be launched from anywhere, and is a critical concern for national security. There is also a different understanding on what cyber warfare needs to entail for nations to be able to defend and/or exploit the cyber space to sustain their territorial integrity and sovereignty. At the same time, the cyber warfare domain is dominated by developed nations, whilst African states are mostly left behind without fully appreciating the comprehensive capabilities (e.g., processes, people, and technologies) required to participate in this arena. This paper extends on the research of the authors in unpacking a comprehensive framework for developing cyber warfare capabilities for African militaries. It unpacks the framework to specific and measurable cyber warfare capability attributes and characteristics. This paper focused only on the offensive objectives of the comprehensive framework. This could  aid  African  militaries  to  develop  and  mature  their  cyber  warfare  capabilities  over  time.  This  paper  also  suggests  the  implementation  roadmap,  considering  all  stakeholders  and  the  most  efficient  way  for  establishing  this  strategic  and  national capability. The significance of the work presented in this paper is that it may aid nation states to systematically develop their cyber warfare capabilities to enable them to participate and/or be ready for participation in the fifth domain of warfare.

Title: A Set-based Approach: searching for the Problem-Solution Eclipse
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: This paper draws attention to some of the unintended consequences of current planning directives, which not only obscure the true  essence  of  complex  endeavours,  but  may  also  inhibit  the  intellectual  capabilities  of  planning  teams  because  of  their prescriptive and voluminous nature. In the Set-based Approach (SbA), we introduce five principles aimed at optimizing planning time and reducing the likelihood of making premature assumptions. SbA extends beyond the scope of military operational planning, offering a flexible planning philosophy that enables teams to deal more adeptly with uncertainty. The SbA concept has undergone informal testing during two pilot programs at the Swedish Defence University.

Title: The Anatomy of Command and Control: a Generic Functional Model
Source title: 28th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: This paper addresses the challenge that alternative command and control (C2) approaches are required to maintain operation effectiveness in the increasingly complex operating environment, but that current doctrinal definitions of C2 are likely to be incompatible with the needs and characteristics of future C2 systems. The fundamental nature of C2 was explored through an examination of 'Purpose' from which the following definition was derived: a system of relationships and activities that ensure the purposefulness, coherence and effectiveness of collective action within an operating enterprise through the design, maintenance and regulation of operations. This was used as the basis for modelling the functions required to enact C2 and the dependencies between them. The result was a Generic Functional Model that codifies C2 in the widest possible terms such that the same framework can be used to describe any instantiation of C2 now or in the future. To facilitate this aim, an 'interpretive' approach was taken: the model does not rely on rigidly defined terms but rather expresses functional concepts that can be interpreted by the user to suit their application and context. It is anticipated that this will have utility for envisaging, developing and assessing novel C2 approaches.

Title: Emergent Defence Organising
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Defence Command and Control (C2) relies on socio-technical systems and hierarchical methods of organising functional elements. This paper builds from threat projections of future operational complexity to explore the Defence organisational limitations in coping with complex problems, and what type of organisational changes could help Defence start to achieve advantage within the inherent complexity of conflict. Through an understanding of some of the historical organisational contexts, the paper provides visualisations of the most significant C2 organisational capability gaps and proposes the development of a more effective Defence enterprise, using emergent relationships between loosely affiliated functional groups to deliver competitive advantage in complex future operating environments.

Title: Consequences for Command and Control of Semiautonomous Systems for Mechanized Warfare
Authors: Oskarsson, P.-A.; Svenmarck, P.; Melbi, A.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The use of unmanned and semi-autonomous systems, such as UAVs and UGVs, in armed forces is expected to increase. Such systems provide new means to enhance military capabilities in intelligence, movement, endurance, fire, and protection. This raises several questions related to command and control (C2), such as the proper echelon level for their control and which tasks they should be used for. Four workshops were conducted with personnel from the Swedish Land Warfare Centre (LWC) to study the potential effects of semi-autonomous systems on C2 for mechanized warfare. The workshops used a tactical board game for mechanized warfare, which allowed participants to cooperate and discuss possibilities and limitations of using semi-autonomous systems. The workshops investigated C2 of mechanized warfare supported by semi-autonomous systems at the platoon level (workshop 1), company level (workshop 2), semi-autonomous systems equipped with the current and potential future level of autonomy (workshop 3), and semiautonomous heavy unmanned combat vehicles (workshop 4). This paper describes the results from workshop 3 and 4, and summarizes the integrated experiences from all four workshops regarding C2 of semi-autonomous systems for mechanized warfare in terms of capabilities and tasks, challenges, level of command, autonomous functions for semi-autonomous systems, and implications for mechanized warfare. The results show that semi-autonomous systems provide several benefits for mechanized warfare in terms of situation awareness, firepower, and risk for human losses. C2 of semi-autonomous systems is most suitable at the company level, which can be realized by introducing an additional command vehicle with system operators.

Title: Causing Chaos for an Adversary Using the Cynefin Framework
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Since 1999, Snowden and co-workers developed the Cynefin framework to help leaders, managers, and policy-makers make decisions in a complex and chaotic world. Drawing on research in systems theory, complexity science, network science, and theories of learning, the framework has been honed on critical business issues. Some of its development has been funded by DARPA. MoD(UK)'s Joint Concept Note 2/17 on the future of C2 suggests that the framework should be applied to C2 doctrine. The Cynefin framework sorts the issues facing leaders into five contexts defined by the nature of the relationship between cause and effect. Four of the contexts - Simple, Complicated, Complex, and Chaotic - require leaders to diagnose situations and to act in contextually appropriate ways. The fifth context - Disorder - applies when it is unclear which of the other four is predominant. Movement across context boundaries requires a shift to a different OODA-like model of understanding and interpretation, a different form of leadership, and a different set of connection strengths between leaders, subordinates, and peers. Kurtz and Snowden (2007) identify ten typical trajectories through two or more contexts. The predominant aim is to create order by moving from the unordered Chaotic and Complex contexts into the Simple or Complicated context. Temporary movement into the Chaotic context and back out again is permitted as a way of disrupting the entrained thinking of experts, but permanent movement into the Chaotic context is regarded as disastrous. In military operations, the aim is to create chaos for the adversary so that he/she loses the will to fight. In Cynefin terms, the adversary has to be forcibly moved into his/her Chaotic context. To do so, the framework must be extended with additional trajectories moving in the opposite direction, from the ordered contexts into the Chaotic context. In this paper, such trajectories have been derived from the 36 ancient Chinese stratagems.
Author Keywords: CYNEFIN FRAMEWORK; MILITARY COMMAND AND CONTROL (C2); CHAOS CREATION STRATEGIES; CHINESE 36 STRATAGEMS; DECEPTION AND INFORMATION OPERATIONS; COMPLEXITY AND SYSTEMS THEORY; OODA LOOP ADAPTATION; CONFRONTATION ANALYSIS

Title: Training a Virtual Assistant for Reconnaissance Target Selection
Authors: Stucken, S.; Cawalla, J.; Schmitz, H.-C.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: In this concept paper, we will discuss possibilities for training a virtual assistant for an S2-operator via reinforcement learning (RL). The S2-operator in the command post of a battalion maintains and assesses a situational picture of the enemy forces. The picture represents the perceived truth. It is usually incomplete, partially outdated and may contain false information. The operator is also provided with a representation of the enemy's expected plan, including an Order of Battle (ORBAT) of the enemy forces. The operator maintains the situational picture by adding or deleting battle space objects (BSOs). New BSOs are added if they have been detected by reconnaissance or if their presence can be inferred from the perceived truth and the plan (enrichment). Moreover, he determines valuable reconnaissance areas and, in cooperation with reconnaissance forces, allocates sensors to them. In our paper, we will assess possibilities of training a virtual assistant, firstly, for the maintenance of the situational picture and, secondly, for the selection of reconnaissance targets and the allocation of sensors. For the second use case, we will define a Markov Decision Process (MDP) and make a preliminary proposal for the graph-based representation of the state space.
Author Keywords: REINFORCEMENT LEARNING; MARKOV DECISION PROCESS; GRAPH NEURAL NETWORKS; RECONNAISSANCE TARGET SELECTION; SENSOR ALLOCATION; SITUATIONAL AWARENESS; ORDER OF BATTLE (ORBAT) MODELING; COMMAND AND CONTROL (C2) OPTIMIZATION; SCARCE DATA MITIGATION

Title: Cyber-Enabled Influence Operations
Authors: Waldman, S.; Watson, S.; Nakhla, N.; Pesteh, F.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The domain of Psychological Operations, otherwise known as Influence Operations, is now transacted almost wholly in cyberspace: that is into networks of online media, social media and other web applications [1]. Basic online influence operations in cyberspace, going back to the mid-2000s, consist of using online applications in a deceptive way--for example creating social media accounts that appear to belong to real, relatable and/or credible individuals, and using them to circulate persuasive content among online social networks [2]. Increasingly, adversarial online influence operations are additionally leveraging cyber-capabilities to push influence operations beyond the limits of what they can achieve using the customary affordances of social media platforms [3]. Cyber capabilities can provide botnets capable of introducing or amplifying social media content on a larger scale and/or at a faster rate than human operators can do [4]. Generative AI can likewise significantly reduce the requirements and expand the reach of Psychological Operations by automating and streamlining the process [5][6]. There is a case for the tactical use of cyber-enabled influence operations targeting adversaries in named military operations. Yet facilitating joint cyber-influence activities raises distinctive challenges for Allied militaries, which typically house cyber and influence capabilities in separate institutions and attributed with different degrees of status [7].  This paper first looks at the tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) of recent major Influence Operation campaigns—in general and then in particular--to establish the degree to which they are already being enabled by cyber capabilities. It subsequently looks at some examples of doctrine to elicit some obstacles for allied militaries to create competitive joint cyber-influence functions and provides some recommendations.

Title: Vulnerability Analysis of a Subset of Decentralized Information Consolidation Mechanisms in C2
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The digitalization of the information space and the decentralization of C2 processes are important steps to accelerate decision and planning cycles, improve situational awareness (SA), and enhance C2 agility in the face of multifaceted threats. While digital technologies are key enablers to generating superiority, their exploitation in hostile environments also present many challenges. Tactical and strategic networks can be attacked kinetically and non-kinetically through cyber and electromagnetic attacks and can propagate malicious codes, faulty signals, and erroneous information, which in turn can detrimentally affect networked C2 nodes, platforms, weapons, and sensors. It is therefore critical to identify the vulnerabilities of networked systems, the direct and indirect effects and the risks posed by non-kinetic threats on overall SA and the conduct of operations. Time-critical systems, such as networked (semi-) autonomous weapons (offensive operations) and battle management threat evaluation and weapon assignment (defensive operations) systems, require special attention owing to the irreversibility of their action. What happens in terms of security if ( i ) networks are intermittently or permanently disabled, ( ii ) information is corrupted with deceptive content, ( iii ) computing processes are corrupted by malicious codes, or ( iv ) control authority is lost by network-enabled weapons and operator (e.g., weapon’s mission can no longer be aborted)? To discuss some of these issues, we focus on decentralized consolidation mechanisms (information fusion and aggregation), which serves as interface systems with decentralized SA and planning capabilities and adopt high-level abstraction models to analyze some of their vulnerabilities. Each mechanism leads to an aggregation dynamical system whose structure reflects the communication network topology. Therefore, each dynamical system is a function of parameters, graph structure, and variables that represent endogenous and exogenous signals to C2 nodes. Each of these elements can be subject to the injection of corrupted signals and codes. The high-level abstraction adopted allows hypotheses to be formulated about possible classes of vulnerabilities and resulting faulty behaviour, such as oscillatory behaviour (undesirable changes in recommendations) and bifurcation leading to instabilities. The decentralized aggregations considered consists of the average, maximum and majority operators, whose applications include Bayesian information fusion, best plan selection using decentralized bidding, risk-averse option selection (e.g., aggregation of threat levels and object classes), and majority-based recommendation. An analytical result provides a condition for possible transitions towards instabilities. Simulations results illustrate the potential effects of vulnerabilities on a class of C2 processes as functions of key parameters.

Title: Mapping & Graphing Uncertainty in Science & Technology
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: This research paper considers evolution to be both incremental and by step change. Science and Technology (S&T) follow different entangled harmonic time constants that combine and separate about conjoined surge / stagnate, and invention / innovation logistic curves. These time constants are used to understand how the Science at different discovery levels may interact with Technology, at different readiness levels - and vice versa. This paper examines the adoption of NASA (1974) Technology Readiness Level (TRL) as a means of assessing against the science. This gave rise to the adoption of a RAAF (2023) Science Discovery Level (SDL) for subjectively situating TRL. The SDL-TRL Instrument identified allows objective assessments to be made for situating science and technology. To arrive at the SDL-TRL instrument, historical analysis of previous scientific ages, associated technologies, and human generations is undertaken. This leads into analysis of future scientific ages, with ethical and moral implications for humanity. Graph Theory is then applied  to  the  front  end  of  a  scientific  (research,  invention,  and  exploration)  and  technology  (development,  innovation,  and exploitation) assessment process. As undertaken by many Government departments, and research bodies. It does so by applying the Scientific Discovery Level against the long-standing Technology Readiness Level (TRL), at different science and technology harmonics (or levels). It applies Graph Theory to consider other causes of decision-making-and-taking (DMT) uncertainty, in addition to science and technology harmonics. It then examines how the identified causes or sources of uncertainty are related to each other, and how each  factor  can  have  an  influence  in  the  contribution  to  uncertainty  of  another  factor.  Graph  theory  is  used  to  quantify  the uncertainty in Decision Assessments (DA) - converted into a matrix, designated as the uncertainty sources instrument (UI). From which an assessment of uncertainty, at that stage of the DMT process, can be made, as a means of aiding science and technology assessments instrumenting (translate, forecast, and operationalize) S&T programs.

Title: Immersive Control Rooms for Human-Robot Teams
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Human-robot teams are increasingly being used in complex and dangerous environments. At the same time, there is growing interest in the potential of immersive technologies such as virtual, augmented, and mixed reality (VR, AR, MR) for revolutionising command and control systems. Control rooms are dynamic high-information environments, where decision-making relies on fast and accurate information and clear communication to manage and respond to time-critical situations. However, control rooms are expensive and logistically challenging to set up and maintain. Further, the presentation of information to control room operators is challenging due to  the  boundaries  of  available  space  and  the  limits  of  traditional  2D  interfaces.  VR  offers  new  opportunities  for  immersive communication and collaboration in human-robot teaming (HRT) for improving situational awareness to support informed decisionmaking.  For  example,  VR  can  enable  visualisation  of  complex  information,  embodied  interaction  for  enhanced  communication, teleoperation and control of remote robots and swarms, and enhanced spatial understanding of complex environments. In this paper,  we  survey  the  existing  research  on  immersive  control  rooms  for  human-robot  teams  and  discuss  challenges  and  open questions.
Author Keywords: IMMERSIVE CONTROL ROOMS; HUMAN-ROBOT TEAMING; VIRTUAL, AUGMENTED AND MIXED REALITY (VR/AR/MR); TELEOPERATION AND AUTONOMOUS ROBOT CONTROL; MULTI-ROBOT SWARM VISUALIZATION; SITUATIONAL AWARENESS IN COMMAND AND CONTROL; COGNITIVE WORKLOAD AND OPERATOR FATIGUE; DECISION SUPPORT SYSTEMS FOR HRT; CYBERSECURITY AND DATA INTEGRITY IN IMMERSIVE ENVIRONMENTS

Title: Architectural concepts to support future C2 systems that enable Multi-Domain Operations
Authors: Peerdeman, M.; Kollmann, F.; Khayari, R. E.; Aceti, L.; Lockwood, S.; Eikelboom, A.; MacLean, M.; Pigeon, L.; van der Venne, T.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract:
In contemporary military thought, the concept of 'Multi-Domain Operations' (MDO) plays a significant role, often perceived as the modern military response to evolving environments, new actors, and emerging threats characterizing the complexity of conflicts today. Even though there is an approved Alliance concept for MDO, a common understanding of what MDO means in practice is lacking. As a result, considerable development to operationalize and implement underlying concepts, which is commencing on a large scale, is done from different national MDO perspectives. This paper recognizes a common focus on the integration of the five military domains but argues that there is a spectrum of activities within MDO that includes other instruments of power.

The traditional domains of land, sea, and air have generally been expanded to include cyber and space, with effects seen not only in the physical world but also in information and cognitive realms. Combined with the intensive collaboration with non-military Instruments of power and the ability to navigate quickly and agilely everywhere in the continuum between war and peace, this provides a qualitative perspective on the complexity and multiplicity of dimensions and entities that span the MDO space. Within NATO, these concepts today already are shaping the roadmap and capability definition and development of future decision support systems. This publication, authored by members collaborating as NATO RTG SAS-IST 171, examines specific principles, requirements and several components for an architecture that would be able to capture support for future Command and Control (C2) systems operating in MDO. The authors explore appropriate architectural forms that could guide solutions in this area. Based on these insights, the authors propose ways to address these challenges and prepare alliance for future combat scenarios.

Title: Beyond Technology: Goal-Oriented Analysis for Integrating Emerging Technologies into Military Command and Control Systems
Authors: Lundberg, J.; Stirna, J.; Zdravkovic, J.; Andersson, K.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: In military operations, the integration of emerging technologies into Command- and Control systems (C2-systems) presents an urgent issue. The introduction of potentially disruptive technologies such as AI, machine learning, and unmanned systems has the potential to significantly enhance operational efficiency. However, such technologies come with ethical dilemmas and challenges concerning their integration into C2-capability containing legacy doctrinal and organizational components. This paper is part of a research  project  following  the  Design  Science  research  (DSR)  process.  The  main  goal  of  the  research  project  is  to  develop  a conceptual framework, with models and methods, that can help designers and commanders in assessing and understanding C2systems from a socio-technical perspective. It also aims to provide insights into how individual subsystems influence the SoS as a whole. In previous articles, the authors have discussed the challenges associated with integrating new technology into military C2systems. This paper further elaborates on findings indicating the need to shift focus from optimizing individual components or subsystems to considering the entire, large, and complex system (aka System of Systems, SoS). This shift highlights a comprehensive and holistic approach which is crucial for ensuring that C2-systems are effective, precise, and adaptable to changing conditions and requirements. However, currently there is a gap in existing research and methodologies that can explain and predict the dynamics between information systems, methods, processes, and organizational development in a SoS. To bridge this gap, it is necessary to adjust the existing frameworks for capability development to adopt a broader, more adaptable, and exhaustive scope. Additionally, a  socio-technical  focus  in  system  development  would  facilitate  a  deeper  understanding  of  the  interdependencies  between organization, processes, and technical system evolution. Previous surveys, conducted as interviews of stakeholders, identified a refined  perception  of  the  challenges  encountered  within  specified  domains,  such  as,  operational  and  development.  The stakeholders' needs were analyzed and modeled as goals for the envisioned framework. The stakeholders communicated how an adjusted framework of methods should function to tackle the challenges with the emerging technologies. By employing DSR and Enterprise Modeling, this paper proposes (i) a concepts model that outlines the principles and the overarching construction of a C2system. This concepts model provides the foundations to goal modeling ensuring coherence and interoperability among different models. We also propose (ii) a goal model for C2-systems representing the objectives for what the envisioned framework needs to accomplish. The study results indicate that the framework is to be useful in systematically identifying and aligning the goals of an artifact  with  the  overarching  objectives,  i.e.,  for  designing  military  C2-systems  and  understanding  the  integration  of  the technologies.  This  research project  contributes  to  ongoing research  on  military innovation,  offering insights into  the  systemic challenges and opportunities that new technologies present within the complex ecosystem of military operations.

Title: AI techniques in Gaming for C2. Strategy detection, explainability and usability in StarCraft II
Authors: Hernandez, C. S.; Connolly, S.; Clayton, M. J.; Lim, M.; Hughson, E.; Smeaton, J.; Valentine, D.; Pravhakaran, A.; Smallman, J.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract:
Video games are an ideal type of simulation environment to create and test new AI developments, as they provide a controllable set of variables and baselines to test results against, and data availability to explore, therefore offering a rapid turnaround on finding which technique is most valuable and has most potential for real-world applications.

As part of the Machine Speed C2 (MSC2) programme run by the UK Defense, Science and Technology laboratory (Dstl), our project explores: (i) piercing the ‘fog of war’ to infer opponents’ positions and (ii) investigating key elements for detecting opponent’s strategy within the game StarCraft II. As with real-world C2, this is a complex game in which a player must make decisions with partial information available in order to defeat an opponent.  Our goal consisted of developing two AI assistants to augment a human player’s decision making by: 1) developing an encoder-decoder neural network architecture to enable nowcasting likely locations of enemy units through the fog of war with an associated confidence level 2) using unsupervised techniques (clustering) and supervised techniques (a neural network classifier) to classify strategies with confidence and probabilistic metrics 3) creating a user-centric explainability framework that translates technical outputs into visual and text explanations based on user-centric understanding 4) performing a usability study to determine which method of communicating the AI outputs is more valuable to non-expert users.

Details and conclusions of this study are presented with recommendations as to how to use the learnings of this project within a C2 environment.

Title: Charting Tomorrow's Command and Control: Utilizing Technical Capability Areas and Vision Workshops
Authors: Tolentino, G. S.; Wood, J. N.; George, A.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: In  addressing Command and Control (C2) capabilities, the Naval Information Warfare Center Pacific's C2 Department employs  Technical  Capability  Areas  (TCAs)  as  organizational  frameworks  to  consolidate  related  efforts  in  product development.  These  TCAs  serve  as  the  cornerstone  for  the  department's  activities,  steering  its  endeavors  towards addressing current military operational requirements. Although the TCAs are clearly delineated and each division within the department effectively meets present operational demands, there remains untapped potential in harnessing these TCAs to shape the future C2 capabilities essential for empowering our warfighters.  This concept paper will shed light on a  vision  workshop  construct  designed  to  assist  engineering  teams  in  forecasting  future  needs  beyond  end-user requirements for their TCA. In addition to creating a future vision, the workshop delineates the trajectories of products, skillsets, knowledge, and facilities necessary to successfully envision, create, and field innovative C2 products. The authors will highlight the workshop's phases and outcomes, detailing the methods to foster a mindset conducive to expansive thinking among participants, the validation techniques applied to workshop outputs, and the transformative impact of these workshop outputs across NIWC Pacific, industry, and academia. The combined TCA and vision workshop approach holds significant potential for any organization navigating the rapidly evolving landscape of technology, where constant product enhancement is paramount. Thus, the establishment of a comprehensive roadmap for product development, alongside the identification and implementation of associated resources, are essential for the organization's continued relevance and success.

Title: Federated Mission Networking and Civil-Military Interoperability - striving for the correct level of design
Authors: Johansson, B.; Herkevall, J.; Kalloniatis, A.; Granasen, M.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: This paper presents a study of interoperability in the light of Civil-Military Cooperation (CIMIC) and Federated Mission Networking (FMN) using the concept of meta-design.  Meta-design is based on the idea that it is hard to foresee all possible points of interaction and their needs in a collaborative context. A degree of 'under design' is therefore desirable to leave space for adaption, thus enabling C2 Agility across multiple non-traditional (or smaller sized) partners, rather than trying to achieve pre-determined paths and solutions for interaction. While the ideal of 'zero-day interoperability' is desirable and should be pursued, we need to consider approaches that provide a mechanism for rapid convergence to a viable interoperable network faster than has been realised in recent decades with partner outside of the FMN collective. FMN, specifically spiral 5, in the context of Civil-Military Cooperation (CIMIC), is used as a case study to investigate if CIMIC is a case where standardisation like FMN is less appropriate due to the requirement to be agile in C2  given  cultural  diversity  across  the  civilian  (non-military)  domain?  Four  interviews  with  subject  matter  experts  have  been  conducted to test the FMN assumptions, which then are discussed in the light of meta-design.

Title: Exploring guiding principles for C2 Capability governance
Authors: Granåsen, M.; Wikberg, P.; Hill, J.; Gaertner, P.; Bryant, D.; Jobidon, M.-E.; Dekker, R.; Kalloniatis, A.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Emerging challenges within the security environment require governments and coalitions to continously develop, adapt or transform military capabilities. Multiple factors impede transformation to command and control (C2) capability and practices. Effective C2 capability governance is needed, encompassing development, adaptation, and maintenance aspects of C2 as a sociotechnical capability. This paper summarises a study that explored principles for C2 capability governance and the identification of describes eight guiding principles. Some principles concern the need to foster innovation and creativity within organizational structures found in defence organizations, whilst others address needs to change the mindsets and culture. Feedback on the principles was collected through a survey, in which participants from military and research communities with C2 experience were asked to rate the relevance and provide comments about the eight principles. The relevance ratings showed support for all eight principles, with mean relevance ratings ranging from 3.6 to 4.3 on a five-point scale. The free-text comments from the 36 survey respondents provided valuable insights. Differences in interpretation of the wording by respondents might explain the large distribution between ratings for some of the principles. Respondents suggested the following improvements: (1) revise the wording of some principles; (2) clarify the definition of C2 capability governance; (3) add other principles to the set of eight; (4) address the risk that the principles are too general by providing examples of how they can be put into practice. In conclusion, the survey results have helped with the development of a set of principles that are needed to promote C2 capability governance.

Title: The Development and Challenges of Multi-Domain Operations and the Characteristics of Collaboration
Authors: Eikelboom, A. R.; van der Venne, T.; Aceti, L.; MacLean, M.; Lockwood, S.; Peerdeman, M. S.; Kollmann, F.; El Abdouni Khayari, R.; Pigeon, L.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Contemporary military thinking in the Alliance is dominated by a concept that seems to have as many variations on the definition as it  does  developers:  Multi-Domain  Operations  (MDO).  Despite  the  general  consensus  on  the  Alliance  Concept  for  Multi-Domain Operations  developed  by  NATO  and  approved  in  April  2023,  Member  States  had  previously  started  to  develop  their  own conceptualization of MDO and even within Member States, interpretations may differ depending on command level or branch. Complicating matters even further, MDO shows considerable conceptual links with other integrating warfighting concepts such as Joint  Interagency  Multi-National  and  Public  (JIMP),  Pan-Domain  Operations,  the  comprehensive  approach,  and  hybrid  conflict. Aiming to unravel some of the complexity surrounding MDO, this paper attempts to address how to characterize the conceptual development and resultant challenges of MDO as the Alliance's dominant warfighting concept . This conceptual analysis is grounded in military, scientific, and doctrinal literature to illustrate how the evolution of MDO as a warfighting concept is intrinsically tied to the challenges it presents actual decision-makers. Using a scenario driven approach, this paper zooms in on collaboration between stakeholders in the context of MDO. The characteristics of these collaborations form the base for the identification of required C2 services for MDO. The analysis is supported with the experiences and perceptions of military subject-matter experts on MDO and related issues. The results are suggested paths ahead for how to tackle these challenges and prepare the Alliance for the future of warfighting.

Title: Creative Decision-Making in Multi-Domain Operations
Authors: Solaki, A.; Kerstholt, J.; Roelofs, M.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The concept of Multi-Domain Operations (MDO) has emerged proposing the orchestration of military activities, across all domains (Air, Land, Sea, Cyber, Space), synchronized with non-military activities, to deliver converging effects on all dimensions (physical, cognitive, virtual). A key principle for MDO is creativity: the ability to analyze the situation from different viewpoints and stimulate the generation of multiple surprising dilemmas to opponents. However, it is unclear whether traditional military decision-making processes are fit for MDO and sufficiently serve creativity. We discuss the implications of MDO for traditional military decisionmaking and identify its limitations. On this basis, we consult scientific literature to recommend creativity-enhancing techniques along decision-making steps. First, we provide recommendations inspired by cognitive science paradigms, suggesting how non-analytical features of human cognition can leverage creative decision-making and how suitably structured dialogical settings allow groups with diverse members to perform better in decision-making tasks, compared to their individual members. We apply this evidence to design structured brainstorms as part of mission analysis and report on the results of a workshop, where a group of military experts were given a multidomain mission scenario, used the structured brainstorm, and assessed the creativity of the process and its outcomes. Second, we explain how Intelligent Decision Support Systems, based on Large Language Models, could further contribute to decision-making steps like COA development, alleviate part of the analytical processing workload and augment the true strengths of human expertise. We close with an overview of preliminary insights and considerations for future work on MDO decision-making.

Title: Defense Against Adversarial Attacks on Large Language Models – A Technology Survey
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are the recent Artificial Intelligence (AI) advancement for their unprecedented capabilities in natural language processing (NLP) tasks, such as query-answering, sentiment analysis, document summarization, and others. These analytical capabilities have a great impact on the command-and-control process of military. Consequently, LLM-empowered data analysis tools are essential for militaries to lead in the information environment. The significant use of LLMs highlights the importance of their trustworthiness in terms of reliability and security particularly in sectors that prioritize safety and privacy protection, such as military, government organizations and healthcare systems. However, reported studies have revealed LLMs’ vulnerabilities and trustworthiness risks in various aspects. Particularly, LLMs are vulnerable to malicious attacks. In the line of malicious attacks, adversarial attacks in the context of LLMs represent an even serious concern in AI safety - LLM outputs can appear so credibly sounding that users may trust LLM-generated content without verification. The severer security and privacy concerns caused by adversarial attacks trigger research efforts to mitigate these threats. This survey summarizes the novel approaches and techniques in this domain to provide a timely and highly relevant review of this emerging research area.

Title: Sentiment Analysis: Fine-Tuning Open-Source Large Language Models
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Sentiment analysis is a Natural Language Processing (NLP) task that analyzes an input text to determine whether its attitude is positive, negative, or neutral. For the military, sentiment analysis becomes an important data analysis capability in the information environment to understand public sentiments toward topics of interest, make assessments of current circumstances, and predict future human behavior, and has a great impact on the command-and-control process of military consequently. Large language models (LLMs) are the state-of-the-art Artificial Intelligence (AI) techniques that have attained cutting-edge performance on a variety of NLP tasks, including sentiment analysis. With the advent of LLMs, there is great potential for sentiment analysis to forge ahead with more improved performance. However, in the course of leveraging LLMs for NLP tasks, current evaluation practices in assessing LLMs for the task of sentiment analysis are insufficient. In this paper, our research effort looks into the fine-tuning approach to adapting pre-trained LLMs for the task of sentiment analysis. The significant challenge for the fine-tuning approach is the need for high-quality labelled data, given the difficulty in obtaining labelled data. We particularly investigate how the data quality would impact the performance of the fine-tuned (target) model. This paper explores the versatility of three open-source LLMs, i.e., DeBERTaV3, ERNIE2 and FlanT5. They were measured against three experiments: 1) each model was fine-tuned on different sizes of a fine-tuning dataset to track the accuracy of the target model. This was done to investigate the minimum cardinality of a dataset required before performance reaching to a plateau; 2) each model was fine-tuned with varying levels of noise to determine the minimum quality of fine-tuning data required before the accuracy began to degrade; and 3) each model was tested on mis-matched datasets to the fine-tuning dataset to track the effect of distribution or domain shift and the target model’s flexibility.

Title: Complexity Implications for Defence and C2
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Military operations are becoming increasingly complex, with significant implications for Defence Command and Control (C2) as many current approaches and systems were designed for industrial age warfare rather than for challenges posed by complexity. To address complexity it is necessary to first understand some of its causes, including how complexity is experienced through a confluence of factors and unpredictable interactions. Multiple C2 challenges arise from complexity and from the related notions of wicked, super-wicked and messy problems; these challenges need to be considered when seeking to improve C2 capability. This paper explores these challenges in the context of Defence assumptions about future C2 capability. It also offers ways to address some of these challenges to effect change and improve future C2 capability by exploiting current sources of inspiration to create an improved approach for dealing with complexity within defence and national security contexts.

Title: The Concept of C2 as a Capability
Authors: Houghton, P.; Vickers, E.; Hansson, L.; Kace, R.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The UK Joint Concept Note 2/17: Future of Command and Control (C2) argued for the need to treat C2 as a more holistic capability and to develop it as “a dynamic and adaptive socio-technical system”. Whilst there has been an acceptance of these ideas since the publication of the JCN in 2017, initiatives to develop C2 as a capability have been slow to appear. This may be due to disagreements or misunderstandings about the nature of C2 or that Defence has historically focussed on improving technical systems. Equally it is possible that an impediment to change is a lack of common understanding of what the original “C2 as a capability” concept entailed. This paper therefore seeks to address this issue by discussing: (i) how C2 capability is currently developed; (ii) the distinction between design and emergence; and (iii) some of the challenges that need to be address by to improve C2 capability development. In summary, it seeks to stimulate discussion and, ultimately, agreement about what “C2 as a capability” means and entails as a step towards improving C2 capability development.

Title: Assurance in Context and Rigor for Employment of Autonomous Systems
Authors: Lange, D. S.; Coronado, B.; Gustafson, E.; Nagy, B.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: To make a claim of providing 'appropriate human judgement' or 'meaningful human control' of an autonomous system, a decision maker should be able to make reasonable predictions about the behavior of the subject system within the environments that it is being deployed. Assurance frameworks can provide the information needed for these predictions. Two frameworks are discussed. Assurance of Machine Learning for Autonomous Systems (AMLAS) and Level of Rigor (LOR). Each of these approach the assurance problem from a diGerent angle and provide diGerent information about the quality of the products being assured. A combination of the two provides additional information. In this paper, we discuss how the combination of AMLAS and LOR can contribute to the legal and ethical use of AI capabilities.

Title: An Open Architecture for Data Fusion
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: While the mathematics of fusion is quite general, every practical fusion engine must be tuned to a particular application to achieve acceptable performance with real data on tactical hardware. Tuning is typically achieved for a specific set of sensors and for specific track types of interest. For example, the fusion engine for a fifth-generation fighter is tuned for that aircraft’s sensors and for the types of air-to-air tracks it needs to engage. The result is a fusion engine which may perform very well in a bounded subset of the multidimensional data space generated by the parameters of the sensor measurements and the track attributes, while performing less efficiently or even failing in other parts of that data space. A joint all-domain command and control system requires addressing a much larger subset of the potential multidimensional data space than that processed by contemporary practical fusion engines. At the same time, there is pressure to re-use existing fusion engines in which a great deal has already been invested. This paper describes how multiple sensor fusion engines can be combined in an open architecture to provide the best overall estimate of the fused sensor track picture while managing the overall computational requirements. It describes how the component fusion engines can be calibrated, the open multi-fusion-engine framework adjusted to ensure that each component engine is fed the data for which it is best suited, and how the outputs from the various fusion engines can be correlated to deliver the final fused track picture.

Title: Plays C2: How to Achieve Network-Centric Warfare with Limited Information Distribution
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The motivating question for this paper is how to continue delivering the self-synchronisation and force effectiveness properties of the Edge C2 Approach when adversaries seek to limit information distribution. The Plays C2 Approach is proposed to address the requirements of broad allocation of decision rights, unconstrained patterns of interaction, while operating with limited distribution of information. The paper proposes a different way of performing sensemaking for the Plays C2 Approach that prioritises shared understanding over shared awareness when information distribution is limited. The implications of the Plays C2 Approach for C2 theory and Network-Centric Warfare theory are examined, proposing to extend the Network-Centric Maturity Model and increasing the variety in achieving the Quality of Collective Decisions.

Title: Effects of Information Distribution on Team Performance in a Dynamic Command and Control Environment with Familiar Teams
Authors: Xinyun Hu, Penny Sweetser, Guihyun Park, Linh Bui, Wesley McTernan
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The success of military and civilian Command and Control (C2) depends on the adaptability of C2 entities to swiftly respond to dynamic changes in complex situations. One facet proposed to enable this agility is the distribution of information in C2 networks. Past research has found that increased information distribution among team members can promote information sharing (IS) and therefore promote team performance, but it has also been shown that tailoring information to specific team roles can prevent cognitive  overload  and  improve  task  coordination.  Further,  as  operational  teams  often  have  pre-existing  team  familiarity, understanding the impact of information distribution on the effectiveness of familiar teams is essential for C2 optimisation. This study aimed to investigate the impact of information distribution (high condition: all team members receiving all information versus low condition: each member receiving information relevant to their role) on team effectiveness within familiar teams in a dynamic C2  environment.  Our  research  employed  three  rounds  of  simulated  firefighting  tasks  with  escalating  difficulty  in  the  C3Fire simulation, injecting social media information during the tactical operation of 43 four-member teams. Groups in the low condition demonstrated their advantage via quicker message acknowledgment and faster reactions to team decisions, as well as saving more critical objects in the most difficult round. Additionally, teams in the low condition outperformed those in the high condition by saving more critical objects when information load was low; however, this advantage diminished as information load increased. Our results suggest that distributing relevant information to familiar teams improves team effectiveness.

Title: The Machine-Speed C2 Human Agent Collective – a design pattern for transforming C2
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: This paper describes work that the UK Defence Science & Technology Laboratory has commissioned to answer the research question – can we design a Human Agent Collective (HAC) that allows C2 practitioners (Humans) and computers (Agents) to work together with clear collaboration principles combining the best qualities of both. This research has been applied in the context of Operational level C2 planning which deals with complex, human-centric problems focused on achieving a successful end-state. The HAC needs to be adaptable to a range of different C2 challenges while also being flexible to evolving technology. We have prototyped, generated some empirical data and demonstrated key enabling technologies while simultaneously evaluating the benefits of the HAC in selected use cases. A number of linked papers in ICCRTS24 describe aspects of this work in greater detail.

Title: Enhancing Operational Effectiveness through Capturing and Exchanging Capability Information between C2IS
Authors: Güldner, A.; Bau, N.; Gerz, M.; Kollmann, F.; Meyer, O.; Persie, C.; Dechand, M.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The success in mission-critical situations depends on a comprehensive understanding of both allied and hostile capabilities. Achieving information superiority across multiple domains in a multi-national setting underscores the pivotal role of interoperability in Command and Control Information Systems (C2IS). Beyond promoting Situational Awareness across heterogeneous C2IS, semantic interoperability holds the promise of enabling non-human entities and automated processes to integrate and operate seamlessly, minimising the need for human intervention to streamline real-time operations. The Multilateral Interoperability Programme (MIP) governs the development of the MIP Information Model (MIM), a semantic reference model that specifies the basis for exchange protocols and operational contents. The MIM has been adopted by the MIP4 Information Exchange Specification (MIP4-IES), which is used by Federated Mission Networking (FMN). Efforts continue to improve its harmonisation with NATO standards, reflecting its continuing relevance. Despite the comprehensive nature of the MIM, its representation of military capabilities is considered not to be in line with current operational procedures. This paper aims to analyse and identify symmetrically structured capabilities in the MIM that are not yet classified as such. Additionally, it proposes a new, streamlined, yet comprehensive semantic structure for capabilities that addresses current redundancies and ambiguities. These efforts aim to create a more coherent and functionally structured framework that enables the accurate and scalable transfer of information on friendly and hostile capabilities across heterogenous C2IS, thereby improving interoperability, consistency and accuracy of operational planning and decision-making processes.

Title: Opportunity Knocks: A Systems-Based Operational Level Planning Process © Aperture Strategy Ltd to Fully Embrace the Promise of Artificial Intelligence
Authors: Firth, A.; Poole, J.; Turner, P.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The pursuit of new technology to support the planning and execution of military operations continues to increase exponentially. The desire to process and analyse huge data sets at speed is matched only by the promise of technology's capability and appetite to do so ever more swiftly in an era when C2 is an aspiring capability rather than an enabling function. To some, Artificial Intelligence suggests even greater reward, disruptively moving the dial from collation and analysis to rationale and reason. This paper explores the socio-technological interface surfaced by research into AI support to operational-level planning within Dstl's Machine Speed C2 project. In doing so it argues that current military doctrine in the Anglo-sphere continues to encourage a mechanistic and linear approach to operational level planning. The paper argues that the art of strategy design to cope with complexity and uncertainty is already dangerously neglected in doctrine and that the direct application of technology risks reinforcement by a 'tool -box' approach to decision support. AI offers a significant opportunity to correct this failing not least by broadening and deepening the perspectives and horizon of the planning process, but to do so it needs to support a planning process that is fit-for-purpose to address the challenging adaptive systems of the contemporary operating environment. There is no doubt that the rapidly increasing capabilities of advanced technology provide an advantage in horizontal integration at the tactical 'sensor -toshooter' level. But there will be little cognitive advantage in realising vertical (strategic to tactical) coherence unless AI plugs into a more iterative and agile C2 framework and planning process. Bringing together observations made during the MSC2 programme and leading‑edge work in the field of systems thinking, the paper recommends a revised approach to the operational level planning process that offers to integrate AI support with the greatest effect. The research reported on in this paper was either funded by, or is related to, the UK MOD Machine Speed Command and Control (MSC2) project. This project was part of the UK Defence Science and Technology Laboratory's (Dstl) AI Programme with the intent to transform C2 by enabling more timely and effective C2 processes across all environments, domains and levels of command, so the Defence enterprise can anticipate and adapt more successfully than adversaries. This paper is one of six presented at the 29th ICCRTS which document different aspects of the MSC2 project which explored the feasibility of a Human Agent Collective (HAC) that combines human insight with machine speed AI agents employing shared digital artefacts, shifting C2 from human teams to human‑machine teams, where humans and AI work together.

Title: Using Decisive Conditions as a Tool in Operational Planning - Definition, Guidelines and Developing a DC 'Agent'
Authors: Firth, A.; Attfield, S.; Asquith, R.; Poole, J.; Forsey, A.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract:
Current doctrine defines the Operational Level of engagement as, ' The level at which campaigns and major operations are planned, conducted, and sustained to accomplish strategic objectives within theatres or areas of operations ' [1,  Lex14].  Its  purpose  and function is critical in translating strategic objectives into tactical activity and managing the development of a military campaign. It is perhaps therefore most prominently at this level that the full challenge of complexity and uncertainty is negotiated. In order to manage this translation, planners develop and integrate 'concepts for operational design'. One of the most prominent of these, perhaps the most important in binding the design together, is a framework of Decisive Conditions.

A Decisive Condition (DC) is defined as, ' A combination of circumstances, effects, or a specific key event, critical factor, or function that, when realized, allows commanders to gain a marked advantage over an opponent or contribute materially to achieving an operational objective ' [1, para 3.12). Doctrine provides some guidance as to how DCs might be derived including that they, ' are logically determined from the factor and CoG Analysis processes ' [1,  para  4.26], but the process -and indeed the product -still leaves much to the interpretation of individuals and organisations.

As part of a current project exploring ways in which Artificial Intelligence might offer support to the Operational Level  Planning P rocess (OPP), a characterisation was developed of the derivation and nature of 'good' DCs in the form of a set of guidelines. Two experienced military planners with a combined total of over forty years' experience of the OPP (who are also co -authors of this paper) were interviewed to elicit these guidelines. The interview was grounded in the review of some example DCs that ranged in quality. The conversation was recorded and analysed and ultimately reduced to sixteen guidelines that were reviewed and adjusted by the planners. The guidelines relate to factors including: structure, syntax, tense, content, verifiability, the promotion of mental models, and relationships with other key concepts such as the Operational End State (OES) and Operational Centre of Gravity (CoG).

Once derived, the guidelines were used in combination with other material to construct prompts as input to a Large Language Model (LLM) to perform the functions of: generating DC titles, generating DC body texts and conducting assessments of DCs from the point of view of their structure, relevance to the OES, overlap and coverage of the OES.

Title: A Conceptual Framework for C2 Capability Lifecycle Governance: Integrating Time, Context, and Organizational Evolution
Authors: Wikberg, P.; Kalloniatis, A.; Dekker, R.; Granåsen, M.; Bryant, D.; Gaertner, P.; Hill, J.; Jobidon, M.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: This paper presents a conceptual framework for Command and Control (C2) Capability Lifecycle Governance, developed as part  of  NATO  HFM-RTG  342.  The  framework  aims  to  guide  the  planning,  maintenance,  and evolution of C2 capabilities from a  lifecycle perspective. First, we provide an overview of some key terms including C2, capability, lifecycle, and governance, drawing from NATO policy and scientific literature. Then we outline seven propositions for C2 capability development over time. These propositions are combined into a conceptual framework depicting how successive organizational generations adapt C2 capabilities to evolving security environments. The framework includes key activities like projects, capability lifecycle management, and C2 itself. Based on this framework, we propose a working definition of C2 Capability Lifecycle Governance. Finally, we discuss implications for developing governance guidelines, including the need to enable network solutions  and  principal-agent  approaches,  communicate  values  and  cultural  aspects  beyond  formal  rules, address cultural variations within NATO, and remain adaptive to geopolitical and technological changes.

Title: C2 Harmonisation Arrangements for Entities with Heterogeneous C2 Approaches
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Current C2 Theory defines the set of C2 Harmonisation Arrangements between organisational entities in Planning Timeframes. This paper examines C2 Harmonisation Arrangements in endeavours with high volatility dynamics. In particular, it focuses on how C2 Approaches change in Executing Timeframes, and the implications for C2 Harmonisation Arrangements. Alternate decision and task allocations is proposed as the key addition for both the orchestration and boundary-spanning dimensions of C2 Harmonisation Arrangements to support endeavours with high volatility dynamics. Three types of decision and task allocations are presented, and their applicability to the set of C2 Approaches is defined. The possible set  of state transitions between C2 Approaches and the requirements to achieve them are presented.

Title: A challenging case for Artificial Intelligence: evaluation of the benefits of AI-enabled socio-technical concept solutions for Operational-level planning
Authors: Turner, P.; Leggatt, A.; Roberts, H.; Ellis, R.; Attfield, S.; Jones, A.; McMillan, M.; Asquith, R.; Forsey, A.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The UK Defence Science and Technology Laboratory’s Machine Speed Command and Control (MSC2) project aims to support the transformation of C2 capability through the adoption of artificial intelligence (AI) and exploitation of data.  MSC2 has developed several functional concepts for Operational-level planning, each of which translates into one or more socio-technical concept solutions, comprising humans and AI agents performing purposeful planning functions collectively and through interactions, supported by data.  This paper describes the formative evaluation of two such concept solutions: the Stakeholder Mapper (a way of developing understanding of multiple actor perspectives and potential responses) and Support to Operational Design (a way of generating Decisive Conditions as part of Operational Design).  The multi-disciplinary research team, comprising C2 researchers, ex-military planners and AI developers, employed socio-technical evaluation methods, to enable early, formative evaluation of potential C2 benefits of the concept solutions and thereby shape requirements for AI development, before major development resources have been committed.  We summarise the C2 benefits of the concept solutions and draw conclusions for the parent concepts, the evaluation approach and the broader goal of MSC2 to support the transformation of C2 capability.

Title: C2 Sandbox - Testing and Validating New C2 Concepts
Authors: Abdelazez, M.; Robb, A.; Ghanmi, A.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Wargaming has become a crucial part of modern defence strategies. It involves simulated conflict scenarios that provide valuable insights  into  organizational  structures,  procedures,  and decision-making  processes.  Wargames offer numerous benefits, such as enhanced planning, improved communication, realistic training, and effective decision-making. Pan Domain Command and Control (PDC2) is the next generation of command and control systems for joint military operations. It allows real-time information sharing and collaboration across all domains, including land, sea, air, space, information and cyber. Given today's multi-domain battlespace's complexity and dynamic nature, wargaming PDC2 is crucial to test, evaluate, and refine its capabilities in realistic scenarios. It also ensures that there is an effective response to evolving threats. This paper discusses an ongoing wargame development effort focusing on evaluating PDC2. The discussion highlights the importance of wargaming in maintaining preparedness for real-world challenges and provides an insight into the development of PDC2 wargames.
