Title: Extended Reality for Multi-Domain Operations
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Pan Domain Command and Control (PDC2) enables seamless command and control across all domains - land, sea, air, space, and cyber. The complexity of modern battlefields demands advanced tools to help commanders effectively navigate and manage these dynamic environments. Enter eXtended Reality (XR), a technology that merges physical and virtual worlds, providing users with immersive and interactive experiences in actual or simulated environments. XR integrates elements of augmented reality (AR), virtual reality (VR), and mixed reality (MR). XR is poised to revolutionize PDC2 through enhanced situational awareness, improved decisionmaking, effective communication, and realistic training. This paper focuses on examining XR's use in C2 decision-making processes. It includes a detailed discussion of the available XR technologies and highlights the potential benefits and risks associated with their use. The paper also identifies possible applications of XR in the field of PDC2, outlines the criteria that can be used to evaluate XR technologies, and includes a survey of current XR-related projects in C2 across various nations.

Title: 055 – Harmony from Within
Authors: Morton, C.; Shigley, P.; Tanner, R.; Galdorisi, G.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: When most people hear the words “command and control” their thoughts immediately turn to warfare between nation states. This is a natural phenomenon. Indeed, there is compelling evidence that Russia’s invasion of Ukraine was hampered by extremely poor command and control mechanisms. However, there is also a need for command and control harmonization within organizations. Simply put, when an external crisis presents itself, an organization must either deal with this event using current routines or create new routines, and new ways to communicate desired actions when previous routines are no longer relevant. Most U.S. government organizations have had embedded routines for decades. Therefore, the challenge of creating new routines is especially acute in these organizations for a plethora of reasons. If new routines need to be created, organizational barriers to agility will need to be overcome. The changes that must be overcome include overcoming knowledge silos, the implementation of decision rights, the development of shared awareness, and new methodologies of synchronizing actions. This concept paper will explore how one large U.S. government organization, with over five thousand employees, created temporary routines during the COVID-19 pandemic. This external forcing function presents an extreme example of the need for organizations to rapidly adapt to an external change. Rather than present something “aspirational” or “hypothetical,” this paper will discuss both the challenges and opportunities that occur when organizations make a dramatic change and adopt temporary organizational routines. Our examination of temporary routine creation in response to the pandemic will contribute to the growing body of work on organizational routines and routine dynamics and will help organizations adapt and resume normal operations during and after crisis, particularly when external change demands new ways of getting things done.

Title: Assessing novel C2 socio-technical AI enabled planning concepts: What we learnt using the Wizard of Oz and other evaluation methods
Authors: Leggatt, A.; Turner, P.; Asquith, R.; Attfield, S.; Roberts, H.; Ellis, R.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract:
This paper describes research conducted for Dstl UK MOD in which the team developed 65 novel C2 concepts that support Operational-Level Planning. This work is specifically aimed to create and assess novel C2 socio-technical concepts that harness AI (Artificial Intelligence) to improve the speed and quality of Operational Level Planning.

We describe the multi-disciplinary collaborative assessment processes that supported the development of the 65 novel planning concepts that are enabled by AI. A range of assessment methods were used that included expert review, laboratory and field assessment methods.

The paper will describe both the review of methods and their application and assessment C2 socio-technical concept solutions and describe the benefits and drawbacks of the specific assessment approaches. Finally, the paper briefly discusses some of the benefits from using these different methods and the insights gained in their application to increase the speed and quality of C2 AI concept solutions.

Title: Cyber effects analysis and characterization
Authors: Nakhla, N.; Tummers, M.; Watson, S.; Sabbagh El-Rami, J.-P.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract:
With the ever increasing and evolving reliance on the cyber environment, it is of interest to characterize the performance and impact of cyber offensive actions. Unlike traditional kinetic operations, which are, in general, defined and bound by physical properties, characterizing and examining the behaviour of non-lethal effects such as cyber, can be a relatively difficult task. This is due to several factors, namely, the fact that cyber operations are less observable than kinetic and can result in a lack of immediate, quantifiable situational awareness and feedback once the action has been launched. Also, the complex and interconnected nature of the cyber environment means that care must be taken to ensure there are no unintended collateral effects beyond the intended target.

To address these issues, in this work, we developed a cyber effects analysis methodology for offensive operations in enterprise networks. We leveraged the Defence Research and Development Canada (DRDC)developed Cyber Gym for Intelligent Learning (CyGIL) environment to execute cyber operations by autonomous agents in an emulated network. By executing optimal attack paths (identified by reinforcement learning), we collected metrics on effects measures of performance (MOPs), such as success likelihood and covertness. We developed a sensitivity analysis approach to characterize the sensitivity of attack MOPs with respect to scenario and environmental factors, such as reachability and variations in network traffic, to mimic operational environments. The results provided in this paper provide insight into the processes and requirements for effective cyber effects analysis and demonstrate the risks and drawbacks associated with certain cyber operations. These findings can also be used to help improve the performance of weak or inconsistent cyber actions. At a higher level, the outcomes of this work could support weaponeering in the joint targeting cycle or could be tailored to inform decision makers for effective offensive cyber operations. Lessons learned were captured based on the experimental analysis conducted and recommendations are provided for similar research activities. Future efforts involve applying the methodology to analyze effects delivered via the radio frequency (RF) domain.

Title: Overmatch Software Armory: Overcoming Barriers to Deployment of Modern Naval C2 Software
Authors: Johnson, C.; George, A.; Jenkins, D.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Modern naval command and control (C2) requires software to enable the rapid ingestion, synchronization, and understanding of a multitude of data required to inform naval activities. The naval C2 environment is both highly complex and extremely critical, requiring the Navy C2 systems and software to rapidly react to mission needs to its ships at sea in bandwidth challenged environments. Given the vital nature of C2 software in the modern battlefield, it is necessary for the military enterprises to prioritize the rapid development and delivery of software. This will require a paradigm shift. For decades software delivery to US Navy ships occurred by engineers and scientist packing up volumes of CD’s or 4MM tapes, in some cases scheduling airfare to fly to wherever the ship may be. Weeks would be spent aboard the platform loading the latest version of software, testing it out to ensure everything functioned properly, and then going through a turnover with ships company for acceptance once everything was up and functional. NIWC Pacific has changed that paradigm by realizing the promise of DevSecOps for rapid and responsive software development, and “Over the Air” (OTA) delivery of new software and version updates to Navy ships. Changes to methods of software development from the traditional “waterfall model” to “the iterative model” then the “spiral model” to “agile”, and now “DevSecOps” have evolved the process of software delivery to provide rapid capability advancements to the warfighters wherever they are, whenever they need it. Additional designs and development patterns have changed from monolithic large bang applications to a smaller micro-service architecture where containers and smaller size applications are delivered enabling more efficient use of the limited bandwidth that ships are forced to operate with. NIWC Pacific has responded to this challenge by creating a robust DevSecOps environment and methods of deploying software “Over the Air” (OTA), with limited to no onboard support for installation. It also provides for the possibility of rolling back to a previous version should a new install prove problematic. The challenge of delivering rapidly responsive software capability OTA for C2 has provided many lessons learned in overcoming challenges that are applicable to the weapons systems software development community. This paper details the creation of the DevSecOps environment, the Overmatch Software Armory, that provides Command and Control (C2) capability to ships and submarines across the US Navy and provides a set of lessons learned for the community to utilize in overcoming common barriers to C2 software development and deployment across US military services and with international partners. Additionally, this paper proposed a set of metrics to measure the progress of the paradigm change and the benefits of utilizing the Overmatch Software Armory to rapidly deploy C2 software over the air.

Title: Examining C2 Manouevre Agility Responsiveness Requirement
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Current C2 Theory does not define the responsiveness requirements of C2 Manoeuvre Agility. The Plays C2 Approach is a counter to adversaries  seeking  to  limit  information  distribution.  Increasing  the  variety  of  the  C2  Approach  Space  by  adding  the  Plays  C2 Approach  provides  an  opportunity  to  examine  the  responsiveness  requirements  of  C2  Manoeuvre  Agility.  Two  types  of responsiveness requirements for C2 Manoeuvre Agility are defined: responsiveness in Planning Timeframes and responsiveness in Executing  Timeframes.  The  four  C2  functions  are  mapped  to  these  two  types  of  responsiveness  requirements.  While  the responsiveness in Planning Timeframes changes all four C2 functions, responsiveness in Executing Timeframes always occurs within the mission objectives context and only changes three C2 functions. The decision support mechanisms that enable transition to each C2  Approach  are  articulated  for  both  planning-based  mechanisms  and  Decider-based  mechanisms.  The  mapping  of  transitions between different C2 Approaches is described, showing which transitions are possible in responsiveness in Planning Timeframes and responsiveness in Executing Timeframes, and thus defining the C2 Agility of each C2 Approach. The key to satisfying C2 Manouevre Agility  for  responsiveness  in  Executing  timeframes  is  defining  alternative  decision  and  task  allocations  in  the  shared  plan  and implementing, ready for activation, the alternative C2 Approach Decider-based decision support mechanisms.

Title: Mobile Ad Hoc Networks in a Zero Trust Architecture for the Tactical Edge
Authors: Oshiro, D.; Shaffer, A.; Singh, G.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Mobile Ad Hoc Networks (MANETs) are resilient and responsive networks capable of supporting the unique requirements at the tactical edge. MANETs can provide critical support at the tactical edge of military operations but the current Department of Defense (DOD) strategy for Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA) does not include guidelines for MANET implementation. The connectivity between users, devices, sensors, and shooting platforms in the information-age battlefield increases the attack surface for adversaries to penetrate these forward-edge networks. Additionally, solutions at the forward edge must be sensitive to the considerations of bandwidth, processing, and power consumption. In this paper, we introduce a set of ZTA requirements that complement MANET secure routing protocols and intrusion detection systems. We survey several MANET routing protocols to identify ones that can be augmented to achieve zero trust principles in the network. This survey leads to identifying the Zone-Based Intrusion Detection System (ZBIDS) running on a hybrid routing protocol as a security solution that can be incorporated into a ZTA.

Title: Cyber C2: Achieving Scrutability and Agency in Cyberspace Operations
Authors: Salmond, D.; Nguyen, V.; Uzunov, A. V.; Nikolova, N.; Desai, P.; Kyprianou, R.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Our thesis is that operating in cyberspace is challenging because cyberspace exhibits extreme variety, high malleability, and extreme velocity. These properties make cyberspace largely inscrutable and limits one's agency in cyberspace, where agency is the ability to exert influence to transform the state or behaviour of the environment. With this thesis, we explore the nature of cyberspace, C2 and diagnosis of the challenges for cyber C2, with treatment to follow in future work. For the C2 expert unfamiliar with cyber, we consider definitions of cyberspace within military doctrine and the open literature. For the purposes of the paper, we adopt a definition that emphasises the digitised nature of cyberspace, admits human behaviours that influence or are influenced by cyberspace, includes logical electromagnetic interactions by radios, and includes the quality of being self-referent in that acting upon cyberspace can create and destroy cyberspace. Similarly, for the cyber expert unfamiliar with C2, we explore the different interpretations of C2 within both doctrine and the research community. We note the increasing realisation that extant C2 approaches are not fit-for-purpose for increasingly complex operations, which are increasingly complex because of the interconnectedness afforded by cyberspace. The unique challenges for the C2 of cyberspace are a consequence of the variety, malleability and velocity of cyberspace and lead to inscrutability and loss of agency. There is a clear need for structures, processes and technologies with the requisite variety, malleability and velocity to improve scrutability and maximise agency in cyberspace. Whilst international research efforts are underway to build better models and tools for doing so, we believe that by addressing variety, malleability and velocity explicitly, we lay a foundation for the development of control structures that maximises agency.
Author Keywords: CYBER COMMAND AND CONTROL; VARIETY CALCULUS; CYBERSPACE SCRUTABILITY; AGENCY IN CYBER OPERATIONS; MALLEABILITY AND VELOCITY OF CYBERSPACE; AUTONOMOUS CYBER DEFENSE AGENTS; MULTIDOMAIN MILITARY DOCTRINE; INFORMATION WARFARE MODELING

Title: Enhancing CADET Analysis with Large Language Model Technologies in C2 Operations SubTitle: Leveraging LLM to Augment CADET in Operational Data Analysis
Authors: Devereaux, Z. P.; Forrester, B. C.; Labrie, M.-A.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Understanding large and complex operational data sets derived from contemporary media is an exceedingly perplexing task. The Cluster Analysis and Data Exploration Toolset called CADET (Labrie, 2023) has been used to sort unstructured text into topics, clusters, sentiments, and named-entities, via vector-embedding and machine-learning (ML). CADET provides the analyst with a detailed overview of the dataset from which an initial understanding is obtained. This paper explores how new large language model (LLM) technologies can be used to enhance CADET analysis for data obtained through operations in the information environment (OIE). This paper explains how CADET + LLM can help answer several typical information environment assessment (IEA) problems with sight-unseen data such as: cluster labelling, hashtag explanation, location inference, and content analysis. Further, the impact of CADET + LLM on OIE wargaming and synthetic data analysis for training purposes is explored. We conclude that CADET + LLM enhances the available analytics used for understanding large social-based datasets, especially with converting unknown unknowns in unstructured text data into known knowns.

Title: Towards a Theory of Integration
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Integration is a key concept in contemporary military affairs that expresses a need for closer working between military capabilities, military and non-military organisations, across domains, and between technological and human systems. However, doctrine rarely discusses how to integrate or its implications. This paper will therefore explore the concept in more detail and will begin to develop a theoretical foundation for integration using concepts from the Variety Calculus, a systems thinking methodology based on cybernetics. This provides a lens through which to view integration, interoperability, cohesion and control to provide a means of envisaging and designing effective organisations, operations, and C2 systems. It conceives of integration as 'the act of forming a system', and it acknowledges that various types of relationship between participants are appropriate under different circumstances. Requisite cohesion expresses the need for balance between tight relationships to enable purposefulness and control, and looser constructs required to address complex situations and to enable innovation, adaptation, and resilience. The Integration Matrix is a 3-dimensional space that equates the simplicity/complexity of the situation to the appropriate characteristics of the integrated enterprise in terms of the diversity participants can express, level of cohesion between them, and the degree of control that is possible over it.

Title: The Sensemaking Assessment Questionnaire: A Tool for Conducting Summative Assessment of Collaborative Sensemaking Environments.
Authors: Turner, N.; Leggatt, A.; Gibson, W. H.; Raywood-Burke, G.; Attfield, S.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Sensemaking is a crucial concept in the context of command and control (C2). It helps leaders and teams interpret and understand complex situations by constructing meaningful narratives from disparate pieces of information and is critical for making informed decisions. It is important to study sensemaking within its natural environment to, (a) understand how it is achieved and the factors that affect it, (b) what design changes improve sensemaking outcomes in any given context. To date, studies of sensemaking have been good at answering the first question with rich, qualitative data. However, quantitative evaluation relies on having suitable measures. The aim of this work was to create an instrument with a theoretically sound factor structure that could support the measurement of distributed sensemaking in complex situations. Items were taken from an existing measure of individual sensemaking [1] and more were generated based on Klein’s Data-Frame Theory [2]. Data were collected to explore the factor structure and reduce items to form the Sensemaking Assessment Questionnaire (SMAQ). The final model has three factors: constructing frames, applying existing frames, and achieving functional understanding. Reliability and validity assessments were conducted and the items were reviewed by a panel of Human Factors experts and ex-military staff and adjusted accordingly. There is a full version of the instrument with twenty-two items and a shortened version with twelve. SMAQ has since been applied to measure distributed sensemaking quality in a C2 experiment and was sensitive enough to detect a difference in sensemaking quality as teams practiced the task [3].

Title: Command and Control in the Future - Operations Coordenation Approach
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The Operations Coordination Approach (OCA) represents a departure from traditional Command and Control (C2) structures in modern military operations, addressing the challenges and complexities inherent in multidomain operations (MDO). OCA emphasizes orchestration, coordination, collaboration, and adaptability, providing a more flexible and resilient framework for managing MDO. This new approach is essential for military effectiveness in a rapidly evolving global environment, where diverse stakeholders and complex challenges necessitate a more agile and responsive command structure. The OCA model acknowledges the need for military leaders to adopt a more diplomatic, people-centric skill set, working effectively with diverse partners, including civilians, industry, and academia. Furthermore, OCA highlights the importance of investing in education and training to develop 'OCA Commanders', individuals with the necessary soft skills, emotional intelligence, and cultural understanding to thrive within modern military operations.

Title: Enhancing Common Ground in a Command and Control Task
Authors: Leggatt, A.; Gibson, W. H.; Attfield, S.; Raywood-Burke, G.; Turner, N.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract:
Distributed sensemaking (DSM) is a process by which humans, teams of humans, or humans and agents/machines share and interpret information in an effort to explain, understand, or otherwise make sense of a situation. Common ground is essential for coordination and cohesion during DSM since team members need mutually consistent understandings of the situation at hand, shared and individual goals in relation to that situation, and their strategies for achieving them.

An experiment was conducted to assess two methods of enhancing common ground in a collaborative C2 task and their effect on the quality of DSM. Groups of three participants collaborated in the C3Fire simulation environment. Two manipulations were used to affect common ground. One involved changing the extent to which positions of units under the control of each participant and locations of fires were included in a shared (plan) view of the simulation environment (situational common ground). The other involved half of the groups conducting structured sessions between experimental runs to discuss aspects of planning and acting that they found to be important for the activity (cultural common ground).

The results showed that the situational common ground manipulation significantly improved participants’ perceptions of the quality of DSM as well as measures of task performance under certain conditions. The results also suggest that the cultural common ground intervention can enhance DSM. These differences were non-significant but participant numbers were low. In general, the results provide good evidence that strategies that increase common ground in C2 also improve the quality of DSM.

Title: Data-Driven Distributed Common Operational Picture from Heterogenous Platforms using Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
Authors: Sur, I.; Raghavan, A.; Rahman, A.; Hare, J. Z.; Cassenti, D.; Busart, C.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The integration of unmanned platforms equipped with advanced sensors promises to enhance situational awareness and mitigate the 'fog of war' in military operations. However, managing the vast influx of data from these platforms poses a significant c hallenge for Command and Control (C2) systems. This study presents a novel multi-agent learning framework to address this challenge. Our method enables autonomous and secure communication between agents and humans, which in turn enables real-time formation of an interpretable Common Operational Picture (COP). Each agent encodes its perceptions and actions into compact vectors, which are then transmitted, received and decoded to form a COP encompassing the current state of all agents (friendly and enemy) on the battlefield.  Using  Deep  Reinforcement  Learning  (DRL),  we  jointly  train  COP  models  and  agent 's  action  selection policies.  We demonstrate  resilience  to  degraded  conditions  such  as  denied  GPS  and  disrupted  communications.  Experimental  validation  is performed in the Starcraft-2 simulation environment to evaluate the precision of the COPs and robustness of policies. We report less than 5% error in COPs and policies resilient to various adversarial conditions. In summary, our contributions include a method for autonomous COP formation, increased resilience through distributed prediction, and joint training of COP models and multi-agent RL policies. This research advances adaptive and resilient C2, facilitating effective control of heterogeneous unmanned platforms.

Title: Building Cyber Warfare Capability: A Phased Approach to Integrating Cyber Operations into Military Structures
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: As the cyber domain increasingly becomes a critical battlefield, modern militaries must develop the capacity to conduct operations in and through the cyberspace. This paper presents a systematic approach to establishing cyber warfare capabilities within existing operational structures, offering a pathway for militaries to incrementally build and enhance their cyber capabilities. The approach emphasizes the integration of cyber warfare into traditional military operations, ensuring that new capabilities are developed in alignment with existing doctrines and structures. By proposing a phased methodology, the paper guides military organizations in creating appropriate command and control structures, training regimes, and technological frameworks to exploit cyberspace effectively. The focus is on developing a scalable and flexible cyber force that can adapt to evolving threats and leverage cyberspace for both defensive and offensive operations.

Title: Modelling Arguments and Battle-space Entities in C2 Planning
Authors: Fletcher, M.; Evett, C.; Visser, J.; Sharma, S.
Source title: 29th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The Military Motivation for this conceptual paper is, as several UK military bodies including the MOD’s Air and Space Warfare Centre have testified, that military planning is hard due to the complexity and dynamics of the battle-space being considered. Being able to plan more efficiently and effectively, taking into account more variables, while being prompted about potential weaknesses in the plan would be highly valuable. This work seeks to develop the foundations that would enable entirely new classes of powerful computer-based planning support tools to be employed by HQ staff during planning in all phases of conflict (from competition through crisis to conflict).

Title: Cyber Risk to Mission Analysis Overview
Authors: Alberts, D. S.; McEver, J. G.
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Over the past several decades, the networking and digitization of military forces have dramatically increased their effectiveness, lethality, efficiency, and agility. However, this growing reliance on cyber capabilities introduces a significant and inherent Cyber Risk to Mission (CRM). This paper presents an analysis methodology and a set of metrics to understand, assess, and manage this cyber-related risk to force and mission, which can result from a loss of capabilities due to external attack, internal error, or system failure. The methodology is patterned after the Joint Risk Assessment Methodology (JRAM) and consists of two inter-dependent components: a \cyber risk to system\" assessment and a resulting \"risk to mission\" assessment. The paper highlights key factors—such as the transformative power of cyber

Title: CRM C2
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The ability to successfully conduct military operations operate in Contested Cyberspace Environments is an existential capability in the 21st Century.   A failure to effectively and efficiently manage Cyber Risk to Mission (CRM) has a significant adverse impact on the mission.   This paper explores selected multi-domain approaches to CRM C2 to determine if CRM C2 Arrangements significantly impact Cyber Risk to Mission.  The implications of the experimental findings are briefly discussed.

Title: Efficient Digital Voice and Chat Communications through Generative AI at the Tactical Edge
Authors: Manso, M.; Jansen, N.; Johnsen, F. T.; Andersen, E. P.
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract:
In this paper, we aim to improve chat and audio services in Disconnected, Intermittent and Limited (DIL) environments. Putting emphasis on the tactical edge and inspired by the NATO Federated Mission Networking (FMN) initiative, we apply artificial intelligence (AI) to improve these collaboration services.

The paper discusses the design and architecture of such next-generation collaboration services, where AI empowers the application to provide text-to-speech and speech-to-text capabilities, connected via an efficient message distribution mechanism. To achieve a realistic and immersive communication experience, we introduce generative AI on the receiver side to reconstruct audio messages with characteristics similar to the original speaker. Our experimental results show that this approach significantly reduces the strain on tactical networks by lowering data rate requirements-achieving up to /nine.pnum.oldstyle/three.pnum.oldstyle./seven.pnum.oldstyle% reduction in transmitted media data compared to traditional voice codecs, and /seven.pnum.oldstyle/two.pnum.oldstyle% reduction when including signaling overhead-while maintaining low end-to-end latency, typically under two seconds.

This work has been conducted as part of the NATO research task group IST-/two.pnum.oldstyle/zero.pnum.oldstyle/one.pnum.oldstyle.
Author Keywords: TACTICAL EDGE COMMUNICATIONS; DISCONNECTED INTERMITTENT LIMITED (DIL) NETWORKS; GENERATIVE AI FOR SPEECH SYNTHESIS; SPEECH-TO-TEXT (STT) AND TEXT-TO-SPEECH (TTS) PIPELINES; MQTT OVER UDP MESSAGE DISTRIBUTION; LOW-BANDWIDTH AUDIO CODECS; ZERO-SHOT SPEAKER ADAPTATION; NATO FEDERATED MISSION NETWORKING (FMN)

Title: Towards Whole-stack Optimization: Leveraging Edge Computing for Next-Generation Tactical Systems
Authors: Opland, A. F.; Andersen, E. P.; Landmark, L.; Johnsen, F. T.
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: As technological advancements accelerate, the future of Command and Control (C2) systems increasingly relies on integrating edge computing to optimize next-generation tactical systems. Modern warfare demands timely decisionmaking and data processing, and edge computing by processing data closer to the point of action, offers significant improvements in efficiency and speed. However, fully leveraging this paradigm requires optimizing the entire technology stack, including hardware, software, network protocols, and data management. This paper proposes a whole-stack optimization approach that harmonizes these components to ensure faster, more accurate decision-making and secure, resilient operations. As part of the symposium theme 'C2 Past, Present, Future,' this paper explores how next-generation tactical edge systems can overcome current limitations by optimizing the entire stack. The integration of edge computing with advancements in artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning will play a significant role in enhancing decision-making and operational effectiveness. By embracing continuous innovation and system optimization, future C2 systems will better support decision-makers on the frontlines, enabling faster, more resilient responses in modern combat scenarios.
Author Keywords: WHOLE-STACK OPTIMIZATION; EDGE COMPUTING; TACTICAL EDGE ENVIRONMENTS; EDGE AI; SOFTWARE-DEFINED NETWORKING; CROSS-LAYER OPTIMIZATION; ADAPTIVE NETWORKING; VALUE OF INFORMATION

Title: Generic Method for Self-Assessment of C2 Capability
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Traditionally, command and control (C2) assessments often focus on the quantification of deficiencies and malfunctions. A key challenge with this approach is that measures may be limited to what is readily quantifiable, rather than what is most relevant to operational effectiveness. Focusing on deficiencies may also reduce evaluated participants' trust in the evaluators, and thus hinder learning. A study was performed with the objective to test a C2 assessment method, based on prior work with generic templates for evaluating civilian crisis management capabilities. The method was evaluated through an assessment conducted during a military C2 exercise. Since the exercise was limited to planning procedures, the assessment could only address planning capability. The assessment was conducted through a moderated discussion among participating staff members, using questions from a generic template. However, the method is intended to enable self-assessment without external support. The staff members found that the method contributed to constructive discussions, potentially improving C2 capability. Although the assessment addressed only one capability, the staff members suggested that assessments should focus on the most important capabilities, since time for evaluations is often limited. In summary, this test demonstrated the method's potential to improve assessments of C2 exercises by generating knowledge that can be used to strengthen C2 capability. Moreover, self-assessment without external assessors may enhance the acceptance of results and their use to improve C2 capability. However, the method remains under development and needs further testing.

Title: e-STEP: Scenario-based-design for autonomous systems
Authors: Gideskog, M.; Robertsson, E.; Green, A.
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The e-STEP methodology was introduced in workshops with the Swedish Armed Forces as a novel and exploratory approach to the traditional STEP framework commonly used for retrospective accident investigation. Earlier work has demonstrated that STEP can be suitable for concept development in the military domain. By further developing the method, hereafter referred to as the e-STEP method, we enhanced it through the involvement of domain experts for exploratory conceptualization and design development of future autonomous systems with varying levels of technology readiness. The key observation and argument for using e-STEP diagrams in this manner, is that its visually represented diagrams appears to suit the officers and specialists who are engaged in the workshops. We could see that they quickly adapted and appeared comfortable in the work progress and by collaborating, officers and researchers, were together able to create diagrams and scenarios to use as practically testable templates in military contexts as well as input for further development of software for autonomy. The practice allowed the workshop participants to understand their own processes before considering the organizational and tactical ramifications of introducing a new technological system.

Title: Using Cognitive Work Analysis to Improve Military Doctrines
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: In response to the deteriorating security situation there is presently a rapid growth and development within the Swedish Armed Forces. New domains for warfare and territorial conflicts such as the cyber domain and space are getting more prominent, and technical systems gain increasing artificial intelligence and autonomy. Sweden is, as other nations, in the process of figuring out how to perform multidomain operation. An important part of this process is the harmonisation of doctrines. Sweden is also a new member of NATO, which requires analyses of correspondences between national and NATO doctrines. The Swedish Air Force has developed an air defence concept to meet the new challenges. This concept needs to be implemented in doctrine as well as technical-tactical development. Cognitive Work Analysis (CWA) is a framework for the analysis, design, and evaluation of complex sociotechnical systems. The analysis focuses on the work the system is expected to perform, which enables considerations regarding work organisation and allocation of responsibilities to human or artificial agents. The CWA framework has been applied to a wide range of domains, while of particular interest for the present project is the application of CWA by Neelam Naikar and colleagues for the improvement of doctrines for the Royal Australian Air Force. The envisioned project aim is to apply the CWA framework to the development of doctrines for the Swedish Air Force. While inspired by Naikar's approach, the CWA framework will be applied somewhat differently, in the hope of contributing to developing it further.

Title: SEAGUARD: An Interoperability Framework for Maritime Border Security involving Unmanned Platforms
Authors: Marco Manso;Bruno Miguel Ferreira;Ioannis Chatzichristos;Bárbara Guerra;Nuno Miguel Abreu;Fabio Augusto de Alcantara Andrade
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The SEAGUARD concept addresses a multi-domain (air, sea, underwater) maritime surveillance approach, involving the deployment, management and coordination of heterogeneous platforms, sensors and information technologies.  SEAGUARD's aim is to deliver a high  level  of  situational  awareness  through  a  holistic  surveillance  system  fitted  to  the  needs  and  ambition  of  modern  border management  authorities.  The  operational  context  of  large  maritime  areas  and  the  nature  of  threats  -  increasingly  dynamic, transnational  and  highly  mobile  -  reflect  the  growing  need  to  have  multiple  and  different  types  of  authorities  involved  in  and coordinating response efforts so that, working together, their common goals are achieved, with superior efficiency and effectiveness. Attaining the SEAGUARD vision requires a high level of interoperability between the diverse and heterogeneous participating entities (organizations, units, people) and technological systems (unmanned platforms and smart devices) in a collective working in a civilmilitary, cross-organisation and cross-border environment.  To enable this advanced synchronization, the SEAGUARD Interoperability Framework (S.IF) implements a set of Command and Control (C2) rules and protocols among participating entities, benefitting from NATO C2 Approaches as foundational references for its novel interoperability approach.
Author Keywords: MARITIME BORDER SURVEILLANCE; MULTI-DOMAIN UNMANNED SYSTEMS; INTEROPERABILITY FRAMEWORK; COMMAND AND CONTROL (C2) ARCHITECTURE; DATA FUSION AND AI-DRIVEN DECISION SUPPORT; CROSS-BORDER COOPERATIVE OPERATIONS; NETWORK-ENABLED CAPABILITY (NEC); HYBRID THREAT MITIGATION

Title: The Game of Strategy: Exploring Theoretical Frameworks for Decision-Making
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Strategic decision-making is a complex process influenced by cognitive biases, uncertainty, and adaptive learning—factors that are equally significant for effective leadership. This paper explores the theoretical foundations of game theory, wargaming, and cognitive psychology, their role in enhancing both decision-making and leadership capabilities across military, political, and civil domains. Beginning with Von Neumann and Morgenstern’s (1944) formulation of game theory, the discussion examines key concepts such as Nash equilibrium (1950), zero-sum and nonzero-sum games, and their applications in strategic interactions where leadership judgment, foresight, and coordination are critical. The role of wargaming in military and organizational strategy is analyzed through historical case studies, including the Prussian Kriegsspiel, the Cuban Missile Crisis, and NATO crisis simulations, highlighting its effectiveness in refining not only tactical and operational planning but also leadership under pressure. Building on insights from cognitive and behavioral psychology, the paper incorporates perspectives from Hogarth’s (1987) work on perceptual learning and judgmental decision-making. This paper links experiential learning (Kolb, 1984) with behavioral decision theory (Kahneman & Tversky, 1979), emphasizing how simulated and perceptual gaming mechanisms cultivate leaders’ situational awareness, pattern recognition, and emotional regulation in volatile contexts. By bridging theoretical frameworks, this concept paper demonstrates how games, simulations, and perceptual learning environments can contribute to more adaptive leadership in high-stakes environments.

Title: Under His Master’s Command or what Shakespeare can teach us about command and control
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Writing during the Elizabethan age of England during the 1590s and early 1600s, playwright William Shakespeare pursued the topic of war, civil unrest and related uncertainty in 21 of his plays. This pursuit reflected the wider tumultuous environment of the Elizabethan age (1558-1603), which was witness to considerable mutability of language, religion, ideas, identity, work, the social order, and command and control. Old sensibilities, certainties and behaviours were in flux from factors such as rising commercial transformation and business wealth that threatened the power of the landed aristocracy, the rule of a virgin queen in a world of men, and uncertainty from the ever-present threat of war and civil unrest to Elizabeth’s rule and her nascent nation. This paper examines Shakespeare’s representation of command and control in a period of friction and complexity with a focus on human behaviours. I will argue that understanding the dynamic interplay of key actors within a system, their decision-making process, their self-consciousness and employment of the system is essential to the effective application of the overall system. Current commentators insist that our current age is unparallelled with unprecedented events, Black Swans, and levels of complexity hitherto unseen. Accordingly, I will show that the comparable level of uncertainty during the Elizabethan age to contemporary times affords an opportunity to draw on Shakespeare for lessons on human actions in command and control systems for the present.

Title: Human Factor Considerations for Distributed Military Teams
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Distributed teams at various scales are rapidly emerging as the new normal rather than the exception in modern workplaces. The 2019 COVID pandemic was the catalyst for a new suite of flexible work arrangements normalised across the private and public sector, with advantages such as reduced work commutes and improved work-life balance for employees, as well as a greater talent pool, improved employee retention and reduced office utilities for employers. The normalisation of remote work and the accompanying improvements in enabling technologies has also spurred the exploration of other distributed team structures. For military organisations, new distributed team concepts offer ways to take advantage of preponderant basing and infrastructure, to create further domain or functional integration, and to create additional operational resilience.

Title: Kubernetes Scheduling for Highly Dynamic Network Environments
Authors: Keefer, L. T.; Griwodz, C.; Johnsen, F. T.
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: This research supports the development of a robust and distributed computer platform for effective Command and Control (C2) operations in dynamic and unpredictable network environments. Efficient orchestration of containerized distributed applications in dynamic network environments is critical for maintaining service continuity and performance, especially in Tactical Edge Environments (TEEs). This paper explores advanced scheduling algorithms for Kubernetes, an open-source container orchestration platform, to enhance its capability in such challenging conditions. Traditional Kubernetes scheduling primarily considers processing power and memory, but our approach integrates network information to optimize service placement and communication efficiency. By leveraging network data, obtained from tactical routers or network monitoring services, we aim to improve the performance of Kubernetes-managed applications. Our study evaluates several different algorithms; evolutionary algorithms, ant colony optimization, and simulated annealing in dynamically adjusting to network conditions, ensuring minimal service disruption and efficient resource utilization. The results indicate that incorporating network-aware scheduling significantly enhances Kubernetes' ability to manage containerized applications in TEEs, leading to improved service delivery and operational efficiency.

Title: Towards Socially Intelligent Agents in Defense: Opportunities, Implications, and Development
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: In defense, effective decisions require not just reasoning about facts, but also reasoning about what teammates and opponents believe, including their beliefs about others' beliefs. As autonomous systems increasingly support or make decisions in high-stakes environments, equipping them with such social reasoning capabilities becomes essential. This paper explores the integration of higher-order Theory of Mind (ToM) reasoning into artificial agents for defense applications. We present two use-cases, inspired by cognitive warfare and swarming, that illustrate how ToM-endowed Intelligent Decision Support Systems (IDSSs) and autonomous systems can infer and act upon the mental states of teammates and adversaries. We critically evaluate existing ToM paradigms in AI, including Large Language Models (LLMs), logic-based, Bayesian, and reinforcement learning approaches, against criteria of robustness, faithfulness, generalizability, interpretability, efficiency, and human-friendliness. Building on this analysis, we propose an architecture that combines LLM-based interaction with symbolic reasoning and epistemic planning, grounded in knowledge graphs. This architecture enables robust, faithful, and interpretable mental state inference and planning.

Title: Emergent C2 Structures and Dynamics in High-Level Video-Games
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The replication of C2 structures in videogames, especially those of a military context, is not necessarily uncommon in today's advanced video-games (Carter, 2015). The emergent properties of the unique context around the video game setting creates bespoke decentralised and desegregated C2 structures for that context, which can provide strategic insight into real-world military applications. This concept paper investigates several online video-game environments and analyses their C2 structures and how those agile C2 structures develop. C2 structure development is explored in the context of real-world learning and experience, through the video-game itself, and through to the unique challenges that force adaptability in solutions. By identifying and analysing unique conditions and case studies that create differing strategies and C2 dynamics, this work aspires to identify patterns that we can apply this adaptive learning to in real-world conditions. By identifying these patterns, this work suggests a reflexive, decentralised, agile and adaptive C2 structure that might enhance decision making. Conditions are investigated in which, even in non-military contexts, C2 structures and dynamics develop to potentially encourage those conditions in safe environments and what technology exists to enable those capabilities and unique C2 structures.

Title: Finding Pathways for Human-Machine Teaming in Command and Control at operational level: Taking advantage of both the best humans can offer and machines can provide.
Authors: Saylam, R.; van Rijn, M.; Bakx, G.; Granåsen, M.; Dekker, R.
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The increasing speed, complexity, and multi-domain nature of modern military operations demand a transformation in how decisions are  made and executed. This study serves as a follow-up to, and provides more detail on, NATO's Cross-Domain Command by exploring how Human-Machine Teaming can be systematically integrated to achieve decision advantage. Building on socio-technical systems theory and lessons from high-reliability areas such as aviation, the study highlights how the cognitive collaboration between humans and AI-enabled systems is reshaping the Command and Control landscape. Through a combination of literature review, doctrinal analysis, operational case studies including Project Maven and Ukraine's Delta system and subject matter expert interviews from NATO C2COE, the study reveals both opportunities and challenges, such as the need for system literacy, the risks of automationinduced skill degradation, and the importance of preserving cognitive diversity in joint decision-making. Rather than offering a prescriptive blueprint, the paper presents practical considerations and concrete, actionable suggestions for embedding HumanTeaming as a foundational enabler across all features of the Cross-Domain Command. Ultimately,  Human-Machine Teaming offers not merely a technological enhancement, but a paradigm shift toward more adaptive, resilient, and effective command leveraging both human judgment and machine capabilities in concert.

Title: Main Effort Assessment
Authors: Stucken, S.; Schmitz, H.-C.; Schadd, M.
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: A crucial task of situational assessment is the determination of the red main effort (ME). Knowing where the forces invest their ME supports the understanding of the adversary 's  inten tion. It is a precondition for the deployment of own forces in defense and delaying operations. The ME should be determined as early as possible, even if a full situational picture is not (yet) available. In this paper, we will, firstly, define features of the ME that enable its determination in a situational picture, with a focus on the tactical level  of  land-based  operations.  These  features  relate  to  the  critical  capabilities  for  specific  courses  of  action,  including reconnaissance and joint fire support, among others, as well as the massing of forces in areas of operations and, thus, the density of troops in subareas. Secondly, we will demonstrate how such ME patterns can be applied for supporting operators in situational assessment and planning. At ICCRTS 2024, we proposed a method for analyzing competing hypotheses on the battlefield in order to determine and evaluate reconnaissance areas and schedule sensors. We will now further develop the approach by clustering hypotheses based on the ME assessment and determine reconnaissance measures aimed at establishing clarity.

Title: Ramping up for the AI Explosion in Military C2: A Performance Benchmark for Autonomous Self-Improving AI Agents Based on Maneuver Warfare Theory
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: We have historically observed a development where AI systems have been used as tools to support decision-making and problemsolving in both trivial, routine situations, as well as more complex, ambiguous situations (for example medical diagnosis, weather forecasting and operations analysis). Presently, various AI systems have been able to take roles in the forefront of complex, dynamic decision-making, while the human decision-maker could retreat to operate more in the background. Future innovations will provide highly autonomous AI systems that not only have the capability to operate entirely without human intervention or supervision, but that such a capability may even be regarded by military strategists as necessary in order to effectively achieve mission objectives and ultimately win the war. The present challenge for AI innovators is to devise autonomous, self-improving AI agents for C2, that are not only able to be victorious in battles, but also to do so with the least possible losses of own forces as  well minimal collateral damage. Thus, I propose that a proper benchmark for a self-improving autonomous AI agent should be based on an established, wellproven theoretical platform: the maneuver warfare theory. In addition to discussing the appropriateness of this proposition, the purpose of this conceptual paper is to suggest an empirical test of whether a self-improving AI agent will be able to learn the principles of maneuver theory to such a degree that it can perform effectively and efficiently in a military C2 system without human supervision.

Title: Conflict Alert Flagging with Open Data Sources
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: In today's dynamic security landscape, timely and accurate intelligence is critical to supporting defense operations and strategic decision-making. While traditional intelligence sources remain invaluable, open-source conflict data-such as GDELT, ACLED, and UCDP-offers new opportunities for near-real-time situational awareness. This paper presents an enhanced methodology for conflict alerting based on GDELT data, introducing a novel 'state change' alerting approach that detects significant increases in activity using a rolling statistical model based on the generalized extreme value ( genextreme ) distribution. By benchmarking this method against existing alert algorithms, and evaluating all approaches using a carefully constructed labeled dataset of positive and negative time segments, the state change method demonstrates superior overall performance in accuracy, recall, and F1-score. The results suggest that this approach can effectively balance sensitivity and specificity, providing valuable alerts without excessive false positives or alert fatigue. The paper also explores initial attempts at applying machine learning to this domain, outlining future directions for integrating statistical modeling and AI in defense-focused conflict monitoring systems.

Title: C2 as a Manoeuvre Domain
Authors: O'Neill, J.; McDonald, J.; Kingston, G.; Mapperson, D.
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The C2 Agility relationship between the Endeavour Space and the C2 Approach Space is recast to C2 as a Manoeuvre Domain. The key terrain for C2 as a Manoeuvre Domain is defined as the allowable state transitions between C2 Approaches within a planning cycle.  The  allowable  state  transitions  between  C2  Approaches  within  a  planning  cycle  are  described  by  their  Decision  Support requirements. C2 as a Manoeuvre Domain for own forces enables reasoning about the preferred C2 Approach as circumstances change. Reasoning about C2 as a Manoeuvre Domain for opposing forces raises the question as to whether the conditions can be set so an opposing force is shaped into selecting a non-preferred C2 Approach creating a relative information advantage for exploitation in Decisive Manoeuvre. Assessing the key terrain reveals how to degrade the ability to meet the Decision Support requirements, shaping the selection of a non-preferred C2 Approach by an opposing force. The Decision Superiority construct then expands to include setting the conditions that enables the creation and exploitation of a relative information and C2 advantage.

Title: Expanding the Military Safety Climate Questionnaire (MSCQ)
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: This study aimed to identify additional safety climate factors by re-examining items previously excluded from two earlier studies on the Swedish Armed Forces. Several excluded items were originally designed to capture features of high-reliability organizations (HROs), particularly relevant in command and control (C2) environments. Using exploratory factor analysis (EFA), a new factor labeled Safety Improvement was identified and validated through confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) on a new sample drawn from a Swedish regiment. The CFA demonstrated acceptable model fit indices. The new factor was integrated into the Military Safety Climate Questionnaire (MSCQ), and its compatibility with the Nordic Safety Climate Questionnaire (NOSACQ-50) was tested. The Safety Improvement factor captures how military personnel engage in reflection, learning, and procedural adaptation-practices critical to effective and safe command and control. These findings underscore that safety climate tools designed for both military and civilian use may overlook key dimensions unique to the military, such as decentralized decision-making and adaptive coordination under pressure. As such, this revised instrument supports not only peacetime safety management but also enhances operational readiness by identifying safety-related vulnerabilities, reducing risk during crises and combat situations.

Title: Burstiness of Activity as a Moderating Variable in Team Cognition: Insights form a C3Fire microworld experiment
Authors: Bäcklander, G.; Spak, U.; Andersson, I.
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Collective intelligence research has identified temporal coordination patterns -particularly \burstiness\" in team activities -as key predictors of team performance. This paper bridges collective intelligence (CI) and command and control (C2) research by demonstrating use of CI methods in analyzing C2 team coordination. We tested whether burstiness mechanisms apply to co-located teams using the C3Fire microworld simulation

Title: Empirical Operationalization of the Law of Requisite Variety for Command and Control
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Operational decision makers encounter numerous challenges in meeting the Commander's intent during execution, requiring agility in responding to the presented situation. These challenges are exacerbated by uncertainties stemming from the fog and friction of war, which are a result of the complex realities. Ashby's Law of Requisite Variety has long been referenced as a control decision making paradigm, yet it has not been operationalized in the tactical environment. From a human-centric perspective, the research innovatively applies fuzzy methods and the Law of Requisite Variety to quantify decision makers' assessments of dynamic situations, building an actionable shared mental model and facilitating greater decisional agility across echelons. This research presents an approach to reducing uncertainties and providing affordances for decision making agility in naturalistic situations.

Title: LLMs for ICCRTS: Situational Awareness in the Research Domain
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Over the past 25 years, the International Command and Control Research and Technology Symposium (ICCRTS) has been a key forum for C2 science development and knowledge exchange. The IC2I repository holds 5,500+ ICCRTS papers, a valuable resource for C2 researchers. Although tech advances have improved military situational awareness, researchers lack similar support to build 'research situational awareness' from the IC2I repository. Existing search functions are limited. However, the rise of AI and NLP offers new possibilities for extracting insights from the IC2I repository. A new web-based prototype aims to help C2 researchers discover relevant information and extract new insights from the repository. Our approach leverages a variety of information-extraction, summarisation, question-answering and search technologies. We outline our methodology for extracting documents from the corpus, which we have downloaded with the approval of the IC2I, and discuss our processes for gathering meta-data about the documents, transforming data into vector embedding spaces, and integrating this with publicly available large language models. This enables capabilities for search, retrieval-augmented generation, and multi-document literature searches. In sharing our experiences using this prototype, we offer initial insights into the potential benefits this technology can bring to the C2 research community, as well as its current limitations. We further deliberate on the future possibilities for this technology and related advancements.

Title: Trust in technology and comprehensive decision making: The mediating role of human-machine interaction and perception of route functionality
Authors: Valaker, S.; Hannay, J. E.; Rise, Ø. R.
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Trust in technology could enable the use of decision support technology, which again could positively affect decision making and command and control to handle complexity [3]. Trust in decision support tools, e.g. a route planning tool for an army battalion, could for example enrich human decision making and make it more comprehensive by providing advice on multiple courses of action.    However,  the  way  trust  has  influence  on  comprehensive  decision  making  is  less  clear,  as  there  are  different  ways  of  interacting  with technology [2; 1]. Drawing on the literature of human-machine interaction we compare two different ways of interacting  with  technology:  division  of  labor  (selecting  who  will  be  responsible  for  tasks)  and  appropriation  (adopting  and adapting  technology).  We  examined  in  a  military  exercise,  whether  a  hypothesized  positive  effect  of  trust  in  technology  on comprehensive decision making, is serially mediated either by a) appropriation and perception of route functionality or b) by division of labor and perception of route functionality. We hypothesized that a more flexible way of interacting with technology, i.e.  appropriation, would be most beneficial for the relationship between trust, technology and decision making. We obtained data on these variables from 52 army cadettes, placed in 17 planning groups, who generated an army battalion plan using a route planning tool called Simulation-supported Wargaming for Analysis of Plans (SWAP). The results indicated that trust in technology and  comprehensive  decision  making  was  serially  mediated  through  appropriation  and  route  functionality,  but  not  serially mediated through division of labor and route functionality. Additional analysis indicated that appropriation at the group level affected  the  perception  of  route  functionality  at  the  individual  level.  Our  study  contributes  to  understanding  how  decision support in the military can be utilized [4]. We conclude the article by discussing theoretical implications, limitations, ideas for future research, as well as practical implications.

Title: Multilateral Interoperability Programme: Advancements in MIP4-5IES
Authors: Bau, N.; Dechand, M.; Gerz, M.; Güldner, A.; Kollmann, F.; Persie, C.
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: In an era in which military operations span across the land, air, sea, cyber, and space domain, the ability of national systems to ' speak the same language' is no longer optional it is mission-critical. The Multilateral Interoperability Programme (MIP), a military standardisation body comprising 21 member nations and NATO, is a leading contributor to international efforts to achieve crossnational and cross-domain interoperability by combining real-time data exchange with semantic consistency. This paper outlines the current state and direction of MIP, focusing on the evolution of the MIP solution to meet emerging operational needs. At the core of the MIP is the MIP Information Model (MIM), which provides the semantic foundation for meaningful and consistent information exchange between national Command and Control Information Systems (C2IS). The MIM defines a shared vocabulary and structure for representing military entities, relationships, and operational activities. Recent updates have focussed on the refinement of the representation of military capabilities and concepts with the aim of reducing ambiguity, enhancing automation, and improving situational understanding, with a particular emphasis on multi-domain operations (MDO). This includes a restructured taxonomy for operational actions, improved support for uncrewed vehicles and sensors, and a systematic revision of references to external sources. Staff concepts such as plans or orders have been adapted to meet evolving operational and technical requirements, enabling a more effective information exchange between command levels. In addition, a tagging mechanism has been introduced to define operationally scoped sub models, with overlays serving as the initial use case. Building on this semantic layer, the MIP4 Information Exchange Specification (MIP4-IES) defines a ReSTful service-oriented architecture that enables the technical exchange of this structured information. It supports real-time data synchronisation, data polling mechanisms, and cross-system interoperability. With recent improvements in exchange patterns, gateway services, overlay services, backwards compatibility, and alignment with the NATO Core Data Framework (NCDF), MIP4-IES promotes scalable, standards-based interoperability aligned with Federated Mission Networking (FMN) objectives. By establishing a standardised foundation for the exchange of command and control (C2) information, these efforts demonstrate how existing national systems can evolve toward a more connected and reliable exchange environment, helping to ensure better cooperation and decision-making in joint and combined operations.

Title: Coping with Complexity in Command and Control
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The theoretical aspects of situational complexity, and the challenges it creates, have been considered numerous times in recent command and control (C2) research. This has postulated that current ways of undertaking sense-making, decision making and planning are inadequate to cope in situations that are dynamically changing mixes of clear, complicated, complex and chaotic aspects. What has been lacking are candidates for a practical and holistic approach, backed up with actionable guidance on new ways of thinking, supporting methods and techniques, and demonstrations of effectiveness across a wide range of situations. Instead, we have a plethora of conceptual thinking and partial solutions, with none being sufficient on their own to guide practitioners. This paper starts to address this deficit by comparing and critiquing recent academic thinking, proposing a starting point for the development of a new approach. Insights from the comparison suggest that one conceptual framework may be the most appropriate for complex situations, but even this is not sufficient or appropriate in its current form. It would likely require tailoring, additional C2-appropriate methods, models and techniques, improvements to documentation and comprehensive testing in relevant settings. If the C2 research community agrees with the deductions put forward in this paper, a place to start development would be the listed mindset features and aligned methodological activities. It is therefore recommended that the C2 research community should develop such an approach, based on the findings in this paper, and then challenge, test, validate and adapt as appropriate using both evaluative and experiential feedback.

Title: Explaining Anomalies at Sea: An Exploratory Study on Textual Explanations and Human Performance in Maritime Surveillance
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) has become increasingly integrated into organizations. What was once an unprecedented novelty evolved into an essential tool for modern business practices. Although AI has the potential to be one of the most powerful tools for data analysis, its potential will be limited if users cannot understand it. This study explored the impact of textual explanations provided by an AI anomaly detector on human decision-making and end-user performance, through an experiment with eight Swedish Coast Guard command center operators. Participants, using an AI prototype system, were tasked with analyzing maritime activity to identify suspicious activity. The results indicate t hat textual explanations increase an end user's decision accuracy in false -positive scenarios but decrease their decision accuracy in true-positive scenarios. The results also suggest that a higher level of an enduser's perceived mental effort does not necessarily lead to decreased user performance. It should be noted that the sample size of this study (both in the number of participants and number of observed cases) is too small to arrive at robust, statistically significant results. Therefore, the quantitative results should be interpreted with caution. Nonetheless, this study provides valuable insights and is intended to serve as a foundation for future research.

Title: Strategy Maps: Reducing the complexity of planning in cyberspace operations
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Cyberspace exhibits extreme variety, malleability, and velocity, which makes it challenging to represent and reason about cyberspace operations. Its complex nature requires large, intricate models susceptible to state space explosion, where the state space expands exponentially based on the number of variables. Changes in any one variable can have dramatic and rapid consequences, which further complicate the representation of cyberspace configurations and reasoning about actions. This paper introduces a method to reduce the dimensionality of cyberspace models by embedding their state spaces into a 2-dimensional latent space, while maintaining the topographical attributes of the original space. Following the identification of suitable equivalence relations, this latent space is segmented into equivalence classes, with the topology of these partitioned regions supporting action-centric reasoning. The resulting object is named a strategy map, as it encapsulates assumptions about the environment, delineates potential end-states, and facilitates the generation of courses of action. By constructing the strategy map, we showcase the capability to execute planning via efficient path-search algorithms, such as the A ∗ algorithm. Our demonstration highlights the computational processing advantages of strategy map planning compared to alternative methods, accompanied by preliminary analysis regarding scalability for more intricate cyberspace segments. Furthermore, we examine the approach's limitations and discuss its potential application beyond cyberspace operations.

Title: Inter-Organisational Coordination in the Shadow of Crisis: Integrating Private Security into Total Defence under Conditions of Heightened Alert
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The  shifting  European  security  landscape  and  the  resurgence  of  hybrid  threats  have  prompted  renewed  attention  to  national resilience and total defence planning. In Sweden, this has catalysed a reconfiguration of civil-military preparedness, emphasising collaboration across public and private sectors. This paper examines the integration of the private security sector-comprising over 25,000 personnel-into Sweden's total defence framework under conditions of heightened alert. Drawing on empirical data from a 2024 scenario-based workshop and tabletop exercise, we analyse legal, organizational, and cultural barriers to effective coordination. Using the inter-organisational coordination maturity model developed by Johansson and Eriksson (2024), we assess the current state of public-private crisis collaboration as predominantly 'ad-hoc,' characterised by fragmented mandates, incompatible communication systems, and limited mutual trust. The findings indicate a need for context-sensitive development across technical, organizational, and social dimensions of interoperability. We propose pathways toward more mature coordination, including legal frameworks for cooperation, joint scenario-based training, secure communication platforms, and cultural bridging mechanisms. By situating empirical insights within a structured theoretical model, this study contributes to both the scholarly understanding and practical advancement of whole-of-society crisis preparedness, particularly in grey-zone scenarios where roles and responsibilities are fluid and time-sensitive coordination is critical.

Title: Integrate or separate 'what' and 'how' in command and control methods? An experimental design to test effects on creativity and response variety
Authors: Spak, U.; Andersson, I.; Bäcklander, G.
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Command and control systems are composed of several components such as methods, organization, personnel and technology. Each component contributes in its own, and in interaction with the other components, to produce direction and coordination for the mission respondent system. In this study, we focus on the method component in general and the decision-making process specifically. Decision-making is a key component in many theories and models of command and control. However, it is not always clear what type of decisions those theories refer to. We recognize two main classes of decisions necessary for producing direction and coordination. The first class of decisions concerns the overall ' what ' of a mission, which includes for example goals and desired end states. The second class of decisions regards the ' how ' of a mission, which includes for example who is doing specific tasks when and where. In this paper we present an experimental design that explores the possibility to enhance creativity and response variety by comparing a separated approach with an integrated approach in a microworld setting.

Title: Weather-related uncertainty and risk in C2: a concrete solution and a vision of the future
Authors: Landmark, K.; Greve, S. M.; Stensrud, R.
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Climate and weather may entail significant risks that restrict or impede operations. Meteorological and oceanographical (METOC) support is the effort to achieve command advantage through heightened awareness of the environment. To simplify the planning process, NATO employs a 'traffic light' system that labels physical conditions as favorable, marginal or unfavorable. This approach discards much information and may give misleading results. We propose a probabilistic method to convey METOC-induced risk and impact to operations compactly, yet with more nuance and precision. We show how to obtain results using only one-dimensional probability distributions, which makes computations more feasible in an operational setting. The method is demonstrated with concrete calculations, using global climatological data. METOC requirements and C2 structures of special operations in a harsh environment, the Arctic, are used as a backdrop the development. Special operations may take place in degraded C2 states, with high risk and low connectivity. Based on experience developing a METOC information system for secure defence platforms, we envision a solution whereby C2 agility and resilience can be preserved in contested C2 environments. Specifically, the recent development of data-driven (AI) weather models can be exploited to perform actual weather predictions near the edge of military networks, thus reducing the need for centralized information distribution. In combination with automated risk and impact assessment, this would allow special operations as well as regular forces to carry out complex operations with decentralized C2.
Author Keywords: METEOROLOGICAL AND OCEANOGRAPHIC (METOC) SUPPORT; PROBABILISTIC RISK ASSESSMENT; COMMAND AND CONTROL (C2) RESILIENCE; EDGE/FOG COMPUTING FOR WEATHER SERVICES; DATA-DRIVEN (AI) WEATHER FORECASTING; UNCERTAINTY QUANTIFICATION IN NWP; ARCTIC SPECIAL OPERATIONS PLANNING; PROBABILISTIC IMPACT MAPPING
