Title: Driving Crisis Leadership Fundamentals in the Swedish Total Defence - Core Concepts and the Road to Cultural Change
Authors: Uhr, C.; Frisell, M.; Rotter, P.
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Proactivity and decisiveness are two ideals strongly associated with successful crisis management and are emphasized by politicians, researchers, and authorities active in different parts of society. However, despite their intuitive simplicity, several questions arise when operationalizing the concepts in a crisis management practice.  This paper examines and discusses various aspects of proactivity and decisiveness, reflecting on how they are used as important building blocks for improving crisis management capacity in Sweden. To support practical applicability, we suggest three aspects of proactivity and highlight the benefits and risks associated with the ideal.  Furthermore,  we  suggest  a  nuanced  interpretation  of  decisiveness  and  how  it  fits  into  an  uncertain  environment  where decisionmakers need to act both quickly and persistently. In the second part of the paper, we draw upon theory of organizational culture and begin with discussing the heterogenous organizational context constituting the subject for change. We then share lessons learnt  from  communicating  change,  highlighting  the  importance  of  conceptual  harmony,  the  effects  of  a  narrative,  and  how experience comes into play when approaching proactivity and decisiveness from a competency perspective. Finally, we elaborate on the difficult question of how to know if a change has taken place.

Title: Words Matter: An Updated Conceptual Framework for Managing Joint Operations in Civil Crises and Armed Conflicts
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: This paper argues for the benefits of a consistent conceptual foundation to function as a common ground in collective efforts to improve crisis management capability. It shows how systems thinking and contemporary cybernetics can be transferred from academic publications to policy documents in a Swedish crisis management context, where numerous heterogenous organizations must act together - rather than in parallel - to achieve best possible outcomes. The guiding principle behind the efforts has been not to replace the many pre-existing intraorganizational crisis management discourses, but rather to promote a second language including descriptions of key terms, and, importantly, how they fit together and can be applied in a whole of society approach. Initially, a brief historical overview is presented followed by a short presentation of theoretical starting points. Then, a summary of the updated conceptual framework for joint operations and its guiding assumptions and values is described. Finally, experiences from the joint development process are systematically presented, followed by reflections on the implementation process.

Title: A Circular Task Model for Pan-Domain Command and Control: Task Analysis through Mixed-Methods Study of the Canadian Department of National Defence Operations
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The study presents a comprehensive task analysis of pan-domain command and control tasks conducted by the National Research Council of Canada for the Canadian Department of National Defence (DND). The research methodology combined documentation review of four key DND documents and semi-structured interviews with five Colonel-ranked commanders. The analysis revealed a three-tiered hierarchical structure of command and control (strategic, operational, and tactical levels) and led to the development of a circular task model that better represents the concurrent nature of these activities. The circular task model depicts various task domains arranged around a circumference, with interconnecting lines showing their mutual influences, all operating within a broader contextual environment. The model emphasizes that tasks occur simultaneously rather than sequentially, with outputs from one domain dynamically influencing inputs to others in real-time. The research concludes by identifying several areas for future validation and development, including the need to empirically verify task domains, characterize the nature and strength of influences between domains, and explore the potential of machine learning in recognizing task and situation patterns. The study suggests an incremental approach to validation, starting with constrained domains and gradually expanding to more complex scenarios.

Title: Exploratory Wargaming with a 'Superhuman' Tactician in the Team: A Controlled Experiment
Authors: Bell, N.; Brynielsson, J.; Cohen, M.; Collander-Brown, S.; Elg, J.; Ericsson, L.; Ivari, J.; Limér, C.; Mannberg, N.
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The paper introduces a prototype decision support system for rapid exploratory wargaming of ground combat together with a conceptual framework for human-machine teaming. An AlphaZero agent provides continuous, GPS-device-like advice on how blue/red should proceed from the current situation to meet/prevent the long-term mission objective for blue. A controlled experiment with 100+ senior officers provides concrete evidence that the utility of wargaming in a digital environment improves with an AlphaZero agent in the team of players.

Title: Explainable Artificial Intelligence for Multi-Domain Command and Control Concept Development
Authors: Ghajar-Khosravi, S.; Banbury, S.; Pelletier, S.; Fang, S.; Hou, M.; Lacasse, L.; Ho, G.
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: In support of the modernization of the North American Aerospace Defense Command (NORAD), several new research initiatives have been proposed for future NORAD multi-domain Command and Control (C2) operations.  The research work at the Defence Research and Development Canada (DRDC) Toronto Research Center (TRC) aims to identify key Human Factors (HF) challenges, and provide  recommendations  for  the  design  and  development  of  future  Artificial  Intelligence  (AI)-enabled  C2  decision  support systems. More specifically, in this paper we explore and discuss the eXplainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) requirements for future multi-domain  C2  systems.  We  introduce  a layered  explanation concept  and  present  the  relevant  Concept  Development  and Experimentation (CD&E) planning activities completed to date. The work is aimed to inform the human-AI interaction design of future AI- and XAI-enabled decision support systems in NORAD operations. The ultimate goal for the proposed XAI concept is to help improve C2 operators' situation awareness, human-AI team performance and mission effectiveness in future multi-domain C2 operations.

Title: An Incremental Approach to User Identity Resolution Across Multiple Social Platforms
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Social media has become a new battlefield in fighting adversarial propaganda, disinformation and fake news, which polarize public opinion and promote violent and hate speech. There are many politically active influencers on social media platforms attempting to sway public opinion on a variety of issues. Being able to identify social media accounts tied to these individuals, military partners can monitor these influences and gain valuable insights for future operations to effectively command and control. Nowadays, it has become increasingly popular for users to have accounts on multiple social media platforms. Identifying the same users across multiple platforms becomes an important military operational capability. However, this is a non-trivial task considering the large scale of online social media data that makes manual verification impossible and complex data quality issues, such as data noise, incompletion, highly unstructured format, etc. In this paper, we present an incremental approach to user identity resolution based on user profiles across different social platforms. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed solution.

Title: Designing a roadmapping tool for C2 capability development
Authors: Norrblom, P.; Lagung, A.; Pettersson, H.; Bergsten, A.; Granåsen, M.
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Developing C2 capability is challenging, as C2 systems are sociotechnical and involve multiple interdependent system components. Roadmapping is a collaborative process that can be used in order to identify and discuss necessary steps to improve C2 capability. The resulting roadmap visualises anticipated development activities within different components of the C2 system and their relationships. This study explores how a roadmapping tool for C2 capability development can be designed. A roadmapping tool prototype was developed in the form of a web-based application implemented as a directed graph, where C2 development steps are visualised as  interconnected  nodes.  The  prototype  development  resulted  in  a  roadmapping  tool  prototype  that  contains  functionality  for creating and editing roadmaps, basic visualisation, and collaborative work. In addition, usability assessment in the form of expert evaluations were carried out, providing results regarding how roadmapping tool usability can be improved at an early development stage. Together, these results provide insights into the design and development process of a roadmapping tool for C2 capability development, covering both tool development and usability assessment. Further work on the roadmapping tool is required, and will include adding more visualisation and analysis functions, performing additional usability assessment, testing to create and handle complex roadmaps, as well as exploring how a roadmap can aid C2 capability development in practice. Based on the results from this study, the authors believe a roadmapping tool can provide useful support to the C2 capability development process.

Title: What Are All these People Doing Here?
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: As we strive to capitalise on the benefits offered by AI in the evolution of our C2 socio-technical systems, we are faced with complex decisions as to how we share capabilities and responsibilities between humans and machines. Many research initiatives in AI and C2 focus on the use of individual AI components to support specific discrete human functions, while assuming that the structure of the HQ, its staffing and associated C2 processes will remain largely intact. This paper argues that this limited approach to AI experimentation and exploitation will fail to deliver the wholesale transformation in military capability that has been demanded by senior staff, lawmakers, governments and politicians. A related but enduring background assumption is that humans have unique strengths and capabilities that mean that we can't replace them. This approach fails, however, to recognise the genuine limitations that humans bring, and potentially precludes the architectural reimagination of HQs that would fully capitalise on the potential of AI. The paper introduces the 'Zero Person Headquarters', a flexible thought experiment that could be used in several different ways to explore the future C2/HQ space, harnessing the ever-developing powers of AI and reducing where possible, reliance on expensive, vulnerable and scarce human staff. The paper concludes that meaningful transformation demands a reappraisal of the relative strengths of AI and humans, and reimagining C2 architectures and approaches, rather than automating existing solutions. It will have succeeded if it manages to promote discussion or provoke debate concerning the relative strengths and weaknesses of AI and people, and to encourage the research community to push boundaries and embrace the use of AI in realising transformative advances for the future of military command and control.
Author Keywords: ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE; COMMAND AND CONTROL (C2) ARCHITECTURE; ZERO-PERSON HEADQUARTERS; HUMAN-MACHINE TEAMING; ETHICAL AND LEGAL FRAMEWORKS FOR AUTONOMOUS SYSTEMS; AI-DRIVEN DECISION SUPPORT; TRANSFORMATIVE MILITARY AI INTEGRATION

Title: Mitigation Strategies for Communications Network Discontinuity for C2 in Tactical Environments
Authors: Chan, K.; Santry, P.; Hawkins, T.
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: This concept paper surveys the critical issue of network discontinuity in tactical communications environments, where reliable connectivity is often compromised due to a range of adversarial, environmental, and systemic factors. As modern military operations increasingly depend on continuous communication for effective command and control (C2), understanding the causes and effects of network disruptions becomes paramount. Military networks operate in the denied, degraded, intermittent, or limited-bandwidth (DDIL) environment, which has the potential to severely inhibit network performance and subsequently C2 and associated C4ISR functionality. Commanders must anticipate some network discontinuity from adversarial, environmental, and/or systemic and technical causes. Techniques and policies can mitigate the impact of these network discontinuities. We explore the spectrum of these approaches and make recommendations for potential mitigation strategies in specific situations. This paper is divided into three sections. The first section reviews recent work on network discontinuity and C2 operating in DDIL environments. The second section proposes a framework in which to systematically consider network discontinuities, C2 and network mitigation strategies. The third section considers multiple use-cases on network mitigation for C2 and network performance. This paper provides insights on how to enable robust C2 despite a dynamic and contested environment and lessons learned on promoting such properties in operational settings.

Title: Supporting Future Military Command- and Control (C2) System Design: Defining Method Components to Enhance the Existing Development Framework
Authors: Lundberg, J.; Andersson, K.; Ralyté, J.; Stirna, J.
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The design of future military C2-systems is increasingly influenced by emerging technologies such as AI and autonomous platforms. These systems must operate across domains, evolve rapidly, and remain coherent with operational doctrine and organizational processes. To manage this, architecture frameworks like the NATO Architecture Framework (NAF) are widely used. NAF supports modeling coherence and ensures traceability from capability needs to system implementations. However, it lacks methodological guidance. To address this gap, we introduce an extension to NAF, building on the principles of Situational Method Engineering (SME). Our proposal includes reusable method components. Specifically, it includes a MAP-based Navigation Guidance that structures design intentions and strategies in alignment with stakeholder needs, and two method chunks, supporting core design activities. One method chunk supports designers to assess the relevance of integrating emerging technologies into existing C2systems. The other method chunk enables exploration of how new technologies perform across operationally relevant and underspecified scenarios. Both chunks are grounded in previously validated stakeholder goals and detailed design requirements, ensuring alignment with military capability needs. Our approach complements NAF by providing methodological guidance for navigating integration decisions. This initial proposal shows how method chunks can enhance design adaptability and address sociotechnical concerns such as stakeholder alignment and doctrinal coherence. While only two method chunks have been constructed so far, the results demonstrate the potential of a modular, goal-aligned method repository to guide C2-system development. In future research, we focus on expanding the method component repository and evaluating the framework in a real-world setting.

Title: Kill Webs by Collaborative & Self-organizing Agents (CSOAs)
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: A single agent represents a single system capable of ingesting local data, indexing, cataloging information, performing knowledge pattern discovery, and separating patterns and anomalies from data. Multiple agents work collaboratively in a peer-to-peer network. Each agent has a peer list. Such multiple agents' collaboration can be modeled as cooperative games. Each agent optimizes its own objective locally. We show that each agent self-organizes or converges to its best value and the whole agent network achieves the best social welfare based on both the quantum adiabatic evolution transformation (QAET), and quantum intelligence game (QIG) or the QAET-QIG framework. We apply the QAET-QIG framework to the kill web concept that can potentially improve the traditional kill chain process or the find, fix, track, target, engage, and assess (F2T2EA) process. The improvement is measured in the values of powerful global optimization, distributed lethality, and load balancing. We show a use case of the QAET-QIG frame in a potential application of mixed sensors, platforms, weapons, and effects.

Title: Governance of C2 as a capability from a lifecycle perspective the way forward
Authors: Wikberg, P.; Bryant, D.; Dekker, R.; Jobidon, M.-E.; Gaertner, P.; Granåsen, M.; Hill, J.; Kalloniatis, A.
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: This paper summarises the work of a NATO-sponsored Research Technology Group (RTG), which was established to explore ways to improve command and control (C2) capability development. The RTG ran from March 2021 to November 2024 and our findings have been presented at the International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium (ICCRTS) since 2021.  This paper provides a summary of the RTG findings and products, including a framework for promoting C2 capability development We argue that a key barrier to effective change is the fact that C2 is not governed as a socio-technical capability. A holistic approach is required for capability development, which needs a focus on people, processes, organisations and technology rather than governing these C2 capability elements in isolation.  We then describe the rationale for governing C2 is a socio-technical capability and the need to take a lifecycle approach and offer a set of guiding principles for 'C2 Capability Lifecycle Governance' for considerations by stakeholders tasked with developing C2 capability. We conclude by outlining the proposal for a new NATO Research Task Group that will advance this work by refining and testing the C2 Capability Lifecycle Governance framework developed under HFM-342. Its aim is to move beyond conceptual understanding toward validated, practical approaches for governing C2 as a socio-technical capability through empirical studies and simulation-based experimentation.

Title: Automating Open-Source Intelligence (OSINT) and Source Characterization with Large Language Model (LLM) for Command and Control (C2)-related Artificial Intelligence Generated Text
Authors: Devereaux, Z. P.; Page-Fortin, M.; Bergeron-Guyard, A.; Archambault, C.
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: This paper explores the current capabilities and limitations of Large Language Models (LLMs) within the Artificial Intelligence (AI) pipeline for the enterprise, particularly focusing on their potential for Open-Source Intelligence (OSINT) and public affairs course-of-action (COA) generations, as well as the thornier task of artificial intelligence generated text (AIGT) detection. While these frontier model systems excel in synthesizing complex data and generating concise insights for blue team, which benefits C2, they falter when addressing nuanced challenges, such as assessing authenticity and provenance in adversarial generated texts from red team. To address the potential future role of LLM in resilient multi-domain C2 within operations in the information environment (OIE), an assessment of strengths and weaknesses of such automations is proposed. Furthermore, automating upstream OSINT and COA tasks, supplemented by rigorous downstream validation mechanisms, offers a pathway to leverage LLM efficiencies for resilient multidomain C2 in cyberspace, while reducing vulnerabilities. This strategic approach underscores the need for a multidimensional sociotechnical framework for C2 to maximize the credibility and reliability of AI-driven intelligence processes for the enterprise. The research paper consists of several small-scale experiments with short social-media-like texts and images derived from the collaboration between the Canadian National Research Council (CNRC) and Defence Research and Development Canada (DRDC) focused on source characterization methods for artificial intelligence generated text (AIGT).

Title: Evaluating SLAM Technology Adaption for COP at Sea
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping Technology (SLAM) has shown applicability to building situational awareness in land and undersea applications but has received little to no investigation for surface navy applications. The US Navy Hybrid Fleet concept will  require  utilizing  exemplary  emerging  technology  from  industry  to  best  equip  Navy  Unmanned  Surface  Vehicle  (USV)  and Unmanned Air Vehicles (UAV)'s for their mission set. Evaluating emerging technologies at sea is a particular challenge because of the increased complexity added due to the movements of the sea and the high cost and time needed to set up experiments. This paper offers a methodology of using Forrester and Meadows System Dynamics style modeling coupled with Monte Carlo analysis to  analyze  SLAM  technology  at  sea  for  enhancing  situational  awareness  in  dynamic  maritime  environments,  and  to  make recommendations  for  the  focus  of  future  experimentation  to  ensure  maximal  value  attained  in  the  experiment  process.  This methodology seems especially useful for iteration in an experiment campaign as described by Alberts and Hayes (2009) as it can be adjusted as experimental data provides real world corrections to the assumptions behind the model.

Title: Data-Driven Distributed Common Operational Picture from Heterogenous Platforms using Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
Authors: Sur, I.; Raghavan, A.; Rahman, A.; Hare, J. Z.; Cassenti, D.; Busart, C.
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: The integration of unmanned platforms equipped with advanced sensors promises to enhance situational awareness and mitigate the 'fog of war' in military operations. However, managing the vast influx of data from these platforms poses a significant c hallenge for Command and Control (C2) systems. This study presents a novel multi-agent learning framework to address this challenge. Our method enables autonomous and secure communication between agents and humans, which in turn enables real-time formation of an interpretable Common Operational Picture (COP). Each agent encodes its perceptions and actions into compact vectors, which are then transmitted, received and decoded to form a COP encompassing the current state of all agents (friendly and enemy) on the battlefield. Using Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL), we jointly train COP models and agent 's action selection policies. We demonstrate resilience to degraded conditions such as denied GPS and disrupted communications. Experimental validation is performed in the Starcraft-2 simulation environment to evaluate the precision of the COPs and robustness of policies. We report less than 5% error in COPs and policies resilient to various adversarial conditions. In summary, our contributions include a method for autonomous COP formation, increased resilience through distributed prediction, and joint training of COP models and multi-agent RL policies. This research advances adaptive and resilient C2, facilitating effective control of heterogeneous unmanned platforms.

Title: Integrating Large Language Models in Wargaming
Authors: Abdelazez, M.; Zaman, H.; Ghanmi, A.
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: This concept paper explores the integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) in wargaming methodologies, examining their potential to enhance both the design and execution phases. Wargaming, a simulation technique used to model conflict scenarios and strategic decision-making, has traditionally relied on human expertise for scenario development, role-playing, and outcome analysis. The paper begins by defining wargaming as a disciplined approach to exploring potential futures through interactive simulation, highlighting its applications in the military. Practical wargaming serves as a critical test bed for robust command and control (C2) systems by allowing leaders to test decision-making processes and an organization's C2 capacity under simulated pressure. It then outlines the critical aspects of wargaming methodology, including scenario design, participant selection, rule development, adjudication processes, and after-action analysis. The paper proposes several innovative applications of LLMs in wargaming, such as generating diverse scenario elements, automating adjudication processes, and enhancing post-game analysis. It further explores the generation of wargaming scenarios by introducing a concept framework that ensures the factuality of the scenario. The framework also introduces a factuality metric. The paper also discusses the ethics and disadvantages of using LLMs for wargaming.

Title: Autonomous generation of different courses of action in mechanized combat operations
Authors: Schubert, J.; Hansen, P.; Hörling, P.; Johansson, R.
Source title: 30th International Command and Control Research & Technology Symposium
Abstract: In this paper, we propose a methodology designed to support decision-making during the execution phase of military ground combat operations, with a focus on one's actions. This methodology generates and evaluates recommendations for various courses of action for  a  mechanized  battalion,  commencing  with  an  initial  set  assessed  by  their  anticipated  outcomes.  It  systematically  produces thousands of individual action alternatives, followed by evaluations aimed at identifying alternative courses of action with superior outcomes. These alternatives are appraised in light of the opponent's status and actions, considering unit composition, force ratios, types of offense and defense, and anticipated advance rates. Field manuals evaluate battle outcomes and advancement rates. The processes of generation and evaluation work concurrently, yielding a variety of alternative courses of action. This approach facilitates the management of new course generation based on previously evaluated actions. As the combat unfolds and conditions evolve, revised courses of action are formulated for the decision-maker within a sequential decision-making framework.
